Quick Reference - Quick Reference Links - Current Tool Versions

Appendices - Appendix B: Complete URL Reference - Appendix C: C64 Memory Map - Appendix D: SID Register Map - Appendix E: VIC-II Register Map - Appendix F: CIA Register Map - Appendix G: 6502/6510 Opcode Reference

Development Workflow Tips

Recommended Starter Setup

  1. IDE: VS64 (Visual Studio Code) or C64 Studio
  2. Assembler: KickAssembler or ACME
  3. Emulator: VICE (x64sc for accuracy)
  4. Debugger: Retro Debugger
  5. Music: GoatTracker 2 or SID-Wizard
  6. Graphics: SpritePad + CharPad or Spritemate (web)
  7. Reference: HVSC for music inspiration, CSDb for demos

Build Pipeline Example

Source (.asm) → Assembler (KickAss/ACME) → Cruncher (Exomizer) → .prg → VICE/Real Hardware

Advanced Pipeline (with loader)

Assets → Converters → Cruncher (per-file) → Disk Image (.d64) → Krill's Loader → VICE/Real Hardware

Quick Reference Links

Category Top Pick Link
Assembler KickAssembler http://theweb.dk/KickAssembler/
C Compiler Oscar64 https://github.com/drmortalwombat/oscar64
IDE C64 Studio / VS64 https://endurion.itch.io/c64studio
Debugger Retro Debugger https://github.com/slajerek/RetroDebugger
Disassembler Regenerator CSDb
Tracker GoatTracker 2 https://sourceforge.net/projects/goattracker2/
Emulator VICE https://vice-emu.sourceforge.io/
Web Emulator vc64web https://vc64web.github.io/
Cruncher Exomizer https://github.com/bitshifters/exomizer
Fast Loader Krill’s Loader CSDb
Music Archive HVSC https://www.hvsc.c64.org/
Scene DB CSDb https://csdb.dk/
Code Wiki Codebase64 https://codebase64.org/
Online Player DeepSID https://deepsid.chordian.net/
Modern Hardware Ultimate-II+L https://ultimate64.com/

Current Tool Versions

Reference for latest stable versions of major development tools (as of December 2024):

Tool Version Released Notes
VICE 3.9 Dec 2024 Major emulator update, improved timing
KickAssembler 5.25 2024 Java-based cross-assembler
GoatTracker 2 2.77 Nov 2024 SID music tracker
cc65 2.19 2023 C compiler/assembler suite
ACME 0.97 2023 Multi-platform cross-assembler
Exomizer 3.1.2 2024 PRG cruncher
C64 Studio 7.7 2024 Windows IDE
Retro Debugger 0.64.72+ 2024 Multi-platform debugger
SpritePad Pro 2.1 2024 Sprite editor
CharPad Pro 3.0 2024 Character/map editor
SID-Wizard 1.9 2023 Alternative SID tracker
Oscar64 1.31+ 2024 Optimizing C compiler

Note: Versions update frequently. Check project websites for latest releases.

🤔 Which C64 Do You Have?

Answer 3 questions to identify your board revision and what it means for repairs and upgrades.

Step 1 of 3

🗺 C64 Interactive Memory Map

Default layout with LORAM=HIRAM=CHAREN=1 (BASIC + KERNAL + I/O visible). Click any region for details and key addresses.

RAM ROM I/O Special

70. Getting Started Flowchart

Use this decision tree to choose your development path.

What Do You Want to Create?

                    ┌─────────────────────┐
                    │  What are you       │
                    │  trying to make?    │
                    └──────────┬──────────┘
                               │
        ┌──────────┬───────────┼───────────┬──────────┐
        ▼          ▼           ▼           ▼          ▼
    ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐
    │ Game  │  │ Demo  │  │ Music │  │ Tool/ │  │ Learn │
    │       │  │       │  │       │  │ App   │  │ ASM   │
    └───┬───┘  └───┬───┘  └───┬───┘  └───┬───┘  └───┬───┘
        │          │          │          │          │
        ▼          ▼          ▼          ▼          ▼

Game Development Path

Simple Game? ──────► Yes ──────► BASIC + BASIC Boss
     │                          or C with CC65
     No
     │
     ▼
2D Action Game? ───► Yes ──────► KickAssembler + SpritePad
     │                          + CharPad + Goattracker
     No
     │
     ▼
Complex Game? ─────► Use C64 Studio (IDE)
                    or VS Code + KickAssembler
                    Consider: Sprite multiplexer
                    + Krill's Loader + Exomizer

Demo Development Path

First Demo? ───────► Study existing demos
     │               Codebase64 tutorials
     │               Start with raster bars
     │
     ▼
Intermediate? ─────► Learn stable raster
     │               FLI/IFLI graphics
     │               Sprite multiplexing
     │
     ▼
Advanced? ─────────► VIC-II tricks (VSP, FPP)
                    Custom loaders
                    Compression

Music Development Path

Beginner? ─────────► Goattracker (cross-platform)
     │               SID Wizard (native)
     │
     ▼
Which style?
     │
     ├── Chiptune ────► Goattracker, learn SID
     │
     ├── Tracker ─────► DefleMask, Goattracker
     │
     └── Live ────────► Cynthcart, MSSIAH

Tool/App Development Path

BASIC Sufficient? ─► Yes ──────► Use built-in BASIC
     │                          Consider BASIC Boss
     No
     │
     ▼
Need Speed? ───────► Assembly (KickAssembler)
     │               or C (CC65)
     │
     ▼
Need GUI? ─────────► Consider C64 OS
                    or custom character UI

Assembler Selection

                    ┌─────────────────────┐
                    │ Pick an Assembler   │
                    └──────────┬──────────┘
                               │
    ┌──────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┐
    │                          │                          │
    ▼                          ▼                          ▼
┌────────────┐          ┌────────────┐          ┌────────────┐
│ Beginner   │          │ Scene Dev  │          │ Portable   │
│            │          │            │          │            │
│ ACME       │          │ KickAsm    │          │ 64tass     │
│ - Simple   │          │ - Powerful │          │ - Clean    │
│ - Good docs│          │ - Scripts  │          │ - Fast     │
└────────────┘          │ - Debug    │          │ - Cross-   │
                        └────────────┘          │   platform │
                                                └────────────┘

IDE Selection

                    ┌─────────────────────┐
                    │ Choose Your IDE     │
                    └──────────┬──────────┘
                               │
    ┌──────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┐
    │                          │                          │
    ▼                          ▼                          ▼
┌────────────┐          ┌────────────┐          ┌────────────┐
│ All-in-One │          │ Modern IDE │          │ Lightweight│
│            │          │            │          │            │

└────────────┘          └────────────┘          └────────────┘

Emulator Selection

Development? ──────► VICE (best debugging)
     │
     ▼
Casual Testing? ───► Retro Debugger (visualization)
     │
     ▼
Cycle-Accurate? ───► Hoxs64 or micro64
     │
     ▼
In Browser? ───────► VirtualConsoles.com

79. Quick Reference Card

A condensed cheat sheet of essential C64 information for rapid lookup during development.

Memory Map Essentials

$0000-$00FF  Zero Page (fast access, 256 bytes)
$0100-$01FF  Stack (grows downward)
$0200-$03FF  System variables, input buffer
$0400-$07FF  Default screen RAM (1000 bytes + spare)
$0800-$9FFF  BASIC program area / Free RAM
$A000-$BFFF  BASIC ROM (8K) / RAM underneath
$C000-$CFFF  Free RAM (4K)
$D000-$DFFF  I/O area / Character ROM / RAM
$E000-$FFFF  KERNAL ROM (8K) / RAM underneath

Key Zero Page Locations

Address Name Purpose
$01 CPU Port Memory banking (bits 0-2)
$03-$04 ADRAY1 Float→Int conversion vector
$05-$06 ADRAY2 Int→Float conversion vector
$14-$15 LINNUM Temp integer storage
$22-$25 - Free for ML programs
$39-$3A CURLIN Current BASIC line
$43-$44 TXTPTR BASIC text pointer
$61-$66 FAC1 Floating point accumulator 1
$69-$6E FAC2 Floating point accumulator 2
$90 STATUS I/O status word
$91 STKEY Stop key flag
A0−A2 TIME Jiffy clock (1/60 sec)
$B7 FNLEN Filename length
$B8 LA Current logical file
$B9 SA Current secondary address
$BA FA Current device number
BB−BC FNADR Filename pointer
FB−FE - Free for ML programs

$01 CPU Port (Memory Banking)

Bits Value Configuration
0 LORAM 0=RAM at $A000, 1=BASIC ROM
1 HIRAM 0=RAM at $E000, 1=KERNAL ROM
2 CHAREN 0=Char ROM at $D000, 1=I/O

Common Values: - $37 - Default (BASIC+KERNAL+I/O) - $36 - BASIC+KERNAL+Char ROM visible - $35 - I/O only, RAM at A000/E000 - $34 - All RAM (no ROMs, no I/O) - $33 - Char ROM + RAM everywhere else

VIC-II Quick Reference (D000−D02E)

Reg Address Function
$D000 Sprite 0 X Horizontal position
$D001 Sprite 0 Y Vertical position
$D010 SPR X MSB Bit 8 of X for all sprites
$D011 CR1 Screen control (YSCROLL, DEN, RSEL, BMM, ECM)
$D012 RASTER Current/compare raster line
$D015 SPR EN Sprite enable (1 bit per sprite)
$D016 CR2 Screen control (XSCROLL, CSEL, MCM, RES)
$D017 SPR Y-EXP Sprite Y expand
$D018 MEMPTR Screen/char memory pointers
$D019 IRR Interrupt register
$D01A IMR Interrupt mask
$D01B SPR PRI Sprite/background priority
$D01C SPR MC Sprite multicolor mode
$D01D SPR X-EXP Sprite X expand
$D020 BORDER Border color
$D021 BGCOL0 Background color 0
$D022-24 BGCOL1-3 Background colors 1-3
$D025-26 SPMC0-1 Sprite multicolor 0-1
$D027-2E SP0COL-7 Sprite colors 0-7

$D011 Control Register 1:

Bit 7: Raster compare bit 8
Bit 6: ECM (Extended Color Mode)
Bit 5: BMM (Bitmap Mode)
Bit 4: DEN (Display Enable)
Bit 3: RSEL (25/24 rows)
Bit 0-2: YSCROLL (0-7)

$D016 Control Register 2:

Bit 4: MCM (Multicolor Mode)
Bit 3: CSEL (40/38 columns)
Bit 0-2: XSCROLL (0-7)

$D018 Memory Setup:

Bits 4-7: Screen RAM = $0400 × value
Bits 1-3: Char ROM/RAM = $0800 × value (in VIC bank)
         Bitmap RAM = $2000 × bit 3

SID Quick Reference (D400−D41C)

Per-Voice Registers (Voice 1 at $D400, +7 for V2, +14 for V3):

Offset Function
+0 Freq Low
+1 Freq High
+2 Pulse Width Low
+3 Pulse Width High (bits 0-3)
+4 Control (GATE, SYNC, RING, TEST, waveform)
+5 Attack/Decay (high/low nybble)
+6 Sustain/Release (high/low nybble)

Control Register (+4):

Bit 0: GATE (1=start, 0=release)
Bit 1: SYNC (sync with voice 3)
Bit 2: RING (ring mod with voice 3)
Bit 3: TEST (disable oscillator)
Bit 4: TRIANGLE waveform
Bit 5: SAWTOOTH waveform
Bit 6: PULSE waveform
Bit 7: NOISE waveform

Global Registers:

Address Function
$D415-16 Filter cutoff (11-bit)
$D417 Filter resonance/route
$D418 Volume/filter mode
$D419 Paddle X (read)
$D41A Paddle Y (read)
$D41B OSC3 output (read)
$D41C ENV3 output (read)

Note Frequency Table (PAL):

Note Freq Note Freq
C-0 $0116 C-4 $1160
C-1 $022C C-5 $22C0
C-2 $0458 C-6 $4580
C-3 $08B0 C-7 $8B00

CIA Quick Reference

CIA 1 ($DC00) - Keyboard/Joystick:

Address Function
$DC00 Port A (keyboard column / joy 2)
$DC01 Port B (keyboard row / joy 1)
$DC04-05 Timer A
$DC06-07 Timer B
$DC08-0B TOD Clock
$DC0D Interrupt Control
$DC0E Control Register A
$DC0F Control Register B

CIA 2 ($DD00) - Serial/VIC Bank:

Address Function
$DD00 Port A (VIC bank, serial)
$DD02 Data Direction A

VIC Bank Selection ($DD00 bits 0-1):

%00 = Bank 3: $C000-$FFFF
%01 = Bank 2: $8000-$BFFF
%10 = Bank 1: $4000-$7FFF
%11 = Bank 0: $0000-$3FFF (default)

Essential KERNAL Calls

Address Name Function
$FFD2 CHROUT Print character in A
$FFE4 GETIN Get character (non-blocking)
$FFCF CHRIN Input character (blocking)
$FFE1 STOP Check STOP key
$FFBA SETLFS Set file parameters
$FFBD SETNAM Set filename
$FFD5 LOAD Load file
$FFD8 SAVE Save file
$FFC0 OPEN Open file
$FFC3 CLOSE Close file
$FFC6 CHKIN Set input channel
$FFC9 CHKOUT Set output channel
$FFCC CLRCHN Clear channels
$FFB7 READST Read status
$FFDE SETTIM Set jiffy clock
$FFDB RDTIM Read jiffy clock

Common Device Numbers

Device Number Purpose
Keyboard 0 Default input
Datasette 1 Tape drive
RS-232 2 Serial communications
Screen 3 Default output
Printer 4-5 Serial printers
Plotter 6 Plotters
Disk 8-11 Floppy drives
Disk 12-15 Hard drives

6502 Opcode Quick Reference

Load/Store:

LDA #$nn / LDA $nn / LDA $nnnn   Load A
LDX #$nn / LDX $nn / LDX $nnnn   Load X
LDY #$nn / LDY $nn / LDY $nnnn   Load Y
STA $nn / STA $nnnn              Store A
STX $nn / STX $nnnn              Store X
STY $nn / STY $nnnn              Store Y

Arithmetic:

ADC - Add with carry      SBC - Subtract with borrow
INC - Increment memory    DEC - Decrement memory
INX/INY - Increment X/Y   DEX/DEY - Decrement X/Y

Logic:

AND - Logical AND    ORA - Logical OR     EOR - Exclusive OR
ASL - Shift left     LSR - Shift right
ROL - Rotate left    ROR - Rotate right

Compare/Branch:

CMP - Compare A      CPX - Compare X      CPY - Compare Y
BEQ - Branch if =    BNE - Branch if ≠
BCS - Branch C=1     BCC - Branch C=0
BMI - Branch N=1     BPL - Branch N=0
BVS - Branch V=1     BVC - Branch V=0

Jump/Call:

JMP $nnnn - Jump              JSR $nnnn - Call subroutine
RTS - Return from sub         RTI - Return from interrupt
BRK - Software interrupt

Stack/Flags:

PHA - Push A         PLA - Pull A
PHP - Push flags     PLP - Pull flags
TXS - X → Stack ptr  TSX - Stack ptr → X
SEC - Set carry      CLC - Clear carry
SEI - Disable IRQ    CLI - Enable IRQ
SED - Decimal mode   CLD - Clear decimal

Transfer:

TAX - A → X    TXA - X → A
TAY - A → Y    TYA - Y → A

C64 Color Values

Value Color Value Color
0 Black 8 Orange
1 White 9 Brown
2 Red 10 Light Red
3 Cyan 11 Dark Gray
4 Purple 12 Medium Gray
5 Green 13 Light Green
6 Blue 14 Light Blue
7 Yellow 15 Light Gray

Screen Codes vs PETSCII

Character Screen PETSCII
@ $00 $40
A-Z $01-$1A $41-$5A
0-9 $30-$39 $30-$39
Space $20 $20
Reverse +$80 -

Useful Formulas

Screen Position: Address = $0400 + (row × 40) + column

Color RAM Position: Address = $D800 + (row × 40) + column

Sprite Pointer: Address = Screen + $03F8 + sprite_number

Sprite Data Address: Address = pointer_value × 64

Character in Bitmap: Address = Bitmap + (char_row × 320) + (char_col × 8)

VIC Frequency: Freq = (cycles_per_second / desired_hz) - 1

SID Note Frequency: Freq_reg = (Hz × 16777216) / clock_freq - PAL clock: 985248 Hz - NTSC clock: 1022727 Hz

80. Project Templates

Ready-to-use starter templates for common C64 project types. Each template includes directory structure, build system, and commented skeleton code.

Template 1: Simple Assembly Game

Directory Structure:

game/
├── Makefile
├── src/
│   ├── main.asm        ; Entry point
│   ├── init.asm        ; Initialization
│   ├── game.asm        ; Game logic
│   ├── sprites.asm     ; Sprite handling
│   ├── input.asm       ; Joystick/keyboard
│   └── sound.asm       ; Sound effects
├── data/
│   ├── charset.bin     ; Custom characters
│   ├── sprites.bin     ; Sprite data
│   └── music.bin       ; Music data
├── tools/
│   └── convert.py      ; Asset conversion
└── build/
    └── game.prg        ; Output

Makefile (KickAssembler):

ASM = java -jar /path/to/KickAss.jar
VICE = x64sc
SRC = src/main.asm
OUT = build/game.prg

all: $(OUT)

$(OUT): $(SRC) src/*.asm data/*
    @mkdir -p build
    $(ASM) -o $(OUT) $(SRC)

run: $(OUT)
    $(VICE) $(OUT)

clean:
    rm -rf build/*

.PHONY: all run clean

main.asm (Entry Point):

//=============================================
// Simple Game Template
// KickAssembler format
//=============================================

// Configuration
.const SCREEN = $0400
.const COLORRAM = $D800
.const SPRITE_PTR = SCREEN + $03F8

// Zero page variables
.const zp_temp = $FB
.const zp_frame = $FC
.const zp_joy = $FD
.const zp_score_lo = $02
.const zp_score_hi = $03

//---------------------------------------------
// Entry Point
//---------------------------------------------
*= $0801 "BASIC Starter"
    // BASIC line: 10 SYS 2064
    .byte $0C, $08, $0A, $00, $9E, $20
    .byte $32, $30, $36, $34, $00, $00, $00

*= $0810 "Main Code"
Start:
    sei                 // Disable interrupts
    
    // Initialize
    jsr InitScreen
    jsr InitSprites
    jsr InitSound
    jsr SetupIRQ
    
    cli                 // Enable interrupts
    
MainLoop:
    // Wait for frame sync
    lda zp_frame
WaitFrame:
    cmp zp_frame
    beq WaitFrame
    
    // Game logic (runs once per frame)
    jsr ReadInput
    jsr UpdatePlayer
    jsr UpdateEnemies
    jsr CheckCollisions
    jsr UpdateScore
    
    jmp MainLoop

//---------------------------------------------
// Includes
//---------------------------------------------
#import "src/init.asm"
#import "src/sprites.asm"
#import "src/input.asm"
#import "src/game.asm"
#import "src/sound.asm"

//---------------------------------------------
// Data
//---------------------------------------------
*= $2000 "Sprite Data"
SpriteData:
    .import binary "data/sprites.bin"

*= $3000 "Charset"
CharsetData:
    .import binary "data/charset.bin"

init.asm (Initialization):

//=============================================
// Initialization Routines
//=============================================

InitScreen:
    // Set border and background
    lda #0              // Black
    sta $D020           // Border
    sta $D021           // Background
    
    // Clear screen
    ldx #0
    lda #$20            // Space character
ClearLoop:
    sta SCREEN,x
    sta SCREEN+$100,x
    sta SCREEN+$200,x
    sta SCREEN+$2E8,x
    lda #1              // White
    sta COLORRAM,x
    sta COLORRAM+$100,x
    sta COLORRAM+$200,x
    sta COLORRAM+$2E8,x
    lda #$20
    inx
    bne ClearLoop
    rts

SetupIRQ:
    // Setup raster IRQ
    lda #<IRQHandler
    sta $0314
    lda #>IRQHandler
    sta $0315
    
    lda #$7F            // Disable CIA IRQs
    sta $DC0D
    sta $DD0D
    lda $DC0D           // Acknowledge pending
    lda $DD0D
    
    lda #$01            // Enable raster IRQ
    sta $D01A
    
    lda #$FA            // Raster line 250
    sta $D012
    lda $D011
    and #$7F
    sta $D011
    
    rts

IRQHandler:
    // Acknowledge IRQ
    lda #$01
    sta $D019
    
    // Increment frame counter
    inc zp_frame
    
    // Music player call would go here
    // jsr PlayMusic
    
    // Return from IRQ
    jmp $EA31           // KERNAL IRQ exit

input.asm (Joystick Reading):

//=============================================
// Input Handling
//=============================================

// Joystick bit masks
.const JOY_UP    = %00000001
.const JOY_DOWN  = %00000010
.const JOY_LEFT  = %00000100
.const JOY_RIGHT = %00001000
.const JOY_FIRE  = %00010000

ReadInput:
    // Read joystick port 2
    lda $DC00
    eor #$FF            // Invert (0=pressed)
    sta zp_joy
    rts

// Check if direction pressed (call after ReadInput)
// Returns: C=1 if pressed
CheckUp:
    lda zp_joy
    and #JOY_UP
    beq NotPressed
    sec
    rts
    
CheckDown:
    lda zp_joy
    and #JOY_DOWN
    beq NotPressed
    sec
    rts
    
CheckLeft:
    lda zp_joy
    and #JOY_LEFT
    beq NotPressed
    sec
    rts
    
CheckRight:
    lda zp_joy
    and #JOY_RIGHT
    beq NotPressed
    sec
    rts
    
CheckFire:
    lda zp_joy
    and #JOY_FIRE
    beq NotPressed
    sec
    rts
    
NotPressed:
    clc
    rts

Template 2: Demo Effect

demo.asm (Raster Bar Demo):

//=============================================
// Raster Bar Demo Template
//=============================================

*= $0801 "BASIC"
    .byte $0C,$08,$0A,$00,$9E,$20,$32,$30,$36,$34,$00,$00,$00

*= $0810 "Demo"
    sei
    
    // Black screen
    lda #0
    sta $D020
    sta $D021
    
    // Setup IRQ
    lda #$7F
    sta $DC0D
    sta $DD0D
    lda $DC0D
    lda $DD0D
    
    lda #<IRQ1
    sta $0314
    lda #>IRQ1
    sta $0315
    
    lda #$01
    sta $D01A
    
    lda #$32            // First raster line
    sta $D012
    lda $D011
    and #$7F
    sta $D011
    
    cli

Loop:
    // Update scroll position, etc.
    inc ScrollPos
    jmp Loop

//---------------------------------------------
// Raster IRQ Chain
//---------------------------------------------
IRQ1:
    pha
    txa
    pha
    tya
    pha
    
    // Stabilize raster
    lda #<IRQ1Stable
    sta $0314
    lda #>IRQ1Stable
    sta $0315
    
    lda #$01
    sta $D019
    
    inc $D012           // +1 line
    tsx
    cli
    nop
    nop
    nop
    nop
    nop
    nop
    nop

IRQ1Stable:
    // Now we're cycle-exact
    ldx #9
Delay1:
    dex
    bne Delay1
    bit $EA
    
    // Draw raster bars
    ldx #0
ColorLoop:
    lda Colors,x
    sta $D020
    sta $D021
    
    // Timing delay for each line
    ldy #7
Delay2:
    dey
    bne Delay2
    bit $EA
    
    inx
    cpx #16
    bne ColorLoop
    
    // Reset colors
    lda #0
    sta $D020
    sta $D021
    
    // Setup next frame IRQ
    lda #<IRQ1
    sta $0314
    lda #>IRQ1
    sta $0315
    
    lda #$32
    sta $D012
    
    lda #$01
    sta $D019
    
    pla
    tay
    pla
    tax
    pla
    rti

//---------------------------------------------
// Data
//---------------------------------------------
ScrollPos:
    .byte 0

Colors:
    .byte 0,11,12,15,1,15,12,11
    .byte 0,6,14,3,1,3,14,6

Template 3: SID Music Player

player.asm:

//=============================================
// SID Music Player Template
//=============================================

.const MUSIC_INIT = $1000   // Init address
.const MUSIC_PLAY = $1003   // Play address

*= $0801 "BASIC"
    .byte $0C,$08,$0A,$00,$9E,$20,$32,$30,$36,$34,$00,$00,$00

*= $0810 "Player"
    sei
    
    // Clear screen
    lda #$93
    jsr $FFD2
    
    // Print title
    ldx #0
PrintTitle:
    lda TitleText,x
    beq DonePrint
    jsr $FFD2
    inx
    bne PrintTitle
DonePrint:

    // Initialize music (song 0)
    lda #0
    jsr MUSIC_INIT
    
    // Setup IRQ for 50Hz playback
    lda #$7F
    sta $DC0D
    sta $DD0D
    lda $DC0D
    lda $DD0D
    
    lda #<MusicIRQ
    sta $0314
    lda #>MusicIRQ
    sta $0315
    
    lda #$01
    sta $D01A
    
    // PAL: Line 311 doesn't exist, use 250
    lda #$FA
    sta $D012
    lda $D011
    and #$7F
    sta $D011
    
    cli

MainLoop:
    // Check for key press
    jsr $FFE4
    beq MainLoop
    
    cmp #'1'            // Song 1
    bne NotSong1
    lda #0
    jsr MUSIC_INIT
    jmp MainLoop
NotSong1:
    cmp #'2'            // Song 2
    bne NotSong2
    lda #1
    jsr MUSIC_INIT
    jmp MainLoop
NotSong2:
    cmp #' '            // Space = stop
    bne MainLoop
    jsr SilenceSID
    jmp MainLoop

MusicIRQ:
    pha
    txa
    pha
    tya
    pha
    
    // Call music player
    jsr MUSIC_PLAY
    
    // Visual feedback
    inc $D020
    dec $D020
    
    // Acknowledge
    lda #$01
    sta $D019
    
    pla
    tay
    pla
    tax
    pla
    jmp $EA31

SilenceSID:
    lda #0
    ldx #24
SilenceLoop:
    sta $D400,x
    dex
    bpl SilenceLoop
    rts

TitleText:
    .text "SID PLAYER - 1/2=SONG SPACE=STOP"
    .byte 13, 0

//---------------------------------------------
// Music Data (load separately or include)
//---------------------------------------------
*= $1000 "Music"
    .import binary "music.sid", $7C  // Skip SID header

Template 4: Cartridge Project

cart.asm (8K Cartridge):

//=============================================
// 8K Cartridge Template
//=============================================

*= $8000 "Cartridge ROM"

// Cartridge header
    .word ColdStart     // Cold start vector
    .word WarmStart     // Warm start vector
    .byte $C3, $C2, $CD, $38, $30  // "CBM80" signature

ColdStart:
    // Hardware init
    stx $D016           // X=$00 from KERNAL
    jsr $FDA3           // Init I/O
    jsr $FD50           // Init memory
    jsr $FD15           // Init I/O vectors
    jsr $FF5B           // Init video
    cli
    
WarmStart:
    // Your program starts here
    
    // Disable BASIC ROM, keep KERNAL
    lda #$36
    sta $01
    
    // Clear screen
    lda #$93
    jsr $FFD2
    
    // Print message
    ldx #0
PrintLoop:
    lda Message,x
    beq MainLoop
    jsr $FFD2
    inx
    bne PrintLoop

MainLoop:
    // Main program loop
    inc $D020
    jmp MainLoop

Message:
    .text "CARTRIDGE ACTIVE!"
    .byte 0

// Fill to 8K
*= $9FFF
    .byte 0

cart16k.asm (16K Cartridge):

//=============================================
// 16K Cartridge Template
//=============================================

// ROML bank ($8000-$9FFF)
*= $8000 "ROML"
    .word ColdStart
    .word WarmStart  
    .byte $C3, $C2, $CD, $38, $30  // "CBM80"

ColdStart:
    stx $D016
    jsr $FDA3
    jsr $FD50
    jsr $FD15
    jsr $FF5B
    cli

WarmStart:
    // Your code here
    jmp MainLoop

MainLoop:
    jmp MainLoop

*= $9FFF
    .byte 0

// ROMH bank ($A000-$BFFF)
*= $A000 "ROMH"
    // Additional 8K of ROM
    // Data tables, graphics, etc.
    
*= $BFFF
    .byte 0

Template 5: Disk Loader with Decrunch

loader.asm:

//=============================================
// Disk Loader with IRQ Loader Template
//=============================================

*= $0801 "BASIC"
    .byte $0C,$08,$0A,$00,$9E,$20,$32,$30,$36,$34,$00,$00,$00

*= $0810 "Loader"
    // Show loading message
    ldx #0
PrintMsg:
    lda LoadMsg,x
    beq DoneMsg
    jsr $FFD2
    inx
    bne PrintMsg
DoneMsg:

    // Load main file
    lda #MainNameEnd - MainName
    ldx #<MainName
    ldy #>MainName
    jsr $FFBD           // SETNAM
    
    lda #0              // Logical file
    ldx #8              // Device 8
    ldy #0              // Load to address in file
    jsr $FFBA           // SETLFS
    
    lda #0              // 0=load
    jsr $FFD5           // LOAD
    bcs LoadError
    
    // Loading succeeded, run it
    jmp $C000           // Jump to loaded code

LoadError:
    // Print error
    ldx #0
ErrLoop:
    lda ErrorMsg,x
    beq ErrDone
    jsr $FFD2
    inx
    bne ErrLoop
ErrDone:
    rts

LoadMsg:
    .text "LOADING..."
    .byte 13, 0

ErrorMsg:
    .text "LOAD ERROR!"
    .byte 13, 0

MainName:
    .text "MAIN"
MainNameEnd:

Build System: Complete Makefile

#=============================================
# Universal C64 Project Makefile
#=============================================

# Tools (adjust paths as needed)
KICKASM = java -jar tools/KickAss.jar
ACME = acme
CC65 = cl65
VICE = x64sc
C1541 = c1541
EXOMIZER = exomizer

# Project settings
PROJECT = myproject
MAIN = src/main.asm
OUTDIR = build

# Assembler selection (kickasm/acme/cc65)
ASM = kickasm

# Output files
PRG = $(OUTDIR)/$(PROJECT).prg
D64 = $(OUTDIR)/$(PROJECT).d64
CRT = $(OUTDIR)/$(PROJECT).crt

#---------------------------------------------
# Main targets
#---------------------------------------------
all: $(PRG)

disk: $(D64)

cart: $(CRT)

run: $(PRG)
    $(VICE) $(PRG)

rundisk: $(D64)
    $(VICE) $(D64)

clean:
    rm -rf $(OUTDIR)/*

#---------------------------------------------
# Assembly
#---------------------------------------------
ifeq ($(ASM),kickasm)
$(PRG): $(MAIN) src/*.asm data/*
    @mkdir -p $(OUTDIR)
    $(KICKASM) -o $@ $(MAIN)
endif

ifeq ($(ASM),acme)
$(PRG): $(MAIN) src/*.asm data/*
    @mkdir -p $(OUTDIR)
    $(ACME) -f cbm -o $@ $(MAIN)
endif

ifeq ($(ASM),cc65)
$(PRG): src/*.c src/*.s
    @mkdir -p $(OUTDIR)
    $(CC65) -t c64 -O -o $@ $^
endif

#---------------------------------------------
# Disk image
#---------------------------------------------
$(D64): $(PRG)
    $(C1541) -format "$(PROJECT),01" d64 $@
    $(C1541) $@ -write $(PRG) "$(PROJECT)"

#---------------------------------------------
# Cartridge (requires cartconv)
#---------------------------------------------
$(CRT): $(PRG)
    cartconv -t normal -i $(PRG) -o $@

#---------------------------------------------
# Compression
#---------------------------------------------
compress: $(PRG)
    $(EXOMIZER) sfx basic $(PRG) -o $(OUTDIR)/$(PROJECT)-packed.prg

#---------------------------------------------
# Phony targets
#---------------------------------------------
.PHONY: all disk cart run rundisk clean compress

VS Code Configuration

.vscode/tasks.json:

{
    "version": "2.0.0",
    "tasks": [
        {
            "label": "Build",
            "type": "shell",
            "command": "make",
            "group": {
                "kind": "build",
                "isDefault": true
            },
            "problemMatcher": []
        },
        {
            "label": "Run in VICE",
            "type": "shell",
            "command": "make run",
            "group": "test",
            "problemMatcher": []
        },
        {
            "label": "Clean",
            "type": "shell",
            "command": "make clean",
            "problemMatcher": []
        }
    ]
}

.vscode/c64.code-snippets:

{
    "Basic Stub": {
        "prefix": "basic",
        "body": [
            "*= $$0801 \"BASIC\"",
            "    .byte $$0C,$$08,$$0A,$$00,$$9E,$$20,$$32,$$30,$$36,$$34,$$00,$$00,$$00",
            "",
            "*= $$0810 \"Code\"",
            "$0"
        ]
    },
    "IRQ Setup": {
        "prefix": "irq",
        "body": [
            "    sei",
            "    lda #$$7F",
            "    sta $$DC0D",
            "    sta $$DD0D",
            "    lda $$DC0D",
            "    lda $$DD0D",
            "    lda #<${1:IRQHandler}",
            "    sta $$0314",
            "    lda #>${1:IRQHandler}",
            "    sta $$0315",
            "    lda #$$01",
            "    sta $$D01A",
            "    lda #${2:$$FA}",
            "    sta $$D012",
            "    lda $$D011",
            "    and #$$7F",
            "    sta $$D011",
            "    cli"
        ]
    },
    "Sprite Setup": {
        "prefix": "sprite",
        "body": [
            "    // Enable sprite ${1:0}",
            "    lda $$D015",
            "    ora #(1 << ${1:0})",
            "    sta $$D015",
            "    // Position",
            "    lda #${2:100}",
            "    sta $$D000 + (${1:0} * 2)  // X",
            "    lda #${3:100}",
            "    sta $$D001 + (${1:0} * 2)  // Y",
            "    // Color",
            "    lda #${4:1}",
            "    sta $$D027 + ${1:0}",
            "    // Pointer",
            "    lda #${5:13}",
            "    sta $$07F8 + ${1:0}"
        ]
    }
}

Project Template Resources

Resource URL
C64 Project Template (GitHub) github.com/search?q=c64+project+template

Cross-Assemblers & Compilers

Popular Assemblers

Name Platform Description Link
KickAssembler Java (cross-platform) Feature-rich assembler with JavaScript-like scripting, excellent documentation, debug symbol output http://theweb.dk/KickAssembler/
ACME Windows, Linux, macOS, AmigaOS Free cross-assembler supporting 6502/65c02/65816 with illegal opcodes https://sourceforge.net/projects/acme-crossass/
64tass Windows, Linux, macOS Turbo Assembler compatible, highly popular in the scene, excellent macro support https://sourceforge.net/projects/tass64/
ca65 Cross-platform Part of cc65 suite, macro assembler with linker, supports multiple object files https://cc65.github.io/
DASM Cross-platform Classic macro assembler by Matt Dillon, multi-processor support https://dasm-assembler.github.io/
xa65 Unix/Linux Multi-pass cross-assembler for 6502 series, fast and efficient https://www.floodgap.com/retrotech/xa/
TMPx Windows, macOS, Linux Cross-platform version of Turbo Macro Pro Various sources
vasm Cross-platform Part of vbcc, supports MOS 6502 http://sun.hasenbraten.de/vasm/
c64jasm JavaScript/Node.js TypeScript/JavaScript assembler with VS Code integration https://github.com/nurpax/c64jasm

Assembler Comparison

Feature KickAssembler ACME 64tass ca65
Macro Support Excellent Good Excellent Good
Illegal Opcodes Yes Yes Yes Yes
Debug Symbols Yes (VICE, C64Debugger) Limited Yes Yes
IDE Integration VS Code, C64 Studio Multiple Multiple Multiple
Learning Curve Moderate Easy Easy Moderate

C Compilers & High-Level Languages

C/C++ Compilers

Name Description Link
cc65 Industry-standard C compiler and assembler suite for 6502 platforms https://cc65.github.io/
Oscar64 Modern optimizing C/C++ compiler with C99 support, variadic templates, lambda functions. Creates highly optimized 6502 code https://github.com/drmortalwombat/oscar64
llvm-mos LLVM-based 6502 compiler supporting C, C++, Rust. Excellent optimization https://github.com/llvm-mos/llvm-mos
KickC C compiler targeting KickAssembler output https://gitlab.com/camelot/kickc
vbcc Retargetable C compiler with 6502 backend http://www.compilers.de/vbcc.html

Other High-Level Languages

Name Language Description Link
TRSE (Turbo Rascal Syntax Error) Pascal Cross-platform IDE with Pascal compiler, ray tracer, tutorials https://lemonspawn.com/turbo-rascal-syntax-error-expected-but-alarm/
Millfork Custom Cross-compiler for a C-like language targeting 6502 https://github.com/KarolS/millfork
Prog8 Custom High-level language for 6502 with C64/X16 targets https://github.com/irmen/prog8
Mad Pascal Pascal FreePascal-based compiler for 6502 GitHub: Mad-Pascal
XC=BASIC 3 BASIC Cross-platform BASIC compiler producing machine code https://xc-basic.net/

Oscar64 Games (Proof of C Performance on C64)

Games written with Oscar64 demonstrating that C/C++ can create fast-paced action games: - Corescape - Multiple arcade-style titles - Various demos and utilities

Disassemblers & Debuggers

Disassemblers

Name Platform Description Link
Regenerator Windows Interactive C64 binary disassembler, works with .PRG and VICE snapshots, customizable output https://csdb.dk/release/?id=149429
6502bench SourceGen Windows Interactive disassembler, generates ACME/64tass/ca65/Merlin32 output, cross-references https://6502disassembly.com/

DIS6502 Cross-platform General 6502 disassembler https://sourceforge.net/projects/dis6502/
jc64dis Java Cross-platform 6502 disassembler Various

Debuggers

Name Platform Description Link
Retro Debugger Windows, macOS, Linux Real-time code/memory debugger with embedded VICE, Atari800, NestopiaUE. VIC/SID/CIA state views, source-level debugging https://github.com/slajerek/RetroDebugger
C64 65XE NES Debugger Windows, macOS, Linux Original name for Retro Debugger https://sourceforge.net/projects/c64-debugger/
IceBro Windows 6502 debugger with VICE connectivity, source-level debugging with KickAssembler https://github.com/Sakrac/IceBro
VICE Monitor Built into VICE Command-line debugger with breakpoints, memory inspection, disassembly Built into VICE

Retro Debugger Features

  • Real-time memory maps and visualization
  • VIC-II, SID, CIA state inspection
  • Timeline rewinding/scrubbing
  • Breakpoints and watchpoints
  • Source-level debugging with KickAssembler .dbg files
  • Remote debug WebSockets server
  • GoatTracker integration
  • Screenshot and export tools

Compression Tools & Crunchers

Cross-Platform Crunchers (PC)

Name Compression Decrunch Speed Description Link
Exomizer Best Slower Industry standard, best compression ratios https://github.com/bitshifters/exomizer
PuCrunch Very Good Fast Cross-platform, supports C64/C128/VIC20/Plus4 https://github.com/mist64/pucrunch
ByteBoozer 2 Good Very Fast Fast decompression, good for games CSDb
Doynamite Good Fast Good balance between size and speed CSDb
TSCrunch Good Fast Faster than Exomizer, similar compression Various
wCrush Good Moderate Based on Crush/Taboo Various

Native C64 Crunchers

Name Description
The Cruncher AB Fast native packer
ByteBoiler Popular with RLE
Cruel Cruncher Arithmetic coder (slow but good)
Sledgehammer Native cruncher

Compression Comparison

Cruncher Relative Size Decrunch Speed Use Case
Exomizer 100% (best) Slow Demos, maximum compression
PuCrunch ~103% Fast General use, multi-platform
ByteBoozer 2 ~105% Very Fast Games, fast loading
Doynamite ~104% Fast Balanced needs

Usage Tips

  • Use Exomizer for demos and one-time decompressions
  • Use PuCrunch/ByteBoozer for games needing fast decompression
  • Pre-packing not needed with cross-crunchers (they analyze data themselves)
  • Consider charpack + crunch for native crunchers

Fast Loaders

Software Fast Loaders

Name Speed Description Link
Krill’s Loader 40x+ Modern IRQ loader, supports 1541/1571/1581, on-the-fly decompression https://csdb.dk/release/?id=189130
Transwarp 40x Insanely fast 1541 loader, requires pre-encoded files By Krill
Spindle Fast IRQ loader for demos, keeps interrupts running during load CSDb
turboDisk 5-8x Simple software fast loader Compute’s Gazette

Hardware/ROM Fast Loaders

Name Speed Type Description
JiffyDOS ~9x KERNAL ROM Highly compatible, requires ROM replacement in C64 and drive
DolphinDOS ~23x Parallel Requires parallel cable
SpeedDOS ~10x Parallel Parallel transfer
Epyx FastLoad ~5x Cartridge Popular cartridge, also includes DOS wedge
Action Replay ~25x Cartridge Warp mode, also freeze/cheat cartridge
Final Cartridge III ~6x Cartridge Also includes GUI, tools

Loader Speed Comparison (Loading 177 blocks)

Loader Time Relative Speed
Stock KERNAL 1:51.5 1x
JiffyDOS ~12s 9x
DolphinDOS ~5s 23x
Transwarp 2.8s 40x

Developer Notes

  • Use Krill’s Loader for new releases
  • JiffyDOS for general use with real hardware
  • Transwarp for pre-encoded distributions
  • Consider SD2IEC/1541 Ultimate compatibility

BASIC Extensions & Compilers

BASIC Extensions

Name Commands Description Link
Simons’ BASIC 114 Popular extension by David Simons (1983), graphics/sound/structured programming Cartridge/Disk
TSB (Tuned Simons’ BASIC) 140+ Bug-fixed and enhanced Simons’ BASIC with 40+ new commands, REU support https://github.com/godot64/TSB
Simons’ BASIC Extension +91 Official extension adding RENUMBER and more Commodore
Super Expander Various Graphics and memory expansion Commodore

BASIC Compilers

Name Output Description Link

PetSpeed Machine Code True compiler, no decompiler available Various
Abacus BASIC 64 P-Code or ML Supports Simons’ BASIC standalone, choice of output Various
ZIP BASIC Pure 6502 ML Extremely fast, some limitations Loadstar
XC=BASIC 3 Machine Code Modern cross-platform BASIC compiler https://xc-basic.net/

TSB Features

  • Multiple procedure calls on single line
  • Natural language programming style
  • DOS Wedge 5.1 integration
  • HSG (High-Speed Graphics) extension
  • REU command package
  • Completely rewritten interpreter routines

40. Build Systems & CI/CD

Automation tools for professional C64 development workflows.

Makefile-Based Build Systems

Basic Makefile Structure:

# Tools (customize paths for your system)
ACME = acme
KICKASS = java -jar KickAss.jar
CC65 = cl65
C1541 = c1541
X64 = x64sc

# Build PRG from assembly
%.prg: %.asm
    $(ACME) --cpu 6510 --format cbm --outfile $@ $<

# Create D64 disk image
%.d64: %.prg
    $(C1541) -format disk,id d64 $@ -write $<

# Run in emulator
run: program.d64
    $(X64) -autostart $<

clean:
    rm -f *.prg *.d64

Assembler-Specific Build Tools

Tool Build System Features
KickAssembler Java + Gradle c64lib retro-assembler plugin, dependency management
cc65 Make Standard C toolchain, linker scripts
ACME Make Simple, fast builds
64tass Make Multi-pass, good for large projects
CBM prg Studio Built-in IDE handles builds internally (Windows)
C64 Studio Built-in IDE with project management (Windows)

c64lib Retro-Assembler (Gradle)

Modern dependency management for KickAssembler projects:

// build.gradle
plugins {
    id "com.github.c64lib.retro-assembler" version "1.6.0"
}

retroProject {
    dialect = "KickAssembler"
    dialectVersion = "5.25"
    libDirs = [".ra/deps/c64lib"]
    libFromGitHub "c64lib/common", "0.4.0"
    libFromGitHub "c64lib/chipset", "0.4.0"
    libFromGitHub "c64lib/text", "0.4.0"
    libFromGitHub "c64lib/copper64", "0.4.0"
}

Build with: ./gradlew build

VS Code Integration

Extension Assembler Features
VS64 Multiple Full IDE experience
Kick Assembler (C64) KickAssembler Build, run, debug integration
vscode-kickassembler KickAssembler Syntax highlighting, build tasks
cc65 Extension cc65 C development support

Docker Containers

Pre-configured build environments:

Container Contents Usage
barrywalker71/kickassembler KickAssembler + Java docker run -v ${PWD}:/workspace barrywalker71/kickassembler:latest /workspace/main.asm
mist64/c64 cc65 + VICE + tools Cross-platform builds
Custom Build your own with cc65, ACME, VICE Reproducible CI environments

CI/CD with GitHub Actions

Example workflow for C64 projects:

# .github/workflows/build.yml
name: Build C64 Project

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
      - name: Install cc65
        run: |
          sudo apt-get update
          sudo apt-get install -y cc65
      
      - name: Install VICE (for c1541)
        run: sudo apt-get install -y vice
      
      - name: Build
        run: make all
      
      - name: Create disk image
        run: make disk
      
      - name: Upload artifacts
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3
        with:
          name: c64-build
          path: |
            *.prg
            *.d64

c1541 Disk Image Commands

Essential commands for build automation:

# Create new D64 disk image
c1541 -format "diskname,id" d64 disk.d64

# Write file to disk
c1541 -attach disk.d64 -write program.prg program

# Write multiple files
c1541 -attach disk.d64 \
  -write game.prg game \
  -write music.prg music \
  -write sprites.prg sprites

# Create D71/D81 images
c1541 -format "disk,id" d71 disk.d71
c1541 -format "disk,id" d81 disk.d81

# Delete file from disk
c1541 -attach disk.d64 -delete oldfile

# List directory
c1541 -attach disk.d64 -list

VICE Integration

Automate testing with VICE emulator:

# Auto-start program
x64sc -autostart program.prg

# Run headless (for CI)
x64sc -console -sound -autostartprgmode 1 program.prg

# With warp mode (fast)
x64sc -warp -autostart program.prg

# Specific machine type
x64sc -model c64c -autostart program.prg

Project Templates

Repository Description Link
yackx/c64 ACME + Make template github.com/yackx/c64
c64lib KickAssembler + Gradle github.com/c64lib
cslarsen/c64-examples Simple Makefile examples github.com/cslarsen/c64-examples
digitsensitive/c64 6502 assembly examples github.com/digitsensitive/c64

BASIC Tokenizers

Convert text BASIC to tokenized PRG:

Tool Platform Notes
petcat Cross-platform Part of VICE; standard tool
tok64 Multi-platform Updated for modern systems
bas.edit Windows Visual BASIC editor
c64list Windows BASIC editor + cross-assembler

Best Practices

  1. Version control: Keep source in text format (not PRG)
  2. Reproducible builds: Use Makefiles or Gradle, document tool versions
  3. Artifact management: Generate D64 images as build outputs
  4. Testing: Use VICE for automated testing where possible
  5. Dependencies: Use c64lib or similar for library management
  6. CI/CD: GitHub Actions works well for C64 projects

41. Operating Systems & Shells

Alternative operating systems and command environments for the C64.

Graphical Operating Systems

GEOS (Graphic Environment Operating System)

Version Year Features
GEOS 1.0 1986 Original release; Mac-like GUI
GEOS 2.0 1988 Improved performance, more apps
GEOS 128 1987 C128 version; 80-column VDC support

Features: - Icon-and-window GUI with mouse/joystick support - Bundled apps: geoWrite (word processor), geoPaint (drawing), geoCalc (spreadsheet) - Desktop accessories (calculator, notepad) - TurboDisk fast loader built-in - REU support for RAM disk and caching - Third-most popular OS of its era (after MS-DOS and Mac OS)

Resources: - Source code: https://github.com/mist64/geos (reverse-engineered) - GEOS archive: lyonlabs.org/commodore/onrequest/geos/ - Wheels enhancement: Third-party upgrade with task switching

C64 OS (Modern - Commercial)

Feature Description
Developer Greg Nacu (OpCoders Inc.)
First Release 2022
Current Version 1.08 (2024)
Price From $45 USD
Website https://c64os.com

Features: - Fast text-mode GUI (not bitmap - much faster than GEOS) - Multiple desktop workspaces with customizable backdrops - Powerful File Manager with tabs, favorites, recursive operations - App Launcher with aliases - Modern storage support: SD2IEC, CMD HD, IDE64 - Wi-Fi networking support - REU support for fast app switching (up to 30 apps) - Wikipedia reader, Image Viewer, Archiver included - Active development with regular updates

Requirements: - Commodore 64/128 (or emulator) - SD2IEC, CMD device, or compatible storage - Mouse recommended (joystick works) - REU recommended for full features

GUI64

Feature Description
Developer WebFritzi Inc.
Released 2024
Style Windows-like GUI
Mode Text mode (character grid)

Features: - Movable windows with taskbar - Mouse and joystick support - Scroll wheel support via Micromys or VICE

Contiki

Feature Description
Developer Adam Dunkels
First Release 2003
Type IoT/Networking OS
Source https://github.com/contiki-os/contiki

Features: - Multitasking microkernel (~10KB RAM, ~30KB ROM) - Full TCP/IP stack (IPv4 and IPv6!) - Web browser, web server, FTP client, IRC client, Telnet - Designed for memory-constrained IoT devices - Runs on C64, C128, Apple IIe, and many microcontrollers

Unix-Like Systems

LUnix

Feature Description
Type Unix-like OS
Last Update 2004
Features Preemptive multitasking, networking, shell

Capabilities: - Command-line interface - Multiple concurrent processes - Network stack with web server - Shell scripting

GeckOS

Feature Description
Type Unix-like microkernel
Status Active (as of 2020)
Source github.com/fachat/GeckOS-V2

Features: - Preemptive multitasking - Relocatable file format - TCP/IP networking - Shell with Unix-like commands

DOS Enhancements

System Type Features
JiffyDOS Firmware upgrade 10-15x faster disk access, enhanced DOS commands
CS-DOS CMD firmware Long filenames, fast access for CMD drives
Dolphin DOS Firmware Parallel cable support, fast loading
Speed DOS Firmware Burst mode for compatible drives

CP/M

Feature Description
Version CP/M 2.2
Requirement Z80 cartridge (Commodore CP/M cartridge)
Limitations 40-column display (most CP/M software expects 80), slow due to bus timing

Available software: COBOL, FORTRAN compilers, various utilities

Command Shells & File Managers

Program Type Features
Directory Master File manager Full-screen interface, file operations
The Final Cartridge III Utility ROM Built-in file manager, ML monitor, fast loader
Action Replay Utility ROM File manager, monitor, trainer maker
CBM FileBrowser Modern SD2IEC/CMD file browser

Comparison Chart

OS GUI Multitasking Networking Storage Status
GEOS 2.0 Bitmap Limited (accessories) No Floppy, CMD, REU Classic
C64 OS Text Via REU Wi-Fi SD2IEC, CMD, IDE64 Active
Contiki GUI Yes TCP/IP v4/v6 Various Maintained
LUnix CLI Yes Yes Various Archived
GeckOS CLI Yes TCP/IP Various Active
CP/M CLI No No Floppy Classic

Getting Started Recommendations

For Productivity: - GEOS 2.0 for classic experience with word processing/graphics - C64 OS for modern interface with SD2IEC/Wi-Fi

For Networking: - Contiki for TCP/IP, web browsing, IRC - C64 OS for modern Wi-Fi integration

For Learning/Experimentation: - GeckOS or LUnix for Unix-like environment - GEOS source code study (github.com/mist64/geos)

For Speed: - JiffyDOS firmware upgrade (works with any software) - C64 OS (text-mode is much faster than bitmap GUIs)

Mobile & Multi-Platform Tools

Mobile Emulators

Android

App Description Link
C64.emu Best Android C64 emulator, based on VICE, native UI, low latency play.google.com (paid ~$4)
RetroArch Multi-system emulator with VICE core, advanced features play.google.com (free)
Frodo C64 Free C64 emulator, older but functional play.google.com
VICE for Android Official VICE port, SDL-based UI play.google.com

C64.emu Features: - Based on VICE 3.9 core - Supports C64, C64DTV, C128, VIC-20, Plus/4, PET, CBM-II - All common file formats (.d64, .g64, .t64, .tap, .crt, .prg) - ZIP/RAR/7Z archive support - Bluetooth/USB gamepad support - Configurable on-screen controls - Low audio/video latency - FastSID and ReSID sound modes

RetroArch VICE Core: - Full VICE emulation suite - Shaders, rewinding, achievements - M3U playlist for multi-disk games - Extensive controller mapping - Available on many platforms

iOS

App Description Notes
C64 Emulator Based on VICE, open source github.com/AndyQ/C64Emulator
Various App Store Check current availability Apple policy varies

Note: iOS emulator availability changes frequently due to App Store policies

Cross-Platform Development Tools

Tools that work across C64/C128/VIC-20/Plus4:

Tool Platforms Supported Description
VICE C64, C64DTV, C128, VIC-20, PET, Plus/4, CBM-II Reference emulator for all systems
cc65 C64, C128, VIC-20, Plus/4, PET, Atari, Apple II, NES C compiler and assembler suite
ACME 6502/65C02/65816 Cross-assembler for all 6502 systems
64tass 6502 family Turbo Assembler compatible
CBM prg Studio C64, C128, VIC-20, Plus/4, C16, PET Windows IDE for all CBM 8-bit
TRSE C64, C128, VIC-20, Plus/4, PET, NES, Game Boy, more Complete IDE with graphics editors
Prog8 C64, C128, PET, Commander X16 Modern structured language
Oscar64 C64, VIC-20, PET C/C++ cross-compiler

Multi-System Considerations

When developing for multiple Commodore 8-bit systems:

Aspect C64 C128 VIC-20 Plus/4
CPU 6510 @ 1MHz 8502 @ 1/2MHz 6502 @ 1MHz 7501/8501 @ 1.76MHz
RAM 64KB 128KB 5KB (expandable) 64KB
Video VIC-II VIC-IIe + VDC VIC-I TED
Sound SID SID VIC TED
Sprites 8 hardware 8 hardware None None
Colors 16 16 16 121

Code Portability Tips: - Use cc65’s platform abstraction for maximum portability - VIC-20 requires careful memory management due to limited RAM - Plus/4 TED chip is similar to VIC-II but not compatible - C128’s native mode offers more RAM but less game compatibility - Test on all target platforms via VICE emulation

RetroArch / Libretro

The vice-libretro core provides:

  • All VICE emulation modules in one package
  • Consistent interface across Windows, Mac, Linux, Android, iOS, Switch, Vita
  • Save states, rewind, netplay
  • CRT shaders for authentic display
  • Achievement support (RetroAchievements)

Repository: github.com/libretro/vice-libretro

Web-Based Mobile Access

Browser-based emulators work on mobile devices:

Emulator URL Mobile Notes
Virtual Consoles C64 virtualconsoles.com/online-emulators/c64 Touch-friendly
Retro Machine retromachine.org Mobile browser support
C64 Online c64online.com Basic mobile support

Note: Performance and usability varies by device and browser

35. SID Hardware Alternatives

Modern replacement chips and add-ons for the MOS 6581/8580 SID sound chip.

Drop-In Replacements

Device Technology Features Price Range Where to Buy
FPGASID FPGA Most accurate emulation; dual SID; stereo; paddle/mouse support; audio input ~€75 fpgasid.de
ARMSID ARM Cortex M4 Excellent accuracy; 6581/8580 modes; paddle support; firmware updates ~€35-40 retrocomp.cz
ARM2SID ARM Dual SID on single chip; stereo output ~€50 retrocomp.cz
SIDKick Teensy 4.1 Dual SID + FM (OPL) emulation; reSID-based; open source ~€40-50 DIY or assembled
SIDKick pico Raspberry Pi Pico Budget dual SID; reSID 0.16/1.0; FM support; open source ~€15-25 DIY or PCBWay
PDsid RP2350 (Pico 2) Ultra-budget; own emulation model; 6581/8580 switch via reset; paddle support; envelope bug fix option ~€10 uni64.com
SwinSID AVR (Atmel) Early replacement; limited accuracy; no paddle support ~€20-30 Various
SwinSID Nano AVR Smallest form factor; same limitations as SwinSID ~€20-30 Various
BackSID ARM Newer design; distortion settings; frequent updates ~€35 backbit.io
KungFuSID STM32/GD32 (ARM) Open source; based on Kung Fu Flash hardware; reSID-based; 6581/8580 emulation; DIY-friendly ~€5-15 DIY (GitHub/JLCPCB)
X-SID FPGA Newer FPGA design Varies Individual Computers

Accuracy Comparison (Community Consensus)

Rank Device Notes
1 FPGASID Gold standard; nearly indistinguishable from real SID
2 ARMSID Excellent; slightly darker sound than original
3 SIDKick/pico Very good; some report darker highs on pico
4 BackSID Good and improving with updates
5 SwinSID Acceptable for basic use; issues with complex tunes
6 PDsid New (2025); good reports; own emulation model; very affordable
7 KungFuSID Improving rapidly; some combined waveform issues; active development

Stereo SID Solutions

Solution Description Link
SIDFX Dual-SID adapter board; supports mixed 6581/8580; dedicated stereo output; 8580 digi fix https://www.sidfx.dk/
Stereo SID Board DIY PCB for adding second SID at D420/D500/$DE00 https://github.com/tebl/C64-Stereo-SID
Ultimate 64 Built-in dual SID sockets plus emulated SIDs gideonzweijtzer.nl
SIDKick/pico Single chip can emulate dual SIDs Built-in feature
FPGASID Emulates 2 SIDs from single device Built-in feature

Common Second SID Addresses

Address Usage
D420 *  * |Mostcommonstereoaddress|| * *D500 Alternative (not on C128)
DE00 *  * |I/Oexpansionarea|| * *DF00 I/O expansion area

Choosing a SID Replacement

For Maximum Accuracy: FPGASID (expensive but best)

Best Value: ARMSID (great sound, paddle support, reasonable price)

Budget Option: SIDKick pico (DIY-friendly, dual SID + FM)

Cheapest DIY Option: KungFuSID (open source, ~€5 BOM, uses Kung Fu Flash or dedicated PCB)

Ultra-Budget Ready-Made: PDsid (~€10, plug and play, paddle support)

For Stereo: SIDFX (with real SIDs) or SIDKick/FPGASID (emulated)

Avoid Removing Real SIDs: Use replacements to save working SIDs in original machines

Configuration Tools

Most replacements include on-device configuration:

  • ARMSID: SYS commands from BASIC; downloadable config tool
  • SIDKick pico: SYS 54301 or SYS 54333 for built-in menu
  • FPGASID: On-board configuration menu
  • PDsid: Hold RESET 3 seconds to toggle 6581/8580; USB firmware updates
  • SIDFX: Hardware switches and software configuration

Resources

  • SID Replacement Wiki: https://www.c64-wiki.com/wiki/SID_replacement
  • SIDKick pico GitHub: https://github.com/frntc/SIDKick-pico
  • ARMSID Info: https://retrocomp.cz/produkt?id=2
  • SIDFX Details: https://www.sidfx.dk/details
  • KungFuSID GitHub: https://github.com/Sgw32/KungFuSID
  • KungFuSID Lemon64 Thread: https://www.lemon64.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=77556
  • PDsid (UNI64): https://uni64.com/shop/index.php?system=4&m=70&item_id=7536
  • PDsid Lemon64 Thread: https://www.lemon64.com/forum/viewtopic.php?t=88188

SID Sound Design Guide

Comprehensive guide to programming the MOS 6581/8580 SID chip for music and sound effects.

SID Architecture Overview

The SID is a 3-voice synthesizer with features rivaling professional synthesizers of its era:

Component Per Voice Shared
Oscillator 3 independent -
Waveforms 4 selectable -
ADSR Envelope 3 independent -
Filter Routable per voice 1 multimode filter
Ring Modulation Voice 1←3, Voice 2←1 -
Hard Sync Voice 1←3, Voice 2←1, Voice 3←2 -

SID Register Map (D400−D418)

Address Register Description
D400−D401 FREQ1 LO/HI Voice 1 frequency (16-bit)
D402−D403 PW1 LO/HI Voice 1 pulse width (12-bit)
$D404 CR1 Voice 1 control register
D405−D406 AD1/SR1 Voice 1 ADSR
D407−D40D Voice 2 Same layout as Voice 1
D40E−D414 Voice 3 Same layout as Voice 1
D415−D416 FC LO/HI Filter cutoff frequency (11-bit)
$D417 RES/FILT Resonance (4-bit) + filter routing
$D418 MODE/VOL Filter mode + master volume

Waveforms

Waveform Bit Character Harmonics Typical Use
Triangle 4 ($10) Mellow, flute-like Odd only Soft leads, bass
Sawtooth 5 ($20) Bright, brassy All Leads, brass, strings
Pulse 6 ($40) Variable, hollow to reedy Varies with duty Versatile, PWM effects
Noise 7 ($80) Random, percussive White/blue noise Drums, effects, wind

Combined Waveforms: Selecting multiple waveforms ANDs them together, creating unique timbres (but not all combinations are useful).

ADSR Envelope

Controls the amplitude contour of each voice:

Phase Values Duration Range
Attack 0-15 2ms - 8 seconds
Decay 0-15 6ms - 24 seconds
Sustain 0-15 Level (not time) 0-100%
Release 0-15 6ms - 24 seconds

Common ADSR Settings: | Sound Type | Attack | Decay | Sustain | Release | |————|——–|——-|———|———| | Pluck/Piano | 0 | 9 | 0 | 0 | | Organ | 0 | 0 | 15 | 0 | | Strings | 12 | 0 | 12 | 9 | | Brass Stab | 2 | 6 | 8 | 4 | | Kick Drum | 0 | 9 | 0 | 0 |

Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)

The “holy grail” of SID sound design (Chris Hülsbeck):

Duty Cycle Sound Character
$000 Silent (DC)
$400 (25%) Hollow, clarinet-like
$800 (50%) Square wave, rich
$C00 (75%) Same as 25% (symmetric)
$FFF Silent (DC)

PWM Animation: Sweep pulse width over time for animated, phasing sound:

; Simple PWM sweep in IRQ
    inc pwm_lo
    bne +
    inc pwm_hi
+   lda pwm_lo
    sta $d402
    lda pwm_hi
    and #$0f
    sta $d403

Ring Modulation

Creates non-harmonic overtones (bells, gongs, metallic sounds):

Voice Modulated By Requirement
Voice 1 Voice 3 Triangle waveform on Voice 1
Voice 2 Voice 1 Triangle waveform on Voice 2
Voice 3 Voice 2 Triangle waveform on Voice 3

Ring Mod Tips: - Modulator frequency affects overtone spacing - Small frequency ratios = bell-like - Large ratios = metallic/harsh - Modulator doesn’t need ADSR (can be silent oscillator)

Hard Sync

Resets one oscillator’s phase when another crosses zero:

Voice Synced To
Voice 1 Voice 3
Voice 2 Voice 1
Voice 3 Voice 2

Sync Tips: - Synced oscillator frequency > master = more harmonics - Sweeping synced frequency = classic “sync sweep” lead - Master frequency sets fundamental pitch

Filter

Single multimode filter shared by all voices:

Mode Bit Character
Low Pass 4 ($10) Removes highs, warm/muffled
Band Pass 5 ($20) Removes highs and lows, nasal/vocal
High Pass 6 ($40) Removes lows, thin/bright
Notch LP+HP Removes narrow band

Filter Parameters: - Cutoff (11-bit): D415/D416 - frequency point - Resonance (4-bit): $D417 high nibble - emphasis at cutoff - Routing: $D417 low nibble - which voices pass through filter

Warning: Filter characteristics vary significantly between 6581 and 8580 chips, and even between individual 6581s!

Digi/Sample Playback

$D418 Volume Trick (6581 only):

; 4-bit sample playback via volume register
    lda sample_data,x
    and #$0f
    sta $d418
  • Works on 6581, silent on 8580 without modification
  • 4-bit resolution, requires significant CPU time
  • Used in games like Ghostbusters, Impossible Mission

PWM Sample Playback: - Higher quality (8-bit possible) - Uses pulse width register - Works on both SID versions

Sound Design Recipes

Sound Waveform ADSR Notes
Bass Pulse 25-50% 0/6/8/2 Filter LP, low cutoff
Lead Saw or Pulse PWM 2/4/10/6 Add vibrato
Strings Saw 10/2/12/8 Chorus via detune
Snare Noise 0/8/0/4 Short, punchy
Kick Triangle 0/9/0/0 Pitch bend down
Hi-hat Noise 0/4/0/1 HP filter, very short
Bell Ring mod 0/11/4/9 Triangle + ring

Advanced Techniques

Technique Description
Arpeggio Rapid note changes to simulate chords
Vibrato Small frequency modulation for expression
Portamento Smooth pitch slides between notes
Wavetable Rapid waveform/param changes per frame
Filter Sweep Animated cutoff for wah/sweep effects
Combined Waveforms AND multiple waves for unique timbres

6581 vs 8580 Differences

Aspect 6581 8580
Filter Variable, “character” Consistent, brighter
Combined Waves More output Less output
Digi via $D418 Works Silent (needs mod)
DC Offset Higher Lower
Sound Character “Gritty,” warm Clean, accurate

Music Editors & Tools

Tool Platform Description

SID Factory II Cross-platform Professional features, reSID
SID-Wizard C64 native Full-featured native tracker
Deflemask Cross-platform Multi-chip tracker with SID support
CheeseCutter Cross-platform Tracker with GoatTracker compatibility

Resource URL Description
SID Primer ffd2.com/fridge/meow/docs/SID.primer Interactive SID tutorial
C64 PRG Chapter 4 Various Official sound programming guide
Codebase64 SID codebase64.org Code examples and tutorials
SID Factory II Tutorial blog.chordian.net Step-by-step composition guide

Music Composition Tutorial

A practical guide to creating SID music using modern cross-platform and native C64 trackers.

Choosing a Tracker

Tracker Platform Best For Link
GoatTracker PC/Mac/Linux Beginners, cross-platform covertbitops.c64.org
SID-Wizard C64 (native) Advanced, native workflow csdb.dk
Deflemask PC/Mac/Linux Multi-chip (not C64-only) deflemask.com
SID-Factory II PC Professional features github.com/Chordian/sidfactory2

GoatTracker Basics

GoatTracker is recommended for beginners due to its PC-based workflow.

Interface Layout:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Pattern Editor (3 channels)             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Track 1 │ Track 2 │ Track 3 │ Instr.   │
│ C-4 01  │ --- --  │ E-4 02  │ Editor   │
│ D-4 01  │ G-4 03  │ --- --  │          │
│ ...     │ ...     │ ...     │          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Wavetable │ Pulsetable │ Filtertable   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

Key Commands:

Key Function
F1-F4 Select octave 1-4
F5 Pattern editor
F6 Order list
F7 Instrument editor
Space Play/Stop
Enter Play from cursor
A-L, Z-M Note entry (keyboard piano)

Creating Your First Sound

Step 1: Basic Pulse Wave 1. Press F7 (Instrument Editor) 2. Set Attack: 0, Decay: 8, Sustain: 8, Release: 4 3. In Wavetable, enter: 41 00 (pulse wave, gate on) 4. Press Space to test

Step 2: Add Movement

Wavetable:
Row 1: 41 00  ; Pulse wave, relative note
Row 2: 40 00  ; Continue pulse
Row 3: FF 00  ; Jump to row 1

Pulsetable:
Row 1: 08 80  ; Pulse width $800 (50% duty)
Row 2: 87 20  ; Sweep right, speed $20

SID Waveforms

Waveform Value Character
Triangle $10 Soft, flute-like
Sawtooth $20 Bright, brassy
Pulse $40 Variable, can be thin to square
Noise $80 Percussion, effects
Combined Sum Metallic, unique timbres

Useful Combinations: - $50 (Pulse + Triangle) = Metallic tone - $30 (Saw + Triangle) = Soft saw - $21 (Saw + Gate) = Standard lead

ADSR Explained

Volume
  │     ╱╲
  │    ╱  ╲
  │   ╱    ╲────────╮
  │  ╱              │╲
  │ ╱               │ ╲
  └─┴───────────────┴──┴──► Time
    A   D    S        R
Parameter Range Effect
Attack 0-F Time to reach peak (0=2ms, F=8s)
Decay 0-F Time to fall to sustain (0=6ms, F=24s)
Sustain 0-F Held volume level
Release 0-F Fade time after key up

Common ADSR Settings:

Sound Type A D S R
Piano/Pluck 0 9 0 0
Pad/String 8 0 F 8
Lead 0 8 8 4
Bass 0 6 0 0
Drum 0 2 0 0

Filter Programming

The SID has one filter shared by all channels.

Filter Types:

Type Value Sound
Low-pass $10 Cuts highs, warm
Band-pass $20 Cuts both ends, nasal
High-pass $40 Cuts lows, thin
Combined Sum Various characters

Filter Sweep (GoatTracker):

Filtertable:
Row 1: 90 17  ; Cutoff $900, res $7, filter ch 1
Row 2: 8F 10  ; Sweep down, speed $10
Row 3: FF 00  ; Loop

Creating Instruments

Lead Sound

ADSR: 08 84
Wavetable:
  21 00  ; Saw + gate
  20 00  ; Saw continue
  FF 01  ; Loop to row 1

Bass Sound

ADSR: 00 60
Wavetable:
  41 00  ; Pulse + gate
  21 00  ; Saw (click)
  20 00  ; Saw continue
  FF 02  ; Loop
Pulsetable:
  08 00  ; Fixed $800

Drum (Kick)

ADSR: 00 50
Wavetable:
  81 30  ; Noise + high note offset
  11 00  ; Triangle
  10 0C  ; Drop pitch
  00 00  ; Gate off

Arpeggio

ADSR: 00 A0
Wavetable:
  21 00  ; Root
  20 04  ; +4 semitones (major third)
  20 07  ; +7 semitones (fifth)
  FF 01  ; Loop

Song Structure

Order List:

Ch1: 00 01 02 00 01 02 03 FE 00
Ch2: 04 05 06 04 05 06 07 FE 04
Ch3: 08 09 0A 08 09 0A 0B FE 08
      ↑intro ↑verse    ↑end ↑loop

Pattern Layout:

Pattern 00 (Intro bass):
C-2 01 ...
--- -- ...
G-2 01 ...
--- -- ...

Tips from SID Composers

  1. Hard Restart - Use $09 (test bit) before notes for clean attacks
  2. Vibrato - Small pitch modulation adds life
  3. Pulse Width Modulation - Essential for evolving sounds
  4. Filter Sweeps - Create movement and interest
  5. Channel Economy - SID only has 3 channels, plan carefully
  6. 6581 vs 8580 - Test on both, filters differ significantly

Exporting Your Music

From GoatTracker: 1. File → Save SID - Creates .sid file for players 2. File → Save PRG - Creates C64 executable

SID Player Addresses: - Init: Call with subtune in A register - Play: Call every frame (50/60Hz)

Integration Example:

    lda #0          ; Subtune 0
    jsr $1000       ; Init music
    
mainloop:
    jsr $1003       ; Play one frame
    ; ... your code ...
    jmp mainloop

Resources

Resource URL Description
GoatTracker Manual Included with GT Official documentation
SID-Wizard Manual csdb.dk Comprehensive guide
Retro64 Tutorial retro64.altervista.org Step-by-step beginner guide
ChipMusic.org chipmusic.org Community forum
HVSC hvsc.c64.org Listen to 50,000+ SID tunes
Remix64 remix64.com SID covers and remixes

Practice Exercises

  1. Hello SID - Create a simple pulse wave instrument
  2. Drum Kit - Make kick, snare, hi-hat using noise + pitch
  3. Arpeggio Lead - Build a rapid chord-cycling sound
  4. Filter Sweep - Create a “wah” effect with filter
  5. Full Song - 16-bar loop with bass, lead, and drums

Name Platform Description Link
SpritePad C64 Pro Windows Professional sprite/tile editor with animation, import/export https://subchristsoftware.itch.io/spritepad-c64-pro

VIC-II Programming Tricks

Advanced graphics techniques that exploit the VIC-II chip’s behavior to achieve effects beyond its documented capabilities.

Understanding Badlines

A “badline” occurs every 8 raster lines when the VIC-II fetches character data from RAM, stealing 40-43 CPU cycles. Understanding badlines is essential for all advanced VIC-II tricks.

Concept Description
Badline Condition Occurs when lower 3 bits of $D011 match lower 3 bits of raster counter
Cycle Stealing VIC-II takes bus control, CPU halted for ~40 cycles
Raster Time PAL: 63 cycles/line, NTSC: 65 cycles/line
Available Cycles Normal line: 63, Badline: ~23 cycles for CPU

Core VIC-II Tricks

Technique Description Use Cases
FLD (Flexible Line Distance) Manipulate $D011 to prevent badlines, creating gaps in display Vertical scrolling effects, opening vertical borders
FLI (Flexible Line Interpretation) Force badlines every raster for per-line color changes High-color bitmap modes (AFLI, IFLI, NUFLI)
VSP (Variable Screen Position) DMA delay trick for fast horizontal scrolling Smooth scrolling at low CPU cost (use with caution - can crash some VIC-IIs)
AGSP (Any Given Screen Position) Combine FLD/FLI for arbitrary screen positioning Demo effects, split screens
Tech-Tech Change X-scroll per raster line Wavy text/graphics effects
Linecrunch Skip display lines by manipulating badline timing Vertical compression, more sprites

Border Tricks

Technique How It Works Result
Open Top/Bottom Border Switch 24/25 row mode at precise moment Sprites visible in vertical border
Open Side Borders Switch 38/40 column mode with cycle-exact timing Sprites visible in horizontal border
Full Border Open Combine both techniques Sprites anywhere on screen

Border Opening Code Pattern:

; Open top/bottom border (simplified)
    lda #$1b        ; 25 rows
    sta $d011
    ; ... wait for correct raster ...
    lda #$13        ; 24 rows  
    sta $d011       ; VIC misses border trigger

Sprite Multiplexing

Display more than 8 sprites by repositioning them after they’ve been drawn:

Approach Complexity Sprites Possible
Zone-based Low 16-24 (fixed zones)
Sorted IRQ Medium 24-32 (Y-sorted)
Full Multiplex High 50+ (complex sorting)

Key Concepts: - Sprites can be repositioned after raster passes their Y position - Y-coordinate sorting determines draw order - Need ~21 raster lines between sprite uses - Sorting algorithms: insertion sort, bucket sort, “Ocean sort”

Resources: - Codebase64: codebase64.org/doku.php?id=base:sprites - Sprite multiplexer sorting: codebase64.org/doku.php?id=base:sprite_multiplexer_sorting - Cadaver’s sprite article: cadaver.github.io/rants/sprite.html

FLI Modes (High Color Graphics)

Mode Resolution Colors Description
Standard FLI 160×200 4 per 4×8 cell Multicolor with per-line color changes
AFLI 320×200 2 per 8×8 + extras Hires FLI
IFLI 320×200 Blended Interlaced FLI (flickering blend)
NUFLI 320×200 Near-full Most advanced, sprite underlays
MUIFLI 160×200 Maximum Multicolor interlaced FLI

FLI Bug: First 3 characters of each row show garbage due to timing constraints.

Stable Raster

Essential for all timing-critical effects:

Method Cycles Jitter Complexity
Double IRQ 0 Medium
CIA Timer 0-1 Medium
Sprite 0 Collision 0 Low-Medium
NOP Slide 0 High

Essential References

Resource URL Description
VIC-II Article (Bauer) zimmers.net/cbmpics/cbm/c64/vic-ii.txt Definitive technical reference
Codebase64 VIC codebase64.org/doku.php?id=base:vic Tutorials and code examples
C64 OS FLI Series c64os.com/post/flitiming1 Deep dive into FLI timing
Fairlight VIC Tricks YouTube 2-hour video tutorial on VIC tricks
VSP Crash Analysis bumbershootsoft.wordpress.com VSP bug explanation and mitigation

Common D011/D016 Manipulations

Register Bits Effect
D011bit3 *  * |DEN|Displayenable(cleartoopenborders)|| * *D011 bit 4 RSEL 24/25 row select
D011bits0 − 2 *  * |YSCROLL|Verticalscroll(0−7)|| * *D016 bit 3 CSEL 38/40 column select
$D016 bits 0-2 XSCROLL Horizontal scroll (0-7)

Sprite Multiplexing Deep Dive

Techniques for displaying more than 8 sprites on screen by reusing hardware sprites as the raster beam moves down.

The Problem

The C64’s VIC-II chip provides only 8 hardware sprites. Many games need more - Green Beret, Ghosts’n Goblins, Turrican, and Creatures all display 20+ moving objects. The solution: sprite multiplexing.

Core Concept

Since sprites are drawn when the raster beam passes their Y-position, we can: 1. Display sprite 0 at the top of the screen 2. Wait until raster passes below sprite 0 3. Reposition sprite 0 to a lower Y-position with new graphics 4. Sprite 0 appears twice - at the top AND bottom

Implementation Steps

1. Virtual Sprite Array

; Define virtual sprites (more than 8)
MAX_VIRTUAL = 24

virtual_x:     .fill MAX_VIRTUAL, 0   ; X coordinates
virtual_y:     .fill MAX_VIRTUAL, 0   ; Y coordinates  
virtual_ptr:   .fill MAX_VIRTUAL, 0   ; Sprite pointers
virtual_color: .fill MAX_VIRTUAL, 0   ; Colors

2. Sort by Y-Coordinate

Sprites must be processed top-to-bottom. Common algorithms:

Algorithm Best Case Worst Case Notes
Insertion Sort O(n) O(n²) Best for nearly-sorted data (typical in games)
Bucket Sort O(n) O(n) Fast but uses more memory
Ocean Sort Variable Poor Hybrid, good for games

Simple Insertion Sort:

; Sort virtual sprites by Y coordinate
; Uses sorted_order[] to store indices
sort_sprites:
        ldx #0
        stx sort_count
        
@outer: cpx #MAX_VIRTUAL
        beq @done
        lda virtual_y,x           ; Get Y of sprite X
        ldy sort_count
        beq @insert               ; First sprite, just insert
        
@find:  dey
        bmi @insert
        sty temp
        lda sorted_order,y
        tay
        lda virtual_y,y
        ldy temp
        cmp virtual_y,x
        bcc @insert               ; Found position
        
        ; Shift entries up
        lda sorted_order,y
        sta sorted_order+1,y
        dey
        bpl @find
        
@insert:
        iny
        txa
        sta sorted_order,y
        inc sort_count
        inx
        bne @outer
@done:  rts

3. Raster IRQ Chain

Set up IRQ to fire before each sprite needs repositioning:

; IRQ handler for sprite multiplexing
sprite_irq:
        pha
        txa
        pha
        tya
        pha
        
        ; Acknowledge VIC interrupt
        lda #$ff
        sta $d019
        
        ; Get current virtual sprite index
        ldx mux_index
        cpx sort_count
        bcs @done                 ; All sprites displayed
        
        ; Map to hardware sprite (0-7)
        lda mux_index
        and #$07
        tay                       ; Y = hardware sprite 0-7
        
        ; Get virtual sprite data
        lda sorted_order,x
        tax
        
        ; Set sprite position
        lda virtual_x,x
        sta $d000,y               ; Sprite X low
        lda virtual_y,x  
        sta $d001,y               ; Sprite Y
        
        ; Set sprite pointer
        lda virtual_ptr,x
        sta $07f8,y               ; Sprite pointer
        
        ; Set color
        lda virtual_color,x
        sta $d027,y
        
        ; Handle X high bit ($D010)
        ; ... (bit manipulation for X > 255)
        
        ; Set up next IRQ
        inc mux_index
        ldx mux_index
        cpx sort_count
        bcs @last_sprite
        
        lda sorted_order,x
        tax
        lda virtual_y,x
        sec
        sbc #21                   ; IRQ before sprite appears
        sta $d012
        jmp @exit
        
@last_sprite:
        ; Reset for next frame
        lda #0
        sta mux_index
        lda #$fb                  ; Bottom of screen
        sta $d012
        
@exit:  pla
        tay
        pla
        tax
        pla
@done:  rti

4. Handle X High Bit ($D010)

For X coordinates > 255:

; Set X position with MSB handling
; X = hardware sprite number (0-7)
; A = virtual sprite X low byte
; C = X high bit (set if X >= 256)
set_sprite_x:
        sta $d000,x               ; X low byte
        lda $d010
        bcc @clear_bit
        ora sprite_bit,x          ; Set high bit
        bne @store
@clear_bit:
        eor sprite_bit,x
        and sprite_bit,x
        eor $d010
@store: sta $d010
        rts

sprite_bit: .byte 1,2,4,8,16,32,64,128

Timing Considerations

Critical timing points:

Issue Solution
IRQ too late Compare $D012 before setting; if late, fire immediately
Sprite flicker Never reposition sprite being drawn (21 lines minimum gap)
Too many on one line Limit to 8 per scanline; disable extras
Music slowdown Check if next IRQ imminent before long operations

Late IRQ Detection:

        ; Set next IRQ position
        sta $d012
        sec
        sbc #3                    ; Safety margin
        cmp $d012                 ; Already passed?
        bcc go_to_irq_directly    ; Yes, handle immediately

Zone-Based Multiplexing

Simpler alternative: divide screen into horizontal zones.

Zone 1: Rasterlines   50-100  (Sprites 0-7)
Zone 2: Rasterlines  100-150  (Sprites 0-7 reused)  
Zone 3: Rasterlines  150-200  (Sprites 0-7 reused)

Advantages: - Simpler sorting (just ensure sprites stay in zones) - Predictable IRQ timing - Used in many commercial games

Limitations: - Sprites can’t freely cross zone boundaries - Requires game design around zones

Practical Limits

Scenario Realistic Maximum
General multiplexer 24-32 sprites
Zone-based (3 zones) 24 sprites (8 per zone)
Optimized assembly 44+ sprites (with tricks)
Per scanline 8 (hardware limit)

Memory Requirements

For 32 virtual sprites:

Sort index:    32 bytes
X positions:   32 bytes  
Y positions:   32 bytes
Pointers:      32 bytes
Colors:        32 bytes
-----------------------
Total:        160 bytes (fits in zero page)

Ready-to-Use Multiplexers

Name Author Features Link
Cadaver’s Multiplexer Lasse Öörni Ocean sorting, well-documented https://cadaver.github.io/rants/sprite.html
C64 Multiplexer C LIB Fabrizio Caruso C interface, 36 sprites https://github.com/Fabrizio-Caruso/C64_Multiplexer_C_LIB
Shallan’s Multiplexer Shallan64 KickAssembler, video tutorial https://github.com/Shallan64/spritemultiplexer
zendar’s Multiplexer zendar Based on Cadaver’s code https://github.com/zendar/spritemultiplexer

Video Tutorials

Resource Description
Shallan64 - Sprite Multiplexing Complete walkthrough with code
Codebase64 Sorting Article Algorithm comparison with benchmarks

Code Example: Simple 16-Sprite Multiplexer

Complete working example (KickAssembler):

//------------------------------------------
// Simple 16-sprite multiplexer
// Sprites 0-7: top half, Sprites 8-15: bottom half
//------------------------------------------

.pc = $0801
:BasicUpstart2(start)

.pc = $1000
start:
        sei
        
        // Setup sprites
        lda #$ff
        sta $d015              // Enable all 8 sprites
        
        // Set initial positions for first 8 virtual sprites
        ldx #7
!loop:  txa
        asl
        asl
        asl
        clc
        adc #24
        sta $d000,x            // X position
        lda #80
        sta $d001,x            // Y position (top half)
        lda #13
        sta $07f8,x            // Sprite pointer
        lda #1
        sta $d027,x            // Color white
        dex
        bpl !loop-
        
        // Setup raster IRQ
        lda #$7f
        sta $dc0d
        sta $dd0d
        lda #$01
        sta $d01a
        
        lda #<irq_switch
        sta $0314
        lda #>irq_switch
        sta $0315
        
        lda #100               // IRQ at line 100
        sta $d012
        lda $d011
        and #$7f
        sta $d011
        
        lda #$35
        sta $01
        cli
        
!main:  jmp !main-

//------------------------------------------
// IRQ: Switch to bottom 8 sprites
//------------------------------------------
irq_switch:
        pha
        txa
        pha
        
        lda #$ff
        sta $d019
        
        // Reposition sprites for bottom half
        ldx #7
!loop:  txa
        asl
        asl
        asl
        clc
        adc #24
        sta $d000,x
        lda #180               // Y position (bottom half)
        sta $d001,x
        lda #14                // Different sprite pointer
        sta $07f8,x
        lda #7                 // Yellow
        sta $d027,x
        dex
        bpl !loop-
        
        // Setup IRQ for next frame
        lda #<irq_reset
        sta $0314
        lda #>irq_reset
        sta $0315
        lda #250
        sta $d012
        
        pla
        tax
        pla
        rti

//------------------------------------------  
// IRQ: Reset for next frame
//------------------------------------------
irq_reset:
        pha
        txa
        pha
        
        lda #$ff
        sta $d019
        
        // Reset to top sprites
        ldx #7
!loop:  txa
        asl
        asl
        asl
        clc
        adc #24
        sta $d000,x
        lda #80
        sta $d001,x
        lda #13
        sta $07f8,x
        lda #1
        sta $d027,x
        dex
        bpl !loop-
        
        // Setup switch IRQ
        lda #<irq_switch
        sta $0314
        lda #100
        sta $d012
        
        pla
        tax
        pla
        rti

EasySplit Compress and split large CRT files skoe.de
EasyCrtMaker Compile multiple cartridges into one image skoe.de
cartconv VICE tool for CRT conversion/creation VICE distribution
EasyFlash 3 USB Utilities PC transfer tools for EF3 USB port GitHub
CRT2CHIP Convert CRT to BIN files for EPROM programming Various

CRT File Format

The CRT format is the standard for C64 cartridge images:

Header (64 bytes):
- Signature: "C64 CARTRIDGE"
- Header length, version
- Cartridge type (determines banking behavior)
- EXROM/GAME line states
- Cartridge name

CHIP Packets (variable):
- Signature: "CHIP"
- Total packet length
- Chip type (ROM, RAM, Flash)
- Bank number
- Starting load address
- ROM image size
- ROM data

Cartridge Types Reference

Type ID Name Size Notes
0 Normal 8KB/16KB ROML at $8000, ROMH at $A000
1 Action Replay 32KB Freezer cartridge
5 Ocean 128-512KB Bank switching via $DE00
19 Magic Desk 32-128KB Bank switching, popular for compilations
32 EasyFlash 1MB 64 banks, ROML+ROMH, 256 bytes RAM at $DF00
33 EasyFlash xbank 1MB Extended banking
57 RGCD 64KB 8 x 8KB banks
60 GMod2 512KB EEPROM save support

EasyFlash Programming Guide

Memory Layout: - 2 x 512KB Flash chips (ROML at $8000, ROMH at A000/E000) - 64 banks selectable via $DE00 - 256 bytes RAM at DF00−DFFF - Control register at $DE02

Control Register ($DE02):

Bit 7: LED control
Bit 2: Game line
Bit 1: EXROM line  
Bit 0: Mode (0=16KB, 1=Ultimax)

Common values:
$04 - Cartridge ROM off
$05 - Ultimax mode
$06 - 8KB cartridge
$07 - 16KB cartridge

Bank Selection ($DE00): - Write bank number (0-63) to select active bank - Both ROML and ROMH switch together - Write-only register
