Buying Tips

  1. Check shipping costs - European shops often have high shipping to USA/Canada and vice versa
  2. Compare prices - Same products available at multiple shops, prices vary
  3. Join forums - Lemon64, Forum64.de often have group buys and deals
  4. Watch for restocks - Popular items like Ultimate-II+ sell out quickly
  5. Read reviews - CSDb and Lemon64 forums have user experiences
  6. Consider DIY - Many projects are open source (Kung Fu Flash, Pi1541)

Marketplaces (Used/Vintage)

Platform Notes
eBay Wide selection but watch for scalpers; check seller feedback
Tindie Indie hardware marketplace; many C64 DIY kits, replacement chips, and accessories from makers
PCBWay Project Shared open hardware projects; C64 PCBs and designs for DIY builds
Facebook Marketplace Local deals possible
Lemon64 Forums Community sales section
Forum64.de German community marketplace

36. Memory Expansion Programming

Programming guides for REU, GeoRAM, and SuperCPU memory expansions.

REU (RAM Expansion Unit)

The Commodore 17xx REU series (1700/1764/1750) uses DMA for fast memory transfers.

REU Models

Model RAM Banks Notes
1700 128 KB 2 C128 targeted
1764 256 KB 4 C64 targeted; includes upgraded PSU
1750 512 KB 8 Most common; upgradeable to 2MB+
Clone/Modern Up to 16 MB 256 1541 Ultimate, RAD, etc.

REU Registers (DF00−DF0A)

Register Address Function
Status $DF00 IRQ status, version bits
Command $DF01 Transfer type (C64→REU, REU→C64, swap, compare)
C64 Base Lo/Hi DF02−DF03 C64 memory address (16-bit)
REU Base Lo/Hi/Bank DF04−DF06 REU address (24-bit)
Transfer Length DF07−DF08 Bytes to transfer (16-bit)
IRQ Mask $DF09 Interrupt configuration
Address Control $DF0A Address counting control

REU Transfer Example (Assembly)

; Transfer screen ($0400-$07FF) to REU bank 0
        LDA #$00
        STA $DF09       ; No interrupts
        STA $DF0A       ; Both addresses count up
        
        LDA #<$0400     ; C64 source low byte
        STA $DF02
        LDA #>$0400     ; C64 source high byte
        STA $DF03
        
        LDA #$00        ; REU destination
        STA $DF04       ; Low byte
        STA $DF05       ; High byte
        STA $DF06       ; Bank 0
        
        LDA #<$0400     ; Length = 1024 bytes
        STA $DF07
        LDA #>$0400
        STA $DF08
        
        LDA #%10010000  ; C64→REU, immediate execute
        STA $DF01       ; Start transfer!

REU Transfer Types (Command Register $DF01)

Bits 1-0 Type Description
00 STASH C64 → REU
01 FETCH REU → C64
10 SWAP Exchange C64 ↔︎ REU
11 COMPARE Verify C64 = REU

GeoRAM Programming

GeoRAM uses simpler banked access (no DMA) through I/O space.

GeoRAM Architecture

  • Window: DE00−DEFF (256 bytes visible at a time)
  • Page Register: $DFFE (selects 256-byte page within block, 0-63)
  • Block Register: $DFFF (selects 16KB block, 0-31 for 512KB)
  • Capacity: 512KB standard, expandable to 4MB

GeoRAM Access Example

; Write byte to GeoRAM page 5, block 2
        LDA #$02        ; Select block 2
        STA $DFFF
        LDA #$05        ; Select page 5 within block
        STA $DFFE
        
        LDA #$42        ; Data to write
        STA $DE00       ; Write to first byte of window
        
; Read byte back
        LDA $DE00       ; Read from GeoRAM

GeoRAM vs REU

Feature REU GeoRAM
Speed ~1 MB/sec (DMA) ~125 KB/sec (CPU)
Access DMA transfers Banked 256-byte window
Complexity REC chip required Simple logic
Paddle/Mouse N/A N/A
Availability Original rare Clones available

SuperCPU Programming

The CMD SuperCPU uses a 65816 processor at 20 MHz.

Key Features

  • CPU: WDC 65816 at 20 MHz
  • RAM: 256 KB on-board; up to 16 MB with SuperRAM card
  • Addressing: Full 24-bit (16 MB) address space
  • Compatibility: Falls back to 1 MHz for I/O operations

SuperCPU Registers

Address Function
$D07A Disable SuperCPU (write $xx)
$D07B Enable SuperCPU (write $xx)
$D0BC SuperCPU detection (bit 7 = 0 if present)
$D0BE Speed register
$D0BF Bank configuration

Detecting SuperCPU

; Check if SuperCPU present
        LDA $D0BC
        BMI not_scpu    ; Bit 7 set = no SuperCPU
        ; SuperCPU detected!
not_scpu:

Speed Switching

; Switch to 1 MHz for timing-critical code
        STA $D07A       ; Disable turbo
        
        ; ... timing-critical code (FLI, loading, etc.) ...
        
        STA $D07B       ; Re-enable turbo

SuperCPU Compatibility Tips

  1. Avoid illegal opcodes - 65816 defines all opcodes differently
  2. Don’t assume address wrap - 65816 has 24-bit addressing
  3. Use 1 MHz mode for timing-dependent code (FLI, tape, disk)
  4. ROM shadowing uses most of the 256KB internal RAM

Modern Memory Expansion Hardware

Device Type Capacity Emulates
1541 Ultimate II+ Cartridge 16 MB REU, GeoRAM
Ultimate 64 Motherboard 16 MB REU, GeoRAM
RAD Expansion Unit RPi-based 16 MB REU, 4 MB GeoRAM REU, GeoRAM
Sidekick64 Cartridge N/A REU (up to 8 SIDs!)

Resources

  • Codebase64 REU Tutorial: https://codebase64.org/doku.php?id=base:reu_programming
  • C64 OS GeoRAM Guide: https://www.c64os.com/post/georamdigi
  • SuperCPU Compatibility: https://www.retro64.ca/code-supercpu-compatible/
  • REU Manual PDF: http://www.zimmers.net/anonftp/pub/cbm/manuals/peripherals/1764_Users_Guide.pdf

42. Ultimate64 & Ultimate II+ Hardware

The Ultimate series by Gideon Zweijtzer represents the pinnacle of modern C64 hardware expansion.

Product Evolution

Product Year Type Key Features
1541 Ultimate 2008 Cartridge Original; 1541 emulation, SD card
1541 Ultimate II 2012 Cartridge USB, MicroSD, improved features
Ultimate II+ 2016 Cartridge Ethernet, 3 USB ports, dual flash
Ultimate II+L 2022 Cartridge Updated components for availability
Ultimate 64 2019 Motherboard FPGA C64 replacement + U2+ built-in
Ultimate 64 Elite 2020 Motherboard Dual SID ZIF sockets, jumperless
Ultimate 64 Elite II 2024+ Motherboard 1080p HDMI, USB-C power, new FPGA

Ultimate II+ Features

Drive Emulation: - Two cycle-accurate 1541 drives - 1571 and 1581 emulation - D64, G64, D71, D81, T64, TAP support - Standalone mode (works without C64)

Storage: - USB 2.0 (sticks, hard drives, card readers) - MicroSD slot - FAT/FAT32/ISO9660 filesystems

Cartridge Emulation: - Final Cartridge III, Action Replay, Retro Replay - Super Snapshot V5, Epyx FastLoad - EasyFlash, Ocean/System 3 cartridges - Normal 8K/16K cartridges

Memory Expansion: - REU: 128KB to 16MB selectable - GeoRAM: up to 4MB - Combinable with cartridge emulation

Audio: - Built-in SID player - Ultimate Audio (8-voice PCM sampler) - MOD file playback - Dual SID support

Networking: - Integrated 100Mbit Ethernet - FTP/TFTP for disk image mounting - Network firmware updates

Ultimate 64 Features

Complete FPGA-based C64 motherboard replacement:

Video Output: - HDMI: 720p (576p50 PAL), zero latency - S-Video and composite via DIN - Scanline emulation, PAL/NTSC switchable - Elite II: 1080p FullHD support

SID Support: - Two physical SID sockets (Elite) - 6581 and 8580 support - Automatic voltage detection (9V/12V) - UltiSID FPGA emulation (no chips needed) - SID-TAP output for recording

Integrated Features: - Full Ultimate II+ functionality built-in - No IEC cable needed for drive emulation - 100Mbit Ethernet + WiFi module - 3x USB 2.0 ports - Active cartridge port

Programming & Development Tools

Tool Platform Description
ultimateii-dos-lib cc65 C library for Ultimate DOS/network functions
Ultimate Command Interface Multiple JSON-RPC control via USB/Ethernet
Ugo Go TCP control for mounting/running programs
UltimateRTC GEOS Sync GEOS clock from Ultimate RTC
C64 Stream OBS Plugin Stream video/audio from U64 to OBS

Ultimate DOS API

The Command Interface allows programmatic control:

// Example using ultimateii-dos-lib (cc65)
#include "ultimate_lib.h"

// Initialize Ultimate interface
uii_init();

// Get DOS status
uii_get_status();

// Load file from SD
uii_load_file("//SD/GAMES/GAME.PRG");

// Mount D64 image
uii_mount_disk("//SD/DISKS/DEMO.D64");

// Network functions
uii_tcp_connect("bbs.example.com", 23);

Key Resources

Resource URL
Official Store ultimate64.com
Documentation 1541u-documentation.readthedocs.io
Firmware Updates ultimate64.com (login required)
Facebook Group “1541 Ultimate”
ultimateii-dos-lib github.com/xlar54/ultimateii-dos-lib
1541U Documentation github.com/GideonZ/1541u-documentation

Comparison: U2+ vs Ultimate 64

Feature Ultimate II+ Ultimate 64
Type Cartridge add-on Full motherboard
Requires C64 Yes No (replaces it)
HDMI output No Yes
Real SID sockets No Yes (Elite: 2)
Built-in speaker Yes Yes
WiFi No Yes
Price range ~€130-150 ~€350-450

Tips & Best Practices

  1. Firmware updates: Check for updates regularly; fixes and features added often
  2. File organization: Use subdirectories on SD/USB for easier navigation
  3. REU size: 16MB REU enables advanced demos and GEOS performance
  4. Fast loading: Mount D64 + use built-in fastloader for best speed
  5. Dual SID: Configure second SID address ($D420, $D500, $DE00) in settings
  6. Network: Use Ethernet for reliable BBS/FTP access

⚡ C64 Chip Quick-Reference

Every main IC. ● = still manufactured   ● = NOS only   ● = modern replacement recommended

Chip Function Board Modern Replacement
6510 / 8500CPU · 1 MHzAll● J-CPU64 (1nt3r.net)
6569 / 8565 VIC-IIVideo, sprites, DMAPAL● VIC-II Kawari (FPGA)
6567 / 8562 VIC-IIVideo, sprites, DMANTSC● VIC-II Kawari (FPGA)

8580 SIDSound · 9V · clean filterC64C● SIDKick Pico / FPGASID / PDsid
6526 CIA ×2Keyboard, joystick, serial, timersAll● J-CIA64 (1nt3r.net)
906114 PLAMemory banking, chip selectAll● neatPLA (CPLD) — top failure
4164 / 4464 RAM64 KB DRAMAll● NOS 4164 · or MCL65 FPGA
2114 Colour RAM1 KB colour memoryAll● HM6514 / 2114L compatible

43. Chip Replacements (Modern Alternatives)

As original Commodore chips become scarce and expensive, the community has developed modern FPGA, ARM, and microcontroller-based replacements. This section covers drop-in alternatives for all major C64/C128 chips.

SID Replacements (6581/8580)

Replacement Technology Features Price Range Source
FPGASID FPGA (Altera MAX10) Dual SID stereo, highest accuracy, paddle support, configurable ~€75 fpgasid.de
ARMSID ARM Cortex M4 6581/8580 emulation, paddle/mouse support, firmware updates ~€40-50 nobomi.cz
ARM2SID ARM Cortex M4 Dual ARMSID on single board, stereo ~€70-80 nobomi.cz
SIDKick Teensy 4.1 reSID-based, dual SID, FM synthesis, MIDI, open source DIY ~€50 github.com/frntc/SIDKick
SIDKick Pico RP2040 (Pi Pico) Inexpensive reSID-based, dual SID, FM, open source DIY ~€15-25 github.com/frntc/SIDKick-pico
SwinSID Nano ATmega88PA Basic replacement, no paddle support ~€20-30 Various sellers
SwinSID Ultimate ATmega Improved SwinSID with paddle support ~€30-40 Limited availability
BackSID ARM Auto-detection, paddle support, firmware updates ~€45-55 backbit.io
reDIP SID iCE40 FPGA Open source hardware, stereo capable DIY github.com/daglem/reDIP-SID
KungFuSID STM32/GD32 Open source alternative to ARMSID DIY ~€15 github.com/Sgw32/KungFuSID

SID Replacement Notes: - FPGASID and ARMSID are considered closest to original SID sound - SIDKick Pico offers best value for DIY builders - All modern replacements support both 6581 and 8580 emulation modes - Paddle/mouse support varies - check before purchase if needed

CPU Replacements (6510/8500/8502)

Replacement Technology Features Compatibility Source
J-CPU64 FPGA Drop-in 6510/8500, illegal opcodes, no heat C64, SX-64, C64 clones 1nt3r.net, retro8bitshop.com
J-CPU128 FPGA 8502 replacement for C128, 1/2 MHz modes C128, C128D 1nt3r.net
MCL64 Teensy 4.1 6510 emulation, optional turbo mode C64 github.com/microcorelabs
MockA65xx FPGA Universal 6502/6510/8502 replacement Multiple systems Various

J-CPU64 Features: - 100% compatible including all illegal opcodes - Passes Wolfgang Lorenz test suite and VICE CPU tests - Works with all C64 motherboard revisions - Compatible with VIC-II Kawari, J-CIA, modern PLA replacements - No heat generation, lower power consumption

CIA Replacements (6526/8521)

Replacement Technology Features Compatibility Source
J-CIA64 FPGA Full 6526/8521 logic, switchable modes C64, C128, SX-64, 1570/1571 1nt3r.net, retro8bitshop.com

J-CIA64 Features: - FPGA-based replacement for MOS 6526 & 8521 - Complete logic implementation including all timing - Solder jumper to switch between 6526 (old) and 8521 (new) modes - Works in 1 MHz and 2 MHz modes (C128) - Tested with WiFi modems, Ultimate II+, SD2IEC, TapeCart, 1351 mouse - Price: ~€40

Note: Amiga 8520 chips can work as emergency CIA replacement but are NOT 100% compatible (timing differences in TOD registers).

VIC-II Replacements (6567/6569/8562/8565)

Replacement Technology Features Compatibility Source
VIC-II Kawari FPGA (Spartan-6) PAL/NTSC switchable, HDMI/DVI, 80-col mode, extra features Most breadbin boards accentual.com, videogameperfection.com
J-VIC FPGA In development by Jani Laatikainen TBD 1nt3r.net

VIC-II Kawari Features: - Supports 6567R56A, 6567R8, 6569R1, 6569R3 chip models - PAL/NTSC switching (software or hardware) - DVI output via micro-HDMI connector - 64KB additional video RAM - Extended features: 80-column text mode, hi-res modes, blitter - No VSP bug - Configurable color palette - Firmware updatable - Available in Mini and Large versions - Price: ~$80-120 USD

Kawari Compatibility: - Works best with breadbin boards (250407, 326298, 250425) - 250469 short boards have known issues with SuperPLA - May require extra socket for clearance

PLA Replacements (906114-01)

Replacement Technology Features Price Source
SuperPLA V4 CPLD High compatibility, low power ~€25 icomp.de
PLAnkton CPLD (XC9536XL) 20mA power, no heat, wide compatibility ~$15-20 Various
neatPLA CPLD Open source, based on dodgyPLA DIY github.com/1c3d1v3r/neatPLA
PLA20V8 2x GAL20V8B DIY-friendly, well documented DIY ~€5-10 freepascal.org/~daniel/c64pla
J-PLA CPLD By Jani Laatikainen ~€15-20 1nt3r.net
XCPLA CPLD Various sellers ~€15-25 Various

PLA Notes: - Original PLA (MOS 906114-01) is the #1 failure-prone chip in C64 - Runs extremely hot (~65°C), high power consumption (~120mA) - All modern replacements run cooler and use less power - 250469 short boards use different chip - standard PLA replacements don’t work - Compatible part numbers: 82S100, 93459PC, 7700, 8700, 906114-01, 251064-01

Clock Generator Replacements (8701)

Replacement Technology Features Source
CSG8701 Replacement Discrete PLL PAL or NTSC versions, replaces original PLL circuit icomp.de
Soigeneris MOS8701 Kit DIY replacement kit soigeneris.com
Various clones ICS525-01 based Open source designs github.com/desaster/c64-dodgypll

8701 Notes: - Used on C64 boards 250425 and later (not needed on earlier boards) - Generates dot clock and color carrier frequencies - Original chip uses delay-based timing (not PLL) - Modern replacements restore original PLL-based design - Separate PAL and NTSC versions typically required

Stereo SID Boards

Board Features SID Types Source
MixSID Integrated mixer, runtime control, paddle support Any combination henning-liebenau.de/mixsid, Tindie
StereoSID Open source, simple design Any combination github.com/tebl/C64-Stereo-SID
SID2SID Classic dual-SID board Same type only Various

8722 MMU Memory Management No replacement; relatively reliable

Commodore 8-Bit Buyer’s Guide Reference

The Commodore 8-Bit Buyer’s Guide maintained by Gregory Nacu at C64OS.com is the most comprehensive visual catalog of currently available hardware for Commodore 8-bit computers.

URL: https://c64os.com/buyersguide/

Guide Statistics

  • 450+ Products, Projects and Kits
  • 100+ Creators
  • 60+ Vendors
  • 12 Categories
  • Compatible with: PET, VIC-20, C64, C64c, SX-64, C16, Plus/4, C128, C128D

Product Categories

Category Description Key Products
Advanced New mainboards, FPGA solutions Ultimate 64/Elite, C64 Reloaded MK2, Turbo Chameleon 64, TheC64 Mini/Maxi, Cassiopei v2
Storage SD cards, flash carts, tape emulation SD2IEC variants, IDE64 v4.2, Kung Fu Flash, EasyFlash 3, Pi1541, uIEC/SD, Tapuino
Expansion Port expanders X-Pander 3, Mini X-Pander, Datel EX64, QuadPort IEC
Audio SID replacements, MIDI, stereo ARMSID, ARM2SID +FM, FPGASID, SwinSID, SIDFX, MixSID, CynthCART, DigiMAX, FM-YAM
Memory RAM expansions 512K/1MB/2MB/4MB GeoRAM, 1750 REU upgrades, VIC-20 expansions (Penultimate+, UltiMem)
ROMs Cartridge ROMs, KERNAL adapters EasyFlash 3, Versa64Cart, Kung Fu Flash, Switchless Kernal Switcher, Dela 87010
Network WiFi, Ethernet, modems 64NIC+, RR-Net MK3, WiModem variants, C64Net WiFi, Link232 WiFi
Video Converters, cables, upgrades RetroTINK 2X, Beam Racer, LumaFix64, S-Video adapters, SCART cables, RF replacements
Input Joysticks, mice, keyboards Competition Pro Retro, MicroMys, MouSTer, Nunchuk64, MechBoard64, 4-player adapters

Power Power supplies, protection Carlsen Electronics PSUs, C64PSU.com, voltage protectors, OVP 64
Component Cases, chips, adapters Sixtyclone PCB, new cases, PLA replacements, 8701 replacements, port adapters

Using the Buyer’s Guide

  1. Browse by Category - 12 categories organized thematically
  2. Feature Pages - Detailed product pages with photos, compatibility, pricing
  3. Summary Cards - Quick price/link info for products without full pages
  4. Vendor Links - Direct links to purchase
  5. Community Reviews - Comments and Q&A on feature pages

Related Resources

Resource URL Description
C64 OS c64os.com Modern OS for C64, requires mouse + mass storage
Afterlife Guide c64os.com/c64os/afterlifeguide User guide for returning C64 users
C64 OS Weblog c64os.com/weblog Technical deep dives, tutorials, product reviews
Video Guide c64os.com/c64os/videoguide Video demonstrations of C64 OS features

The Buyer’s Guide is actively maintained. Follow @gregnacu on X/Twitter with #8bitbuyer for updates.

Cartridge Development

A guide to creating cartridge-based software for the C64, from simple 8K ROMs to advanced bankswitched designs.

Cartridge Basics

The C64 expansion port allows ROM cartridges to be mapped into the CPU’s address space:

Type Size Address Range EXROM GAME
8K 8 KB $8000-$9FFF (ROML) 0 1
16K 16 KB $8000-$9FFF + A000−BFFF 0 0
Ultimax 8K/16K $8000 + E000−FFFF 1 0

Memory Mapping:

$8000-$9FFF  ROML (Low ROM, active when EXROM=0)
$A000-$BFFF  ROMH (High ROM, active when EXROM=0, GAME=0)
$E000-$FFFF  ROMH (Ultimax mode, active when GAME=0, EXROM=1)

Cartridge Header

All cartridges must start with a magic signature at $8000:

* = $8000

; Cartridge signature
    .word coldstart      ; Cold start vector
    .word warmstart      ; Warm start vector
    .byte $C3, $C2, $CD, $38, $30  ; "CBM80" magic

coldstart:
    ; Initialization code
    jmp main

warmstart:
    ; Warm start (RESTORE key)
    jmp main

CRT File Format

The CRT format is the standard for cartridge images:

Header Structure (64 bytes):

Offset Size Description
$0000 16 Signature “C64 CARTRIDGE”
$0010 4 Header length (big-endian, usually $40)
$0014 2 Version (usually $0100)
$0016 2 Hardware type (see below)
$0018 1 EXROM line state
$0019 1 GAME line state
$001A 6 Reserved
$0020 32 Cartridge name (null-padded)

Hardware Types:

ID Type Description
0 Normal Standard 8K/16K cartridge
1 Action Replay Freezer cartridge
3 Final Cartridge III Utility/freezer
5 Ocean Type 1 Bank-switched (128K-512K)
7 Fun Play Bank-switched
19 Magic Desk Simple bank-switching
32 EasyFlash 1MB flash + 256 bytes RAM
60 GMod2 Bank-switched + EEPROM save

CHIP Packets (follow header):

Offset Size Description
$0000 4 Signature “CHIP”
$0004 4 Total packet length
$0008 2 Chip type (0=ROM, 1=RAM, 2=Flash)
$000A 2 Bank number
$000C 2 Load address
$000E 2 ROM image size
$0010 … ROM data

Bank Switching Cartridges

For programs larger than 16K, bank switching is required.

Magic Desk (Simple Banking)

Up to 1MB (128 banks of 8K at $8000):

; Select bank (0-127)
lda #bank_number
sta $DE00

; Disable cartridge (access RAM underneath)
lda #$80
sta $DE00

Ocean Type 1 (Classic Games)

128K-512K, used by many commercial games:

; Banks 0-15: $8000-$9FFF
; Banks 16-31: $A000-$BFFF (some variants)

; Select bank
lda #bank_number
sta $DE00

Ocean Type 1 Games: Shadow of the Beast, Terminator 2, Navy Seals, RoboCop 2

EasyFlash (Modern Standard)

1MB flash ROM + 256 bytes RAM, most versatile modern cartridge:

; EasyFlash registers
EASYFLASH_BANK   = $DE00  ; Bank select (0-63)
EASYFLASH_CONTROL = $DE02  ; Control register

; Bank layout: 64 banks x 16K each
; Each bank: 8K ROML ($8000) + 8K ROMH ($A000 or $E000)

; Select bank
lda #bank_number
sta EASYFLASH_BANK

; Control register bits:
; Bit 0: GAME line
; Bit 1: EXROM line  
; Bit 2: Mode (0=Ultimax at $E000, 1=16K at $A000)
; Bit 7: LED

; Enable 16K mode
lda #%00000100
sta EASYFLASH_CONTROL

; Disable cartridge (jump to RAM)
lda #%00000111
sta EASYFLASH_CONTROL

EasyFlash RAM: 256 bytes at DF00−DFFF (directly writable)

GMod2 (With Save Support)

Bank-switched + M93C86 EEPROM for game saves:

; Select bank (same as Ocean)
lda #bank_number
sta $DE00

; EEPROM access via $DE00 bits 6-7
; (Complex protocol - see documentation)

Creating CRT Files

Using cartconv (VICE tool):

# 8K cartridge
cartconv -t normal -i game.bin -o game.crt

# 16K cartridge
cartconv -t normal -i game.bin -o game.crt -l 0x4000

# EasyFlash
cartconv -t easy -i game.bin -o game.crt

# Ocean Type 1
cartconv -t ocean -i game.bin -o game.crt

Using prg2crt (various implementations):

# Convert PRG to CRT
prg2crt -i game.prg -o game.crt -t EasyFlash

EasyFlash Development

EasySDK Structure:

project/
├── src/
│   ├── startup.asm    # EasyFlash startup code
│   ├── main.asm       # Main program
│   └── data.asm       # Bank data
├── eapi.asm           # EasyFlash API
└── Makefile

EasyFlash Startup Code:

* = $8000
    ; ROML bank 0 - startup

    .word coldstart
    .word warmstart
    .byte $C3, $C2, $CD, $38, $30  ; CBM80

coldstart:
    sei
    ldx #$ff
    txs
    cld
    
    ; Set up EasyFlash
    lda #$87        ; LED on, 16K mode, cart active
    sta $DE02
    
    ; Copy decrunch code to RAM
    ; ... 
    
    jmp main

warmstart:
    jmp coldstart

Programming EasyFlash (via EasyProg): 1. Create CRT image with cartconv or EasyCrtMaker 2. Load EasyProg on C64 3. Select CRT file from disk/SD 4. Flash to cartridge

Choosing a Cartridge Type

Use Case Recommended Type Max Size
Simple utility Normal 8K/16K 16 KB
Game without saves Magic Desk 1 MB
Game with saves GMod2 512 KB
Development/multi-game EasyFlash 1 MB
Maximum compatibility Ocean Type 1 512 KB

Hardware for Development

Device Features
EasyFlash 3 In-system programmable, USB
Kung Fu Flash SD card, CRT emulation
1541 Ultimate II+ CRT loading, freezer
TeensyROM USB, SD, many cart types
BackBit Cartridge + disk emulation

Resources

Resource URL Description
CRT Format Spec vice-emu.sourceforge.io Official format docs
EasyFlash skoe.de/easyflash Original project
World of Jani blog.worldofjani.com Cartridge tutorials
Codebase64 codebase64.org Banking examples
cartconv Included with VICE CRT creation tool

Peripherals Guide

Programming guide for C64 input devices: joysticks, paddles, mice, light pens, and MIDI interfaces.

Control Port Pinout

The C64 has two 9-pin DE-9 control ports:

    1 2 3 4 5
     6 7 8 9
Pin Function CIA Register
1 Joystick Up / POT Y Button DC00/DC01 bit 0
2 Joystick Down / Paddle Fire DC00/DC01 bit 1
3 Joystick Left / Paddle 2 Fire DC00/DC01 bit 2
4 Joystick Right DC00/DC01 bit 3
5 POT Y (Paddle/Mouse Y) SID D419/D41A
6 Joystick Fire / Light Pen DC00/DC01 bit 4
7 +5V Power
8 GND Ground
9 POT X (Paddle/Mouse X) SID D419/D41A

Joystick Programming

Reading Joystick:

; Port 2 (most games use this)
JOY2 = $DC00

; Port 1 (conflicts with keyboard)
JOY1 = $DC01

read_joystick:
    lda JOY2
    eor #$FF        ; Invert (active low)
    sta joy_state
    
    ; Check individual directions
    and #%00000001  ; Up
    beq .not_up
    ; Handle up
.not_up:

    lda joy_state
    and #%00000010  ; Down
    beq .not_down
    ; Handle down
.not_down:

    lda joy_state
    and #%00000100  ; Left
    beq .not_left
    ; Handle left
.not_left:

    lda joy_state
    and #%00001000  ; Right
    beq .not_right
    ; Handle right
.not_right:

    lda joy_state
    and #%00010000  ; Fire
    beq .not_fire
    ; Handle fire
.not_fire:
    rts

joy_state: .byte 0

Joystick Direction Table:

; Combined direction values (after EOR #$FF)
; $00 = None
; $01 = Up
; $02 = Down
; $04 = Left
; $05 = Up+Left
; $06 = Down+Left
; $08 = Right
; $09 = Up+Right
; $0A = Down+Right
; $10 = Fire
; $11 = Fire+Up (etc.)

BASIC Joystick Reading:

10 J=PEEK(56320)
20 IF (J AND 1)=0 THEN PRINT "UP"
30 IF (J AND 2)=0 THEN PRINT "DOWN"
40 IF (J AND 4)=0 THEN PRINT "LEFT"
50 IF (J AND 8)=0 THEN PRINT "RIGHT"
60 IF (J AND 16)=0 THEN PRINT "FIRE"
70 GOTO 10

Paddle Programming

Paddles use SID’s analog-to-digital converters.

Paddle Registers:

Address Function
$D419 POT X (Paddle 1 or 3)
$D41A POT Y (Paddle 2 or 4)

Reading Paddles:

; SID POT registers
POTX = $D419
POTY = $D41A

; CIA port selection
CIA1_PRA = $DC00

read_paddles:
    ; Select Port 1 paddles
    lda CIA1_PRA
    and #%00111111
    ora #%01000000    ; Set bit 6 for Port 1
    sta CIA1_PRA
    
    ; Wait for SID to sample (~512 µs)
    ldx #$80
-   dex
    bne -
    
    ; Read paddle values (0-255)
    lda POTX
    sta paddle1_x
    lda POTY
    sta paddle1_y
    
    ; Select Port 2 paddles
    lda CIA1_PRA
    and #%00111111
    ora #%10000000    ; Set bit 7 for Port 2
    sta CIA1_PRA
    
    ; Wait again
    ldx #$80
-   dex
    bne -
    
    lda POTX
    sta paddle2_x
    lda POTY
    sta paddle2_y
    rts

paddle1_x: .byte 0
paddle1_y: .byte 0
paddle2_x: .byte 0
paddle2_y: .byte 0

Paddle Buttons:

; Paddle fire buttons use joystick lines
; Paddle 1 fire: JOY bit 2 (left)
; Paddle 2 fire: JOY bit 3 (right)

Mouse Programming (1351)

The Commodore 1351 mouse transmits position via SID POT registers.

1351 Operating Modes: - Proportional mode (default): True mouse movement - Joystick mode (hold right button at power-on): Emulates joystick

Reading 1351 Mouse:

; The 1351 sends position modulo 64 to SID POT registers
; Movement is calculated as change in POT values

POTX = $D419
POTY = $D41A

; Variables
old_pot_x:   .byte 0
old_pot_y:   .byte 0
mouse_x:     .word 0  ; 16-bit position
mouse_y:     .word 0

read_mouse:
    ; Read current POT values
    lda POTX
    and #%01111110    ; Bits 1-6 are significant
    sta new_pot_x
    
    lda POTY
    and #%01111110
    sta new_pot_y
    
    ; Calculate X delta
    lda new_pot_x
    sec
    sbc old_pot_x
    
    ; Handle wraparound (values are 0-126)
    cmp #64
    bcc .x_pos
    ora #%11000000    ; Sign extend negative
.x_pos:
    clc
    adc mouse_x
    sta mouse_x
    bcc +
    inc mouse_x+1
+
    
    ; Calculate Y delta (similar)
    lda new_pot_y
    sec
    sbc old_pot_y
    cmp #64
    bcc .y_pos
    ora #%11000000
.y_pos:
    clc
    adc mouse_y
    sta mouse_y
    bcc +
    inc mouse_y+1
+
    
    ; Save current as old
    lda new_pot_x
    sta old_pot_x
    lda new_pot_y
    sta old_pot_y
    
    ; Read buttons
    ; Left button: Fire (bit 4)
    ; Right button: Up (bit 0)
    lda $DC01         ; Port 1
    eor #$FF
    sta mouse_buttons
    rts

new_pot_x:     .byte 0
new_pot_y:     .byte 0
mouse_buttons: .byte 0

Important Notes: - Call read_mouse from IRQ handler before keyboard scan - Keyboard scan switches POT lines temporarily - Wait >1.6ms after keyboard scan before reading POTs

Light Pen Programming

The VIC-II has built-in light pen support.

Light Pen Registers:

Address Function
$D013 Light Pen X (bits 0-7, actual = value × 2)
$D014 Light Pen Y
$D019 bit 3 Light pen IRQ flag

Reading Light Pen:

; VIC-II captures light pen position automatically
; when signal detected on control port pin 6

LPENX = $D013
LPENY = $D014

read_lightpen:
    lda LPENX
    asl             ; Multiply by 2 for actual X
    sta lpen_x
    
    lda LPENY
    sta lpen_y
    
    ; Check if triggered (optional IRQ)
    lda $D019
    and #%00001000
    beq .no_trigger
    
    ; Light pen triggered
    lda #%00001000
    sta $D019       ; Acknowledge
    
.no_trigger:
    rts

lpen_x: .byte 0
lpen_y: .byte 0

Light Pen Limitations: - X resolution is halved (only 160 positions) - Several pixels of jitter - Only works with CRT displays (not LCD) - VIC-II latches on negative edge of LP signal

MIDI Programming

MIDI interfaces use the MC6850 ACIA chip at various I/O addresses.

Common MIDI Interface Addresses:

Interface Control Status TX RX
Passport/Syntech $DE00 $DE00 $DE01 $DE01
Sequential $DE00 $DE00 $DE01 $DE01
Datel/Siel $DE04 $DE06 $DE05 $DE07
Kerberos $DE00 $DE02 $DE01 $DE03
Namesoft $DE00 $DE02 $DE01 $DE03

Basic MIDI Receive:

; Generic MIDI receive (Datel-style addresses)
MIDI_STATUS  = $DE06
MIDI_DATA    = $DE07

; Initialize MIDI
midi_init:
    lda #$03        ; Master reset
    sta $DE04
    lda #$15        ; 8N1, divide by 16
    sta $DE04
    rts

; Receive MIDI byte (polling)
midi_receive:
    lda MIDI_STATUS
    and #$01        ; Check RDRF (receive data register full)
    beq midi_receive
    lda MIDI_DATA
    rts

; Send MIDI byte
midi_send:
    pha
-   lda MIDI_STATUS
    and #$02        ; Check TDRE (transmit data register empty)
    beq -
    pla
    sta $DE05       ; Transmit register
    rts

Send MIDI Note:

; Send Note On
; A = channel (0-15)
; X = note (0-127)
; Y = velocity (0-127)

send_note_on:
    ora #$90        ; Note On status
    jsr midi_send
    txa
    jsr midi_send
    tya
    jsr midi_send
    rts

; Send Note Off
send_note_off:
    ora #$80        ; Note Off status
    jsr midi_send
    txa
    jsr midi_send
    lda #$00        ; Velocity 0
    jsr midi_send
    rts

MIDI Software

Software Type MIDI Support
Cynthcart Synthesizer Datel, Passport, Sequential, Kerberos
MSSIAH Sequencer Built-in interface
SID-Wizard Tracker Multiple interfaces
Station 64 Synthesizer Various
Prophet 64 Sequencer Datel

Modern USB Solutions

Device Features
TeensyROM USB MIDI host, plays SID via MIDI
Kerberos MC6850-based, emulates classics
MSSIAH Savyour USB keyboard/mouse to joystick port
Mouster/TIKUS USB mouse to 1351 emulation

Resources

Resource URL Description
Control Port c64-wiki.com/wiki/Control_Port Pin documentation
1351 Manual project64.c64.org Official docs
C64 MIDI c64midi.com Interface comparison
Cynthcart github.com/omarberg/cynthcartC64 MIDI synth source

67. REU Programming Guide

The RAM Expansion Unit (REU) provides additional RAM via DMA transfers, enabling graphics buffering, RAM disks, and expanded data storage.

REU Models

Model RAM Notes
1700 128 KB C128-oriented, upgradeable
1764 256 KB C64-specific, includes 2.5A PSU
1750 512 KB C128-oriented, most desirable
Ultimate II+ 16 MB Modern emulated REU
1750 Clone 512 KB-2 MB Third-party, lower power

How It Works

The REU cannot be directly addressed by the C64’s 16-bit address space. Instead: 1. Program the REC (RAM Expansion Controller) registers at DF00−DF0A 2. Trigger a DMA transfer (STASH, FETCH, SWAP, or VERIFY) 3. DMA runs at ~1 MB/sec, halting the CPU during transfer

REC Register Map (DF00−DF0A)

Address Name Description
$DF00 Status Read-only status (bit 4: size indicator)
$DF01 Command Transfer type and execution control
DF02−DF03 C64 Base Start address in C64 memory (lo/hi)
DF04−DF05 REU Base Start address in REU memory (lo/hi)
$DF06 REU Bank Bank number (0-7 for 512KB)
DF07−DF08 Length Transfer length (lo/hi)
$DF09 IRQ Mask Interrupt control
$DF0A Control Address fix options

Command Register ($DF01) Bits

Bit Name Description
7 Execute 1 = Start DMA immediately
6 Reserved Always 0
5 Autoload 1 = Restore addresses after transfer
4 FF00 0 = Execute on $FF00 write, 1 = Immediate
3-2 Reserved Always 0
1-0 Type 00=STASH, 01=FETCH, 10=SWAP, 11=VERIFY

Transfer Types

Type Bits Description
STASH %00 C64 → REU (store to expansion)
FETCH %01 REU → C64 (retrieve from expansion)
SWAP %10 Exchange C64 ↔︎ REU contents
VERIFY %11 Compare C64 with REU (sets status)

Basic REU Transfer Routine

; REU Register definitions
REU_STATUS  = $DF00
REU_COMMAND = $DF01
REU_C64BASE = $DF02
REU_REUBASE = $DF04
REU_REUBANK = $DF06
REU_LENGTH  = $DF07
REU_IRQMASK = $DF09
REU_CONTROL = $DF0A

; STASH: Copy $2000 bytes from C64 $4000 to REU bank 0, address $0000
stash_screen:
    lda #<$4000         ; C64 source low
    sta REU_C64BASE
    lda #>$4000         ; C64 source high
    sta REU_C64BASE+1
    
    lda #<$0000         ; REU dest low
    sta REU_REUBASE
    lda #>$0000         ; REU dest high
    sta REU_REUBASE+1
    
    lda #0              ; Bank 0
    sta REU_REUBANK
    
    lda #<$2000         ; Length low
    sta REU_LENGTH
    lda #>$2000         ; Length high
    sta REU_LENGTH+1
    
    lda #%10010000      ; Execute + Autoload + STASH
    sta REU_COMMAND     ; DMA starts NOW
    rts                 ; Returns after DMA complete

; FETCH: Copy from REU back to C64
fetch_screen:
    ; ... setup same addresses ...
    lda #%10010001      ; Execute + Autoload + FETCH
    sta REU_COMMAND
    rts

Detecting REU Presence and Size

detect_reu:
    ; Try writing/reading $DF02-$DF08
    ldx #7
    lda #$55
detect_loop:
    sta $df02,x
    dex
    bpl detect_loop
    
    ldx #7
check_loop:
    lda $df02,x
    cmp #$55
    bne no_reu          ; Values didn't stick = no REU
    dex
    bpl check_loop
    
    ; REU detected! Check size via $DF00 bit 4
    lda REU_STATUS
    and #$10
    beq reu_128_256     ; Bit 4 clear = 1700 or 1764
    ; Bit 4 set = 1750 (512KB+)
    
    ; For exact size, try writing to higher banks
    ; and reading back
    rts
    
no_reu:
    ; No REU present
    rts

Common REU Uses

Double Buffering:

; Swap visible screen with REU buffer
    lda #%10010010      ; Execute + Autoload + SWAP
    sta REU_COMMAND

Fast Memory Copy (C64 RAM to C64 RAM):

; Use REU as intermediary for fast copy
; 1. STASH source to REU
; 2. Change C64 base address
; 3. FETCH to destination

RAM Disk: Store entire disk contents in REU for instant access (RAMDOS, GEOS).

REU with BASIC (C128 Only)

REM STASH 8KB from $4000 to REU bank 0
STASH 8192, $4000, 0, 0

REM FETCH it back
FETCH 8192, $4000, 0, 0

REM SWAP contents
SWAP 8192, $4000, 0, 0

Resources

Resource URL
Codebase64 REU codebase64.org/doku.php?id=base:reu_read_and_write
C64 Wiki REU c64-wiki.com/wiki/REU
Commodore64.se REU commodore64.se/wiki/index.php/REU_Programming
Inside Commodore DOS pagetable.com/docs/Inside%20Commodore%20DOS.pdf

34. Tape Preservation Tools

Essential tools and hardware for archiving, converting, and working with C64 cassette tape data.

Software Tools

Tool Description Link
TAPClean Premier tape analysis/repair tool - checks, repairs, remasters TAP/DMP files; supports 100+ loader formats https://sourceforge.net/projects/tapclean/
TAPClean Front End GUI for TAPClean with hex monitor, disassembler, BASIC untokenizer, graphical TAP analysis https://www.luigidifraia.com/c64/
WAV-PRG Converts between WAV audio and PRG/TAP; supports custom turbo loaders via plugins https://wav-prg.sourceforge.io/
Audiotap Converts TAP to/from WAV; can record/play directly from soundcard https://wav-prg.sourceforge.io/
c64tapedecode Command-line toolset: wav2tap, tap2wav, c64tapedecode, taphist https://github.com/lunderhage/c64tapedecode
TAPMoni TAP visualization tool - converts bytes to graphical pulses; C2N head alignment https://www.luigidifraia.com/c64/
CBM BASIC Lister Generate readable BASIC listings from PRG files (Windows/Linux GTK) https://www.luigidifraia.com/c64/

Preservation Hardware

Device Description Link
DC2N5-LC Standalone tape dumping device (ARM-based); dumps PET/C64/VIC-20/C16/Spectrum/BBC/MSX tapes; playback support https://www.luigidifraia.com/c64/dc2n/
DC2N v2 Original SD card version; makes DMP preservation files https://www.luigidifraia.com/c64/dc2n/
Tapuino Arduino-based Datasette emulator (~$20); plays TAP files from SD card; recording support https://github.com/sweetlilmre/tapuino
Tapuino Reloaded Enhanced Tapuino with 128x64 LCD, SMD PCB, multi-machine support (C16/C64/VIC-20) Various shops
TrueTape64 DIY PC-to-Datasette adapter using microcontroller https://github.com/francescovannini/truetape64
Tape Port Monitor Displays tape signal on TFT; intercepts filenames; debugging tool https://www.luigidifraia.com/hardware/

File Formats

Format Description Usage
TAP Standard tape image format; stores pulse durations; v0/v1/v2 versions Emulators, Tapuino, preservation
DMP High-resolution preservation format from DC2N; interrupt-driven capture Archives, conversion to TAP
T64 Container format for extracted programs; higher abstraction than TAP Emulators (less precise)
WAV Audio recording of tape; can be converted to TAP Soundcard capture

Workflow: Tape → Digital

  1. Capture: Use DC2N (standalone) or Audiotap (soundcard) to create DMP/WAV
  2. Convert: dc2nconv (DMP→TAP) or wav2tap (WAV→TAP)
  3. Analyze: TAPClean to check integrity, identify loader types
  4. Repair: TAPClean FE for manual fixes, hex editing
  5. Test: Load in VICE or real hardware via Tapuino/DC2N

Resources

  • C64 Tapes Database: http://tapes.c64.no/ - Massive tape preservation archive
  • Archiving Guide: https://www.pagetable.com/?p=1002 - Detailed tape archiving tutorial
  • Tape Loaders List: http://c64tapes.org/ - Documentation of loader formats

Disk Format Deep Dive

Understanding C64 disk formats is essential for preservation, development, and working with copy-protected software.

Format Hierarchy

Level Description Examples
Physical Media Actual 5.25” floppy disk Magnetic coating, tracks, sectors
Media Encoding How bits are represented GCR (Group Code Recording)
Sector Format Logical data organization Headers, data blocks, checksums
File System Directory and file structure CBM DOS, BAM, directory entries

D64 Format (Standard Disk Image)

The most common format, representing decoded sector data from a 1541 disk.

Track/Sector Layout:

Tracks Sectors/Track Speed Zone Bytes
1-17 21 3 (fastest) 7,820
18-24 19 2 7,170
25-30 18 1 6,300
31-35 17 0 (slowest) 6,020
Total 683 sectors 174,848 bytes

File Sizes: - 35 tracks, no errors: 174,848 bytes - 35 tracks, with errors: 175,531 bytes - 40 tracks, no errors: 196,608 bytes - 40 tracks, with errors: 197,376 bytes

Track 18 (Directory Track): - Sector 0: BAM (Block Availability Map) + disk name/ID - Sectors 1-18: Directory entries (8 per sector, max 144 files)

BAM Structure (Track 18, Sector 0):

$00-01: Track/sector of first directory sector (18/1)
$02:    DOS version ('A')
$03:    Unused ($00)
$04-8F: BAM entries (4 bytes per track)
$90-9F: Disk name (16 chars, padded with $A0)
$A0-A1: $A0 padding
$A2-A3: Disk ID (2 chars)
$A4:    $A0
$A5-A6: DOS type ('2A')

D64 Limitations: - No sector header information (ID bytes, checksums) - Cannot represent non-standard GCR or copy protection - No half-track or speed zone data

G64 Format (Raw GCR Image)

Stores raw GCR-encoded data exactly as read from the disk head.

Header Structure:

$0000-0007: Signature "GCR-1541"
$0008:      Version byte ($00)
$0009:      Number of tracks (usually $54 = 84 for half-tracks)
$000A-000B: Maximum track size (little-endian)
$000C-015B: Track data offsets (84 entries × 4 bytes)
$015C-02AB: Speed zone offsets (84 entries × 4 bytes)

Why 84 Tracks? G64 stores both full tracks (1-42) and half-tracks, allowing precise representation of protection schemes that use half-track data.

GCR Sector Structure:

1. SYNC mark (~10+ $FF bytes)
2. Header block (10 bytes GCR = 8 bytes decoded):
   - Header ID ($08)
   - Checksum
   - Sector number
   - Track number
   - Disk ID (2 bytes)
   - Padding ($0F, $0F)
3. Header gap (~9 bytes)
4. SYNC mark
5. Data block (325 bytes GCR = 260 bytes decoded):
   - Data ID ($07)
   - 256 bytes sector data
   - Checksum
   - Padding ($00, $00)
6. Tail gap (variable)

G64 Advantages: - Preserves copy protection schemes - Stores non-standard GCR data - Supports long tracks, custom densities - Half-track support

GCR Encoding

GCR (Group Code Recording) converts 4-bit nybbles to 5-bit patterns, ensuring clock synchronization.

GCR Conversion Table:

Nybble GCR Nybble GCR
$0 01010 $8 01001
$1 01011 $9 11001
$2 10010 $A 11010
$3 10011 $B 11011
$4 01110 $C 01101
$5 01111 $D 11101
$6 10110 $E 11110
$7 10111 $F 10101

GCR Rules: - Never more than 2 consecutive 0-bits - Never more than 8 consecutive 1-bits (SYNC uses 10+) - Invalid GCR patterns used for copy protection

NIB Format

Raw nibble data from disk imaging tools like nibtools. Even lower-level than G64.

Tools for NIB: - nibtools - Disk imaging with parallel cable - ZoomFloppy - USB-based disk transfer - Kryoflux - Flux-level disk imaging

Copy Protection Schemes

Scheme Era Techniques Companies
Read Errors 1983-85 Intentional sector errors Early software
Extra Tracks 1984+ Tracks 36-40 data Firebird
Half Tracks 1985+ Data between standard tracks Big Five, System 3
Fat/Wide Tracks 1985+ Overlapping track data Early EA, Activision
Vorpal 1985+ Custom GCR format, fast loader Epyx
RapidLok 1986+ Track 36 key, sync counting Various
V-MAX! 1987+ Slowed motor, non-standard GCR Access, Cinemaware

Vorpal Protection: - Custom GCR encoding (not standard CBM format) - Fast loading (~10x normal speed) - Used by Epyx (California Games, etc.) - Multiple versions with different schemes

RapidLok Protection: - Key encoded on track 36 - Sync length checking - Time-bomb hooks in game code - 6 major versions

V-MAX! Protection: - Motor speed manipulation - Non-standard GCR patterns - Very fast loading - 7+ variations

Preservation Tools

Tool Purpose Link
nibtools Disk imaging to G64/D64 github.com/OpenCBM/nibtools
ZoomFloppy USB disk transfer store.go4retro.com
Kryoflux Flux-level preservation kryoflux.com
DirMaster D64/G64 editing style64.org
c1541 Command-line D64 tools Part of VICE

Resources

Resource URL Description
Peter Schepers Formats ist.uwaterloo.ca/~schepers/formats.html Definitive format documentation
C64 Preservation Project c64preservation.com Protection scheme analysis
Visualizing 1541 Disks pagetable.com/?p=1070 Visual GCR explanation
VICE File Formats vice-emu.sourceforge.io/vice_17.html Emulator format documentation

Serial Bus & IEC Protocol

Understanding the C64’s serial bus is essential for disk drive programming, fastloader development, and hardware projects.

Bus Overview

The C64 serial bus (commonly called “IEC bus”) is a simplified version of the IEEE-488 parallel bus used on PET computers.

Physical Connector (6-pin DIN 45322):

Pin Name Function
1 SRQ Service Request (active low)
2 GND Ground
3 ATN Attention (active low)
4 CLK Clock (active low)
5 DATA Data (active low)
6 RESET System Reset

Signal Characteristics: - All lines are active-low (LOW = asserted) - Open collector/drain outputs - Any device can pull a line low - Lines float high when released (~5V)

Protocol Layers

┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│ KERNAL API (LOAD, SAVE, OPEN, etc.) │  Layer 4: Application
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│ File Protocol (channels, commands)  │  Layer 3: File System
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Byte Transfer (serial bit-banging)  │  Layer 2: Data Link
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Electrical (CLK, DATA, ATN lines)   │  Layer 1: Physical
└─────────────────────────────────────┘

Standard Byte Transfer

The C64 sends/receives one bit at a time using the CLK and DATA lines.

Talker sends byte to Listener:

CLK  ─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─
      └─┘ └─┘ └─┘ └─┘ └─┘ └─┘ └─┘ └─┘
DATA ─X───X───X───X───X───X───X───X────
       b0  b1  b2  b3  b4  b5  b6  b7

Timing (Standard Serial): - Bit setup time: 60-70 µs - Clock pulse: 20 µs minimum - Total: ~80 µs per bit - Result: ~400 bytes/second

EOI (End Or Identify) Handshake

When sending the last byte, the talker signals EOI:

  1. Talker holds CLK low, releases DATA
  2. Talker waits >200 µs (instead of normal ~60 µs)
  3. Listener detects timeout, acknowledges by pulling DATA low briefly
  4. Transfer continues normally

ATN (Attention) Commands

When ATN is asserted, all devices listen for commands:

Command Code Description
LISTEN $20-$3E Device $00-$1E becomes listener
UNLISTEN $3F All devices stop listening
TALK $40-$5E Device $00-$1E becomes talker
UNTALK $5F Talker stops talking
OPEN $60-$6F Open channel 0-15
CLOSE E0−EF Close channel 0-15
DATA F0−FF Select channel 0-15 for data

KERNAL IEC Routines

Routine Address Function
LISTEN $FFB1 Send LISTEN command
TALK $FFB4 Send TALK command
SECOND $FF93 Send secondary address
TKSA $FF96 Send secondary after TALK
CIOUT $FFA8 Send byte to serial bus
ACPTR $FFA5 Receive byte from serial bus
UNTLK $FFAB Send UNTALK
UNLSN $FFAE Send UNLISTEN

Low-Level IEC Programming

CIA #1 ($DD00) Serial Port Bits:

Bit Name Function
3 ATN OUT ATN line output (directly active)
4 CLK OUT Clock line output (directly active)
5 DATA OUT Data line output (directly active)
6 CLK IN Clock line input
7 DATA IN Data line input

Reading the Bus:

    lda $dd00
    and #%11000000    ; Mask CLK IN and DATA IN
    ; Bit 6 = CLK, Bit 7 = DATA

Setting Lines:

    lda $dd00
    ora #%00100000    ; Pull DATA low
    sta $dd00
    
    lda $dd00
    and #%11011111    ; Release DATA
    sta $dd00

JiffyDOS Protocol

JiffyDOS replaces the slow serial protocol with a faster byte-bang method.

Key Differences: - Uses both CLK and DATA for 2-bit parallel transfer - Sends nibbles (4 bits) at a time - Requires timing precision (~20 µs windows) - Auto-detects JiffyDOS-capable devices

Speed Comparison:

Protocol C64 + 1541 C64 + SD2IEC
Standard Serial ~400 B/s ~650 B/s
JiffyDOS ~2,400 B/s ~8,600 B/s
Theoretical Max ~25,000 B/s ~25,000 B/s

JiffyDOS Detection: During ATN command, before the last bit: 1. Controller releases DATA, delays releasing CLK 2. If device pulls DATA within ~400 µs, it’s JiffyDOS-capable 3. Both sides switch to fast protocol

Building a Custom Fastloader

Basic Turbo Protocol Concept:

; Simple 2-bit parallel receive (concept)
turbo_receive:
    ldx #$00        ; Clear received byte
    ldy #4          ; 4 iterations (2 bits each)
    
.loop:
    ; Wait for clock edge
-   lda $dd00
    bpl -           ; Wait for CLK high
    
    ; Read 2 bits from CLK and DATA
    asl             ; Move DATA IN to carry
    rol             ; Rotate into X
    asl $dd00       ; Move CLK IN to carry  
    rol             ; Rotate into X
    
    dey
    bne .loop
    
    txa             ; Result in A
    rts

Drive-Side Programming

The 1541 has its own 6502 CPU. Serial port is on VIA #1 ($1800):

Register Address Function
VIA1 PB $1800 Port B data (serial lines)
VIA1 PA $1801 Port A data (head step)
VIA1 DDRB $1802 Port B direction
VIA1 DDRA $1803 Port A direction

1541 Serial Port Bits ($1800):

Bit Function
0 DATA IN
1 DATA OUT
2 CLK IN
3 CLK OUT
4 ATN ACK
7 ATN IN

Fastloader Types

Loader Method Speed Compatible
Epyx FastLoad Cartridge + drive RAM 5x Most games
JiffyDOS KERNAL + DOS ROM replace 6-10x 99%+
Final Cartridge III Cartridge 5x Most software
Vorpal Custom GCR format 10x+ Specific titles
IRQ Loader Custom, per-title 10x+ Demo/game specific

Resources

Resource URL Description
Michael Steil’s IEC Guide pagetable.com/?p=1135 Definitive protocol docs
CBM Bus Documentation github.com/mist64/cbmbus_doc Complete bus family
sd2iec Source github.com/rkrajnc/sd2iec Fastloader implementations
JiffyDOS Info jiffydos.com Official site
1541 ROM Disassembly ftp.zimmers.net Annotated source

69. 1541 Drive Programming

The 1541 disk drive is a computer in its own right, with a 6502 CPU, 2KB RAM, and 16KB ROM.

1541 Hardware Overview

Component Description
CPU MOS 6502 @ 1 MHz
RAM 2 KB ($0000-07FF)||ROM|16KB(C000-$FFFF) - CBM DOS
VIA 1 $1800-$180F - Serial bus interface
VIA 2 $1C00-$1C0F - Drive motor/head control
Buffers 5 × 256-byte buffers in RAM

1541 Memory Map

Address Description
$0000-$00FF Zero page (drive variables)
$0100-$01FF CPU Stack
$0200-$02FF Command buffer (input from C64)
$0300-$03FF Buffer #0
$0400-$04FF Buffer #1
$0500-$05FF Buffer #2
$0600-$06FF Buffer #3
$0700-$07FF Buffer #4 / BAM buffer
$1800-$180F VIA #1 (serial bus)
$1C00-$1C0F VIA #2 (drive mechanics)
C000−FFFF DOS ROM

Direct Access Commands

Command Syntax Description
M-R M-R CHR(lo)CHR(hi) CHR$(len) | Read drive memory | | M-W | `M-W` CHR$(lo) CHR(hi)CHR(len) data Write drive memory
M-E M-E CHR(lo)CHR(hi) Execute code at address
B-R B-R: ch dr tr se Read block to buffer
B-W B-W: ch dr tr se Write buffer to block
B-E B-E: ch dr tr se Execute code from disk
U1 U1: ch dr tr se Read block (alternative)
U2 U2: ch dr tr se Write block (alternative)
& &filename Load and execute USR file

Reading Drive Memory (BASIC)

10 OPEN 15,8,15
20 OPEN 5,8,5,"#"
30 REM READ 32 BYTES FROM $C000
40 PRINT#15,"M-R"CHR$(0)CHR$(192)CHR$(32)
50 FOR I=1 TO 32
60 GET#5,A$:IF A$="" THEN A$=CHR$(0)
70 PRINT ASC(A$);
80 NEXT
90 CLOSE 5:CLOSE 15

Writing Drive Memory (BASIC)

10 OPEN 15,8,15
20 REM WRITE 3 BYTES TO $0500
30 PRINT#15,"M-W"CHR$(0)CHR$(5)CHR$(3);
40 PRINT#15,CHR$(169)CHR$(0)CHR$(96);
50 REM THAT'S: LDA #$00, RTS
60 CLOSE 15

Executing Drive Code (BASIC)

10 OPEN 15,8,15
20 REM FIRST WRITE CODE TO $0500
30 PRINT#15,"M-W"CHR$(0)CHR$(5)CHR$(5);
40 REM INC $1C00 (toggle LED) + RTS
50 PRINT#15,CHR$(238)CHR$(0)CHR$(28)CHR$(96);
60 REM EXECUTE IT
70 PRINT#15,"M-E"CHR$(0)CHR$(5)
80 CLOSE 15

Writing Drive Code (Assembly)

; Send code to 1541 and execute it
; Uses Kernal I/O routines

send_drivecode:
    ; Open command channel
    lda #15
    ldx #8
    ldy #15
    jsr $ffba           ; SETLFS
    lda #0
    jsr $ffbd           ; SETNAM (no name)
    jsr $ffc0           ; OPEN
    
    ; Send M-W command
    ldx #15
    jsr $ffc9           ; CHKOUT
    
    lda #'M'
    jsr $ffd2           ; CHROUT
    lda #'-'
    jsr $ffd2
    lda #'W'
    jsr $ffd2
    lda #<$0500         ; Address low
    jsr $ffd2
    lda #>$0500         ; Address high
    jsr $ffd2
    lda #drivecode_end - drivecode  ; Length
    jsr $ffd2
    
    ; Send the code bytes
    ldx #0
send_loop:
    lda drivecode,x
    jsr $ffd2
    inx
    cpx #drivecode_end - drivecode
    bne send_loop
    
    jsr $ffcc           ; CLRCHN
    
    ; Now execute it
    ldx #15
    jsr $ffc9
    lda #'M'
    jsr $ffd2
    lda #'-'
    jsr $ffd2
    lda #'E'
    jsr $ffd2
    lda #<$0500
    jsr $ffd2
    lda #>$0500
    jsr $ffd2
    jsr $ffcc
    
    ; Close channel
    lda #15
    jsr $ffc3           ; CLOSE
    rts

drivecode:
    ; This code runs IN THE 1541!
    ; Flash drive LED 10 times
    ldx #20
flash:
    lda $1c00
    eor #$08            ; Toggle LED bit
    sta $1c00
    
    ldy #$ff            ; Delay
delay1:
    dey
    bne delay1
    
    dex
    bne flash
    rts
drivecode_end:

VIA #1 Registers (Serial Bus)

Address Bit Description
$1800 7 ATN IN
$1800 6-5 Device number switches
$1800 4 ATN acknowledge OUT
$1800 3 CLOCK OUT
$1800 2 CLOCK IN
$1800 1 DATA OUT
$1800 0 DATA IN

VIA #2 Registers (Drive Control)

Address Bit Description
$1C00 7 SYNC detected
$1C00 6-5 Bit rate (speed zone)
$1C00 4 Write protect sense
$1C00 3 LED (1=on)
$1C00 2 Motor (1=on)
$1C00 1-0 Stepper motor phase
$1C01 - Read/write data byte

Job Queue System

The 1541 uses a job queue at $00-$05 for disk operations:

Address Buffer Description
$00 0 Job code for buffer 0
$01 1 Job code for buffer 1
$02 2 Job code for buffer 2
$03 3 Job code for buffer 3
$04 4 Job code for buffer 4

Job Codes:

Code Name Description
$80 READ Read sector
$90 WRITE Write sector
$A0 VERIFY Verify sector
$B0 SEEK Position head
$C0 BUMP Head to track 1
$D0 JUMP Execute in buffer
$E0 EXECUTE Motor on + execute

Fast Loader Basics

Standard transfer: ~400 bytes/sec Fast loaders can achieve: ~10-20 KB/sec

Techniques used: 1. Custom 2-bit parallel protocol using CLK/DATA lines 2. Disable ROM interrupts in drive 3. Upload custom code to drive RAM 4. Synchronize C64 and 1541 timing

Resources

Resource URL
Inside Commodore DOS pagetable.com/docs/Inside%20Commodore%20DOS.pdf
1541 Memory Map sta.c64.org/cbm1541mem.html
Codebase64 Drivecode codebase64.org/doku.php?id=base:drivecode
1541 Manual zimmers.net/anonftp/pub/cbm/manuals/drives/1541-manual.txt

72. Commodore 1570 Internals

The 1570 was a transitional drive released primarily in Europe, bridging the gap between the 1541 and 1571.

1570 Overview

Specification Value
CPU MOS 6502 @ 2 MHz
RAM 2 KB ($0000-$07FF)
ROM 32 KB (8000−FFFF)
DOS CBM DOS V3.0
Capacity 170 KB (single-sided)
Format GCR + MFM capability
Interface Serial IEC bus
Release 1985 (Europe)

Hardware Description

The 1570 is essentially a 1571 board in a 1541 case with a single-sided drive mechanism: - Alps mechanism with track-0 sensor and index hole sensor - 1541 transformer and power supply - Full 1571 electronics (can read MFM disks) - Cannot use second side (single head mechanism)

Key Differences from 1541

Feature 1541 1570
Track 0 detection Head bump Photo-interrupter
Index hole sensor No Yes
MFM capability No Yes
CPU speed 1 MHz 2 MHz
ROM size 16 KB 32 KB
Burst mode No Yes (C128 only)

1570 Memory Map

Address Description
$0000-$00FF Zero page (job queue, variables)
$0100-$01FF CPU Stack
$0200-$02FF Command buffer
$0300-$06FF Data buffers (4 × 256 bytes)
$0700-$07FF BAM buffer
$1800-$180F VIA #1 (serial bus)
$1C00-$1C0F VIA #2 (drive control)
$4000-$400F WD1770 FDC (MFM controller)
$6000-$600F MOS 6526 CIA
8000−FFFF DOS ROM

Detecting 1570 vs 1571

; Read ROM byte to distinguish drives
detect_157x:
    ; Open command channel and send M-R
    ; Read byte from $FF2F
    ; 1570: Different ROM signature
    ; 1571: $A9 (LDA immediate)

1570-Specific Notes

  • ROM is patched from 1571 (has some bugs not present in 1571)
  • Cannot use 1571 upgrade ROM directly
  • Fast serial works with C128
  • CP/M disk reading works (MFM capability)
  • Relatively rare outside Europe/Australia

73. Commodore 1571 Internals

The 1571 was Commodore’s flagship 5.25” drive, designed as the perfect companion for the C128.

1571 Overview

Specification Value
CPU MOS 6502 @ 2 MHz
RAM 2 KB ($0000-$07FF)
ROM 32 KB (8000−FFFF)
DOS CBM DOS V3.0
Capacity 340 KB (double-sided)
Tracks 70 (35 per side)
Blocks 1,328 free
Format GCR native, MFM supported
Interface Serial IEC bus (fast serial capable)
Release 1985

Hardware Components

Component Description
MOS 6502 Main CPU @ 1 or 2 MHz
MOS 6522 VIA × 2 I/O controllers
MOS 6526 CIA Timer/serial interface
WD1770/WD1772 MFM floppy controller
64H157 Gate Array Address decoding, GCR

1571 Memory Map

Address Description
$0000-$00FF Zero page
$0100-$01FF CPU Stack
$0200-$02FF Command buffer
$0300-$06FF Data buffers
$0700-$07FF BAM buffer
$1800-$180F VIA #1 (serial bus)
$1C00-$1C0F VIA #2 (drive mechanics)
$2000-$2003 WD1770 FDC registers
$4000-$400F CIA 6526
8000−FFFF DOS ROM (32 KB)

WD1770 FDC Registers ($2000)

Offset Register Description
$00 Status/Command Status (read) / Command (write)
$01 Track Current track number
$02 Sector Current sector number
$03 Data Data register

1571 Operating Modes

1541 Emulation Mode:

OPEN 15,8,15,"U0>M0":CLOSE 15
  • Single-sided operation (170 KB)
  • 1 MHz CPU speed
  • Full 1541 compatibility

1571 Native Mode:

OPEN 15,8,15,"U0>M1":CLOSE 15
  • Double-sided operation (340 KB)
  • 2 MHz CPU speed
  • Fast serial with C128

Burst Mode Commands

Burst mode provides dramatically faster transfers on C128:

Command Code Description
Burst Read $30 Fast sector read
Burst Write $32 Fast sector write
Burst Inquire $34 Query disk format
Burst Format $36 Fast format
Burst Chgutl $3A Change device utilities

1571 Programming Example

; Switch 1571 to native mode from C64
switch_1571_mode:
    lda #15
    ldx #8
    ldy #15
    jsr $ffba           ; SETLFS
    lda #0
    jsr $ffbd           ; SETNAM
    jsr $ffc0           ; OPEN
    
    ldx #15
    jsr $ffc9           ; CHKOUT
    
    ; Send mode switch command
    lda #'U'
    jsr $ffd2
    lda #'0'
    jsr $ffd2
    lda #'>'
    jsr $ffd2
    lda #'M'
    jsr $ffd2
    lda #'1'            ; 1 = 1571 mode, 0 = 1541 mode
    jsr $ffd2
    
    jsr $ffcc           ; CLRCHN
    
    lda #15
    jsr $ffc3           ; CLOSE
    rts

Reading MFM (PC/CP/M) Disks

The 1571 can read various MFM formats:

REM Read CP/M disk directory
OPEN 1,8,0,"$0:*=P"

Supported MFM formats include: - CP/M (Kaypro, Osborne) - MS-DOS (with software: Big Blue Reader) - Custom formats up to 400 KB

1571 Variants

Model Description
1571 Standalone external drive
1571 (C128D) Built into European C128D
1571CR Cost-reduced, uses 5710 FDC chip

74. Commodore 1551 Internals

The 1551 was a unique drive designed exclusively for the Plus/4 family, using a parallel TCBM interface.

1551 Overview

Specification Value
CPU MOS 6510T @ 1 MHz
RAM 2 KB
ROM 16 KB
DOS CBM DOS V2.6
Capacity 170 KB (single-sided)
Tracks 35
Blocks 664 free
Interface TCBM parallel bus
Release 1985

What Makes the 1551 Special

The 1551 uses the TCBM (Tandem Commodore Bus Master) parallel interface instead of the slow serial bus: - Connects via cartridge port (not serial port) - ~5× faster than 1541 with C64 - Requires “paddle” cartridge interface - Only works with Plus/4, C16, C116

TCBM Bus Architecture

Signal Description
D0-D7 8-bit parallel data bus
DAV Data Available
ACK Acknowledge
STATUS Drive status
DEV Device select

6510T Processor

The 1551 uses a unique variant of the 6510: - Same instruction set as 6502/6510 - Built-in I/O port like 6510 - Only used in this drive - Different pinout from standard 6510

1551 Memory Map

Address Description
$0000-$00FF Zero page (job queue)
$0100-$01FF CPU Stack
$0200-$02FF Command buffer
$0300-$06FF Data buffers
$0700-$07FF BAM buffer
$4000-$4003 6523T TPI #1
$6000-$6003 6523T TPI #2
8000−BFFF Reserved
C000−FFFF DOS ROM (16 KB)

TPI 6523T Registers

The 6523T (Tri-Port Interface) provides I/O:

Offset Register Description
$00 Port A Data Parallel data bus
$01 Port B Data Control signals
$02 Port C Data Status/handshake
$03 Control Mode control

Speed Comparison

Drive Computer Transfer Rate
1541 C64 ~400 bytes/sec
1551 Plus/4 ~1,600 bytes/sec
1541 Plus/4 (serial) ~300 bytes/sec

Programming the 1551

From Plus/4 BASIC:

REM Standard file operations work the same
LOAD "PROGRAM",8
SAVE "PROGRAM",8

REM Directory listing
DIRECTORY

REM Device 8 is default
OPEN 15,8,15,"command"

Paddle Interface

The 1551 requires a special cartridge (“paddle”) that: - Plugs into Plus/4 expansion port - Contains 6523 TPI chip - Provides TCBM bus connection - Mimics memory-mapped I/O at FEC0−FEFF

Modern 1551 Alternatives

Device Description
TCBM2SD SD card adapter with TCBM interface
Pi1551 Raspberry Pi-based emulator

75. Commodore 1581 Internals

The 1581 was Commodore’s most advanced serial-bus drive, using 3.5” disks for 800 KB storage.

1581 Overview

Specification Value
CPU MOS 6502 @ 2 MHz
RAM 8 KB
ROM 32 KB
DOS CBM DOS V10 (3D)
Capacity 800 KB
Tracks 80 (logical)
Sectors 40 per track
Blocks 3,160 free
Directory entries 296 max
Format MFM (3.5” DD)
Interface Serial IEC (burst capable)
Release 1987

Hardware Components

Component Description
MOS 6502 Main CPU @ 2 MHz
WD1770/WD1772 MFM floppy controller
MOS 8520 CIA I/O and serial interface
3.5” mechanism Same as Amiga A1010

1581 Memory Map

Address Description
$0000-$00FF Zero page (job queue at $02-$0B)
$0100-$01FF CPU Stack
$0200-$02FF Command buffer
$0300-$07FF Work area
$0800-$0AFF Error message buffer
$0B00-$0FFF Buffer space
$1000-$1FFF Track cache buffer (4 KB)
$2000-$3FFF Additional RAM
$4000-$400F WD1772 FDC
$6000-$600F CIA 8520
8000−FFFF DOS ROM (32 KB)

WD1772 FDC Registers ($4000)

Offset Read Write
$00 Status Command
$01 Track Track
$02 Sector Sector
$03 Data Data

Job Queue ($02-$0B)

Similar to 1541/1571 but expanded:

Address Buffer Description
$02 0 Job 0 (command parsing)
$03 1 Job 1
$04 2 Job 2
… … …
$0B 9 Job 9

Job Codes

Code Name Description
$80 READ Read sector
$90 WRITE Write sector
$A0 VERIFY Verify sector
$B0 SEEK Seek to track
$C0 FORMAT Format track
$D0 JUMP Execute buffer code
$E0 EXECUTE Execute with spinup

Disk Format

Parameter Value
Physical tracks 80 per side
Physical sectors 10 per track (512 bytes)
Logical sectors 40 per track (256 bytes)
Directory track 40
BAM tracks 40 (sectors 1-2)
Total blocks 3,200 (3,160 free)

Partition Support

The 1581 introduced partitions (subdirectories):

REM Create a partition
OPEN 15,8,15,"/0:MYPART,1,800":CLOSE 15

REM Select a partition
OPEN 15,8,15,"/0:MYPART":CLOSE 15

REM Return to root
OPEN 15,8,15,"/0:":CLOSE 15

Burst Commands

Command Code Description
Read $00 Burst sector read
Write $02 Burst sector write
Inquire $04 Query format
Format $06 Burst format
Query Status $08 Get status
Dump Cache $0A Dump track cache

Burst Protocol Example

; C128 burst read example
burst_read:
    lda #$00        ; Burst read command
    sta $dd00       ; Send to drive via CIA
    
    ; Wait for data ready
wait_burst:
    bit $dd00
    bvc wait_burst
    
    lda $dd01       ; Read data byte
    ; ... continue reading
    rts

Auto-Boot Feature

The 1581 can auto-execute a file named COPYRIGHT CBM 86:

REM Create auto-boot file
SAVE "COPYRIGHT CBM 86",8

This file loads and runs automatically when drive is reset.

Track Cache Buffer

The 1581 has a 4 KB track cache at $1000-$1FFF: - Caches entire track (10 sectors × 512 bytes) - Dramatically speeds sequential access - Reduces head seeks

Detecting Drive Type

; Read ROM signature to identify drive
detect_drive:
    ; Send M-R command to read $FF2F
    ; 1581: $01
    ; 1571: $A9 
    ; 1541: $AA

Or in BASIC:

100 A=65331:DT$="UNKNOWN"
110 HI=INT(A/256):LO=A-256*HI
120 OPEN1,8,15
130 PRINT#1,"M-R";CHR$(LO);CHR$(HI)
140 GET#1,A$:T=ASC(A$)
150 IF T=108 THEN DT$="1581"
160 IF T=173 THEN DT$="1571"
170 IF T=170 THEN DT$="1541"
180 PRINT"DRIVE TYPE: ";DT$
190 CLOSE1

1581 vs PC Floppy Format

Feature 1581 PC 720KB
Tracks 80 80
Sectors/track 10 × 512 9 × 512
Sides 2 2
Capacity 800 KB 720 KB
File system CBM DOS FAT12

With Big Blue Reader software, the 1581 can read/write PC disks.

Drive Comparison Summary

Feature 1541 1551 1570 1571 1581
Disk size 5.25” 5.25” 5.25” 5.25” 3.5”
Sides 1 1 1 2 2
Capacity 170 KB 170 KB 170 KB 340 KB 800 KB
Blocks free 664 664 664 1,328 3,160
Format GCR GCR GCR+MFM GCR+MFM MFM
CPU 6502 6510T 6502 6502 6502
CPU speed 1 MHz 1 MHz 2 MHz 2 MHz 2 MHz
RAM 2 KB 2 KB 2 KB 2 KB 8 KB
ROM 16 KB 16 KB 32 KB 32 KB 32 KB
Interface Serial TCBM Serial Serial Serial
Burst mode No No Yes Yes Yes
Partitions No No No No Yes
Track cache No No No No Yes

Demo Effect Tutorials

Graphics Mode Extensions

Mode Resolution Colors Description
FLI (Flexible Line Interpretation) 160×200 16 per 4×1 Changes color RAM every line via $D018 manipulation
AFLI (Advanced FLI) 320×200 Variable Hires FLI, no color RAM needed
IFLI (Interlaced FLI) 160×200 ~50+ Two FLI images alternating with X-scroll shift
NUFLI (New Underlayed FLI) 320×200 High AFLI + sprite underlays, developed by Crest
MUIFLI/MUCSU FLI Various High Multicolor underlayed variants
SHF (Super Hires FLI) 320×200 High Extended color modes

Classic Demo Effects

Effect Description Technique
DYCP (Different Y Character Positions) Wavy text scroller Plot characters into charset at varying Y offsets
DXYCP DYCP with X movement Pre-shifted font data, pixel-level positioning
FLD (Flexible Line Distance) Stretch/compress display Manipulate $D011 Y-scroll during frame
FPP (Flexible Pixel Positioning) Arbitrary line Y-placement FLI variation for rotation/barrel effects
VSP (Variable Screen Position) Horizontal bitmap offset Cycle-exact $D011 timing
Tech-Tech Horizontal line wobble X-position changes per line via $D016
AGSP Combined VSP + Linecruncher Enables smooth bitmap scrolling games
Sprite Stretching/Crunching Extended sprite height Manipulate sprite Y during display

Sprite Techniques

Technique Description Use Case
Sprite Multiplexing Reuse sprites below raster Display 16-64+ sprites
Zone Multiplexing Fixed vertical zones Simpler, predictable timing
Sorting Multiplexer Dynamic Y-sorted sprites Flexible positioning
Toggleplexing Alternate frames Simple doubling without raster tricks
Border Sprites Display sprites in border Open top/bottom border first

Comprehensive Demo Coding Tutorials

Resource Description Link
An Introduction to Programming C-64 Demos Linus Åkerlund’s comprehensive guide (FLI, DYCP, interrupts, scrollers) https://www.antimon.org/code/Linus/
Codebase64 Demo Programming Wiki with code examples for all major effects https://codebase64.org/doku.php?id=base:demo_coding_introduction
The Raistlin Papers Advanced techniques (DXYCP, optimization) https://c64demo.com/
Dustlayer VIC-II tutorials and visual explanations https://dustlayer.com/
0xC64 Scroller tutorials (1×1, 4×4 dynamic) http://www.0xc64.com/

DYCP Implementation

Basic Concept: 1. Create a 40-column × 6-row character matrix on screen 2. Each column uses 2 consecutive charset characters 3. Plot font data at variable Y offset within the 16 bytes 4. Use sine table for smooth wave motion 5. Scroll X position via $D016 for horizontal movement

Resources: - DYCP tutorial: http://www.antimon.org/dl/c64/code/dycp.txt - GitHub example: https://github.com/Twilight1971/C64–DYCP-Scroller

Sprite Multiplexer Implementation

Key Steps: 1. Sort virtual sprites by Y-coordinate (insertion sort recommended) 2. Map virtual sprites to 8 hardware sprites 3. Set up raster IRQ chain for each “zone” 4. Update sprite registers when raster passes sprite bottom 5. Handle X MSB ($D010) efficiently

Resources: - Cadaver’s Sprite Multiplexing: https://cadaver.github.io/rants/sprite.html - Shallan’s Video Tutorial: https://youtube.com/watch?v=LPdjmjoMUEg - C64 Multiplexer Library: https://github.com/Fabrizio-Caruso/C64_Multiplexer_C_LIB

FLI Implementation

How FLI Works: 1. Set up bitmap mode with color RAM 2. Create IRQ at top of FLI area 3. On each line, write new value to $D018 (VIC memory control) 4. This forces VIC to re-read color data 5. Creates “FLI bug” (3 black chars on left) - unavoidable

Memory Layout: - 8 screen RAM copies at different offsets - Bitmap data at standard location - Cycle-exact timing required

Useful Code Examples (GitHub)

Repository Description Link
C64–DYCP-Scroller KickAssembler DYCP example https://github.com/Twilight1971/C64–DYCP-Scroller
C64-VSP VSP code for KickAssembler GitHub (Twilight1971)
spritemultiplexer Lasse Öörni-based multiplexer tutorial https://github.com/zendar/spritemultiplexer
c64lib Copper64 (raster effects library) https://github.com/c64lib

Demo Effect Databases

Resource Description Link
C64 Demo World Records Categorized effects with video examples https://noiseehc.github.io/c64wrd/
Demozoo C64 Tagged productions searchable by effect https://demozoo.org/
CSDb Release database with effect tags https://csdb.dk/
Name Description Link
HVSC (High Voltage SID Collection) Largest C64 music archive - 50,000+ tunes, regularly updated https://www.hvsc.c64.org/
CGSC (Compute’s Gazette SID Collection) Collection of SIDs from Compute’s Gazette Various
Ultimate SID Collection Curated collection of 256 tunes recorded from real hardware https://ultimatesid.dk/
The SID Homepage Classic portal for SID music, players, interviews, and HVSC updates http://www.sidmusic.org/sid/
SOASC= Project Entire HVSC recorded from real C64s Various

Demo Programming & Advanced Topics

Stable Raster Techniques

Technique Description Complexity
Double IRQ Two interrupts to eliminate jitter Moderate
Auxiliary Timer CIA timer to measure jitter Advanced
Bad Line Sync Synchronize with bad lines Moderate
Sprite Sync Use sprite collision for timing Moderate
Polling Method $D012 comparison loop Basic

Key Concepts

  • Bad Lines: Every 8th line where VIC-II steals cycles for character fetches
  • Raster Interrupt: VIC-II interrupt triggered at specific scan line
  • NMI Handling: Non-maskable interrupts (RESTORE key, CIA2)
  • VIC-II Timing: 63 cycles/line (PAL), 65 cycles/line (NTSC)
  • Sprite Multiplexing: Reusing sprites on different scan lines

Resources

Resource Description Link
An Introduction to Programming C-64 Demos Comprehensive demo coding guide https://odd.blog/
Stable Raster Routines Technical article on raster stabilization https://codebase64.org/doku.php?id=base:making_stable_raster_routines
VIC-II Timing Detailed timing information C64-Wiki

Game Development Tools & Frameworks

Game Makers & Engines

Name Description Link
C64 Game Maker Windows IDE using Oscar64/LLVM-MOS, visual game development https://agpx.itch.io/c64-game-maker
GameForge - C64 Node-based visual scripting with Oscar64, in development https://futurevisionlabs.itch.io/gameforge-c64
SEUCK (Shoot-’Em-Up Construction Kit) Classic game maker for shoot-em-ups Various
Vision BASIC REU-enhanced BASIC with compiler for game development Commercial
c64engine Game engine framework for C64 https://github.com/leissa/c64engine

Sprite Multiplexers

Name Description
Various multiplexer libraries Allow more than 8 sprites via raster tricks
C64 Game Framework Lasse Öörni’s framework including multiplexer

Game Development Notes

  • Oscar64 proven capable of fast-paced action games
  • LLVM-MOS provides excellent optimization
  • Sprite multiplexing essential for complex games
  • Consider fast loader integration (Krill’s Loader)
  • Use compression for asset management

Testing & Debugging

Systematic testing and debugging is crucial for reliable C64 software development.

Test Suites

Wolfgang Lorenz Test Suite

The definitive CPU test suite for 6502/6510 emulator verification.

Aspect Coverage
All opcodes Including undocumented/illegal opcodes
Addressing modes All 13 addressing modes
Flags N, V, B, D, I, Z, C behavior
Edge cases Page boundary crossing, wrap-around

Download: zimmers.net/anonftp/pub/cbm/crossplatform/emulators/pc64/

VICE-maintained version includes fixes for newer C64 revisions.

Klaus Dormann’s 6502 Test Suite

  • Includes decimal mode testing
  • Standalone (~16KB)
  • Platform-independent

VICE Test Programs

Comprehensive tests for all C64 subsystems:

testprogs/
├── CPU/           # CPU instruction tests
├── CIA/           # Timer and I/O tests
├── VICII/         # Video chip tests
├── SID/           # Sound chip tests
├── interrupts/    # IRQ/NMI behavior
└── general/       # Lorenz suite + others

Repository: sourceforge.net/p/vice-emu/code/HEAD/tree/testprogs/

VICE Monitor

The built-in VICE monitor is a powerful debugging tool.

Entering the Monitor: - Alt+M (or Alt+H on some systems) - SYS 64738 to reset and enter - Breakpoint triggers

Essential Commands:

Command Description Example
d Disassemble d 1000 1050
m Memory dump m 0400 0500
r Show registers r
g Go (continue) g 1000
x Exit monitor x

Breakpoints:

Command Description Example
break Set breakpoint break 1000
watch Watch memory watch load 0400
trace Trace execution trace on
until Run until condition until 1000
delete Remove breakpoint delete 1

Memory Examination:

(C:$1000) m d000 d030    ; View VIC-II registers
(C:$1000) m dc00 dc10    ; View CIA #1
(C:$1000) m d400 d420    ; View SID registers
(C:$1000) > 1000 4c 00 10 ; Write bytes

Conditional Breakpoints:

break 1000 if .A == $ff
break 2000 if @(0400) == $01
watch store d020 if .A != @(d020)

Saving/Loading State:

save "debug.bin" 0 1000 2000    ; Save memory range
load "debug.bin" 0 1000         ; Load memory
snapshot "save.vsf"             ; Save emulator state

C64 Studio Debugging

C64 Studio offers integrated debugging with VICE:

  1. Set breakpoints in source code (click line number)
  2. Step through code (F10 = step over, F11 = step into)
  3. Watch variables in memory view
  4. Inspect registers in real-time
  5. View symbols from debug info

Debugging Techniques

Memory Corruption Detection

; Fill unused memory with pattern
    ldx #0
    lda #$aa
fill:
    sta $c000,x
    inx
    bne fill
    
; Later, check for corruption
check:
    lda $c000,x
    cmp #$aa
    bne corrupted

Timing Verification

; Visual timing check (border color)
    inc $d020       ; Change border at start
    ; ... code to time ...
    dec $d020       ; Restore at end

Stack Debugging

; Save stack pointer for analysis
    tsx
    stx $02         ; Store in zeropage

Printf-Style Debugging

; Output debug value to screen
debug_print:
    sta $0400       ; Show accumulator on screen
    rts

Common Issues & Solutions

Problem Symptoms Solution
Off-by-one Loops run wrong count Check loop conditions, use BNE vs BEQ
Page crossing Timing issues, wrong data Align tables, check addressing modes
Stack overflow Random crashes Balance JSR/RTS, check recursion
IRQ conflicts Glitches, hangs Save/restore registers, check timing
Uninitialized Random behavior Initialize all variables
Timing race Intermittent bugs Add NOPs, check raster timing

Diagnostic Cartridges

Product Tests Source
Dead Test RAM, ROM, basic CPU Works on dead machines
586220 Full system diagnostic Original Commodore
Diagnostic Harness CIA, User/Joy ports Various
64 Doctor Comprehensive Commercial

Automated Testing

Using VICE for automated tests:

# Run test with timeout
x64sc -autostartprgmode 1 -limitcycles 10000000 test.prg

# With monitor commands
x64sc -moncommands test.mon test.prg

Monitor script example (test.mon):

break $2000
g
m 0400 0410
quit

Resources

Resource URL Description
VICE Testbench vice-emu.pokefinder.org/wiki/Testbench Test program status
Visual 6502 visual6502.org CPU test programs
Codebase64 Debugging codebase64.org Debugging tutorials
C64 Studio github.com/GeorgRottensteiner/C64Studio Integrated debugger

Cross-Development Complete Workflow

A comprehensive guide to setting up a modern cross-development environment for C64 programming.

Overview

Modern C64 development typically follows this workflow:

┌─────────────┐    ┌───────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌────────────┐
│ Write Code  │───►│  Compile  │───►│ Test in  │───►│ Deploy to  │
│ (PC/Mac)    │    │ (Assembler│    │  VICE    │    │ Real C64   │
└─────────────┘    └───────────┘    └──────────┘    └────────────┘

Recommended Tool Combinations

Skill Level IDE Assembler Debugger
Beginner CBM prg Studio Built-in VICE
Intermediate VS Code + VS64 KickAssembler C64 Debugger
Advanced VS Code + custom ACME/KickAss VICE Monitor
C Development VS Code cc65/Oscar64 VICE

VS Code Setup (Recommended)

Step 1: Install Prerequisites

# Java (required for KickAssembler)
# Windows: Download from adoptium.net
# Mac: brew install openjdk
# Linux: sudo apt install default-jdk

# VICE Emulator
# Download from vice-emu.sourceforge.io

Step 2: Install KickAssembler

Download from theweb.dk/KickAssembler and extract to a known location (e.g., C:\Tools\KickAssembler or ~/tools/kickassembler).

Step 3: Install VS Code Extensions

  1. VS64 by rolandshacks - Full C64 development environment
  2. Kick Assembler (C64) by Paul Hocker - KickAssembler syntax support

Step 4: Configure VS Code

Create .vscode/settings.json in your project:

{
    "kickassembler.kickAssJar": "C:/Tools/KickAssembler/KickAss.jar",
    "kickassembler.javaBin": "java",
    "kickassembler.viceBin": "C:/Tools/VICE/bin/x64sc.exe",
    "kickassembler.outputDir": "./build"
}

Project Structure

myproject/
├── src/
│   ├── main.asm          # Main entry point
│   ├── graphics.asm      # Sprite/char data
│   ├── music.asm         # SID music
│   └── includes/
│       ├── macros.asm
│       └── constants.asm
├── assets/
│   ├── sprites.png       # Source graphics
│   └── music.sid         # Source music
├── build/                # Output directory
├── tools/                # Converters, scripts
├── Makefile              # Build automation
└── .vscode/
    ├── settings.json
    ├── tasks.json
    └── launch.json

Build Automation with Make

Basic Makefile:

# Configuration
ASM = java -jar $(KICKASS)/KickAss.jar
VICE = x64sc
C1541 = c1541
SRC = src/main.asm
OUT = build/game.prg
D64 = build/game.d64

# Default target
all: $(OUT)

# Compile
$(OUT): $(SRC)
    @mkdir -p build
    $(ASM) -o $(OUT) $(SRC)

# Create D64 disk image
disk: $(OUT)
    $(C1541) -format "game,01" d64 $(D64) -write $(OUT) "game"

# Run in VICE
run: $(OUT)
    $(VICE) -autostartprgmode 1 $(OUT)

# Run with debugging
debug: $(OUT)
    $(VICE) -autostartprgmode 1 -remotemonitor $(OUT)

# Clean build artifacts
clean:
    rm -rf build/*

.PHONY: all disk run debug clean

Usage:

make          # Compile
make run      # Compile and run in VICE
make debug    # Compile and run with monitor
make disk     # Create D64 image
make clean    # Remove build files

VS Code Tasks Configuration

Create .vscode/tasks.json:

{
    "version": "2.0.0",
    "tasks": [
        {
            "label": "Build",
            "type": "shell",
            "command": "make",
            "group": { "kind": "build", "isDefault": true },
            "problemMatcher": []
        },
        {
            "label": "Build and Run",
            "type": "shell",
            "command": "make run",
            "group": "build"
        },
        {
            "label": "Build and Debug",
            "type": "shell",
            "command": "make debug",
            "group": "build"
        }
    ]
}

Debugging with VICE

Launch Configuration (.vscode/launch.json):

{
    "version": "0.2.0",
    "configurations": [
        {
            "type": "vice",
            "request": "launch",
            "name": "Debug C64",
            "program": "${workspaceFolder}/build/game.prg",
            "viceExecutable": "x64sc",
            "viceArgs": ["-remotemonitor"]
        }
    ]
}

Symbol Files for Debugging:

KickAssembler can generate symbol files:

java -jar KickAss.jar -symbolfile -showmem main.asm

Load symbols in VICE monitor:

(C:$0000) ll "main.vs"

C64 Debugger Integration

C64 Debugger by Samar provides visual debugging:

  1. Configure KickAssembler to output debug info:

    java -jar KickAss.jar -debugdump main.asm

  2. Launch C64 Debugger with your PRG:

    c64debugger -wait 2500 -prg build/game.prg -autojmp

Deploying to Real Hardware

Method Device Speed Notes
SD2IEC SD card adapter Fast Copy PRG/D64 to SD
Ultimate II+ USB/Network Very fast Ethernet/USB support
1541 Ultimate USB Fast Network boot possible
Pi1541 Raspberry Pi Medium Cycle-accurate 1541
ZoomFloppy USB to IEC Slow Direct to real 1541

SD2IEC Deployment:

# Copy to SD card
cp build/game.prg /media/sd2iec/
# Or create D64 and copy
make disk
cp build/game.d64 /media/sd2iec/

Network Deployment (Ultimate II+):

# Using ultimate_lib
ultimate_cli 192.168.1.100 run build/game.prg

CI/CD with GitHub Actions

.github/workflows/build.yml:

name: Build C64 Project

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v3
    
    - name: Setup Java
      uses: actions/setup-java@v3
      with:
        distribution: 'temurin'
        java-version: '17'
    
    - name: Download KickAssembler
      run: |
        wget http://theweb.dk/KickAssembler/KickAssembler.zip
        unzip KickAssembler.zip -d kickassembler
    
    - name: Build
      run: |
        java -jar kickassembler/KickAss.jar src/main.asm -o build/game.prg
    
    - name: Upload Artifact
      uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3
      with:
        name: game-prg
        path: build/game.prg

IDE Platform Features
C64 Studio Windows All-in-one, sprite/char editors, VICE integration
CBM prg Studio Windows BASIC + Assembly, multiple CBM targets
Relaunch64 Cross-platform Java-based, multiple assembler support
Sublime + plugins Cross-platform Lightweight, syntax highlighting
