Networking Projects

Connect your C64 to the modern internet via Ethernet, WiFi, or serial connections.

Hardware Options

Ethernet Cards

Device Chip Interface Features
RR-Net MK3 CS8900A Expansion port 10Mbit, standalone/Retro Replay
64NIC+ CS8900A Expansion port 10Mbit, ROM socket for 16 images
TFE (The Final Ethernet) CS8900A Expansion port 10Mbit, original design
ETH64 W5100 Expansion port 10/100Mbit

WiFi Modems

Device Interface Features
WiModem User Port ESP8266, Hayes compatible, $55
C64Net WiFi User Port ESP8266, BASIC programs included
Link232 WiFi User Port ESP-based, telnet
SwiftLink WiFi Cartridge Internal WiFi, 38400 baud

Integrated Solutions

Device Type Networking
Ultimate 64 FPGA C64 Built-in Ethernet
Ultimate II+ Cartridge Ethernet + emulated Swiftlink
Turbo Chameleon 64 Cartridge/Standalone Ethernet capable

Software Stack

┌────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Applications (IRC, FTP, Web, BBS)  │
├────────────────────────────────────┤
│ TCP/IP Stack (IP65, Contiki, etc.) │
├────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Network Driver (CS8900A, SLIP)     │
├────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Hardware (Ethernet/WiFi/Serial)    │
└────────────────────────────────────┘

Contiki OS

A complete TCP/IP operating system for the C64.

Features: - TCP/IP stack (~10KB RAM, ~30KB ROM) - Multitasking - GUI with mouse support - Web browser, IRC, FTP, Email clients

Installation: 1. Download from contiki.cbm8bit.com 2. Configure IP settings with IPCONFIG 3. Requires RR-Net, TFE, ETH64, or compatible

Applications included:

App Function
WebBrowser Simple HTTP browser
IRC Internet Relay Chat client
Email POP3/SMTP client
FTP File transfer
WGET Download files
Telnet Remote terminal
DHCP Auto IP configuration

IP65 Library

Lightweight TCP/IP stack for C64 development.

Features: - TCP and UDP support - DNS resolution - DHCP client - ~8KB footprint - cc65 compatible

Example (HTTP GET):

#include <ip65.h>

void main() {
    if (ip65_init()) return;
    dhcp_init();
    
    // Connect to server
    tcp_connect(server_ip, 80);
    tcp_send("GET / HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n", 18);
    
    // Receive response
    while (tcp_recv(buffer, sizeof(buffer)) > 0) {
        // Process data
    }
    tcp_close();
}

BBS Connectivity

Terminal Programs:

Program Features
CCGMS Most popular, PETSCII/ASCII, XMODEM
Striketerm Modern features, C128 support
Novaterm Feature-rich, VT100
KipperTerm Ethernet-native, RR-Net
GuruTerm PETSCII telnet for RR-Net

Popular BBS Systems:

BBS Address Port
Particles! particlesbbs.dyndns.org 6400
Antidote antidote.hopto.org 6400
Cottonwood cottonwoodbbs.dyndns.org 6502
CommodoreServer commodoreserver.com Various

Connecting via WiFi Modem:

AT                    ; Check modem
OK
ATW"SSID,password"    ; Connect to WiFi
OK
ATDT"bbs.example.com:6400"  ; Dial BBS
CONNECT

Running a C64 BBS

Software Options:

BBS Software Features
Color 64 Popular, feature-rich
C-Net Many versions, extensible
Image BBS Graphics capable
EBBS Simple, lightweight

Hardware Setup:

Internet ──► Router ──► TCPSER/PC ──► RS-232 ──► C64 + BBS
                    or
Internet ──► Router ──► WiFi Modem ──► User Port ──► C64 + BBS

TCPSER (PC as modem gateway):

tcpser -v 25232 -p 6400 -s 2400 -l 7
# Listens on TCP 6400, connects to COM7 at 2400 baud

Multiplayer Gaming

NetRacer: - 8 simultaneous players - LAN or Internet - Demonstrates networking potential

Vortex 2: - Multiplayer space game - Server at vortex.jammingsignal.com

Network Programming

CS8900A Register Access (RR-Net):

CS8900_BASE = $DE00

; Read packet
read_packet:
    lda CS8900_BASE+$00  ; PacketPage pointer
    ; ... process packet data

IP65 with cc65:

#include <ip65.h>

int main(void) {
    // Initialize network
    if (ip65_init()) {
        printf("Network init failed\n");
        return 1;
    }
    
    // Get IP via DHCP
    if (dhcp_init()) {
        printf("DHCP failed\n");
        return 1;
    }
    
    printf("IP: %d.%d.%d.%d\n", 
        cfg_ip[0], cfg_ip[1], 
        cfg_ip[2], cfg_ip[3]);
    
    return 0;
}

Modern Services

Service URL Description
CommodoreServer commodoreserver.com Cloud storage, V-1541
Retro BBS List telnetbbsguide.com BBS directory
SyncTERM syncterm.bbsdev.net Modern BBS client (for testing)

WiFi Modem AT Commands

Common commands for ESP8266-based modems:

Command Function
AT Check modem
ATW"ssid,pass" Connect to WiFi
ATW? Show current WiFi
ATDT"host:port" Connect (dial)
ATH Hang up
AT&K0 Disable flow control
ATS0=1 Auto-answer on 1 ring
AT+CONFIG Save settings

Resources

Resource URL Description
Contiki contiki.cbm8bit.com OS download
IP65 github.com/oliverschmidt/ip65 TCP/IP library
Lemon64 Network Thread lemon64.com Software list
C64 OS Networking c64os.com/networkingguide WiFi setup
CCGMS csdb.dk Terminal program

PAL vs NTSC Development

Writing portable code that works on both PAL and NTSC C64 systems.

Key Differences

Property PAL (Europe) NTSC (Americas) Old NTSC
VIC-II Chip 6569 6567R8 6567R56A
CPU Clock 985,248 Hz 1,022,727 Hz 1,022,727 Hz
Cycles/Line 63 65 64
Raster Lines 312 263 262
Cycles/Frame 19,656 17,095 16,832
Frame Rate 50.125 Hz 59.826 Hz 60.99 Hz
Visible Lines ~284 ~235 ~234
Border Lines 112 63 62

Timing Impact

Processing Time: - PAL has ~15% more CPU cycles per frame than NTSC - PAL code that uses all available time will break on NTSC - Particularly problematic for raster effects and music

Music Playback: - PAL music plays ~20% faster on NTSC - NTSC music plays ~16% slower on PAL - Most noticeable with drum patterns and tempo

Detection Code

Simple Detection:

; Detect PAL/NTSC by counting raster lines
detect_system:
    sei
    
    ; Wait for raster line 0
-   lda $d012
    bne -
    
    ; Count lines until wrap
    ldy #0
-   cpy $d012
    beq -
    iny
    bne -
    
    ; Y now contains highest raster line + 1
    ; PAL: 312 ($138), NTSC: 263 ($107) or 262 ($106)
    
    cpy #$38      ; Check if > 55 (PAL has line $138)
    bcs .is_pal
    
    ; NTSC
    lda #0
    sta system_type
    rts
    
.is_pal:
    lda #1
    sta system_type
    rts

system_type: .byte 0  ; 0=NTSC, 1=PAL

Robust Detection:

; More reliable - count cycles in frame
detect_system_robust:
    sei
    
    ; Synchronize to frame start
    lda #$ff
-   cmp $d012
    bne -
-   cmp $d012
    beq -
    
    ; Count iterations until next frame
    ldx #0
    ldy #0
    
-   inx
    bne +
    iny
+   lda $d012
    bne -
    
    ; Y:X = iteration count
    ; PAL: ~4900, NTSC: ~4200
    
    cpy #18       ; Threshold
    bcc .is_ntsc
    
    lda #1
    .byte $2c     ; BIT abs to skip
.is_ntsc:
    lda #0
    sta system_type
    cli
    rts

Portable Raster Code

Problem: Raster loops must complete in one scanline.

Fixed-Cycle Loop (PAL):

; PAL: 63 cycles per line
raster_loop_pal:
    lda color_table,x   ; 4
    sta $d020           ; 4
    sta $d021           ; 4
    inx                 ; 2
    
    ; Waste cycles to fill 63
    nop                 ; 2
    nop                 ; 2
    nop                 ; 2
    bit $ea             ; 3
    
    dey                 ; 2
    bne raster_loop_pal ; 3 (taken) = 28 cycles
                        ; Need 35 more for 63 total

Portable Version:

; Detect and branch to correct routine
setup_raster:
    lda system_type
    beq .use_ntsc
    
    ; PAL: Use 63-cycle loop
    lda #<raster_pal
    sta raster_ptr
    lda #>raster_pal
    sta raster_ptr+1
    rts
    
.use_ntsc:
    ; NTSC: Use 65-cycle loop
    lda #<raster_ntsc
    sta raster_ptr
    lda #>raster_ntsc
    sta raster_ptr+1
    rts

Portable Music

Tempo Adjustment:

; Music player call frequency adjustment
; PAL: call every frame (50 Hz)
; NTSC: skip every 6th frame for ~50 Hz equivalent

play_music:
    lda system_type
    beq .ntsc_adjust
    
    ; PAL - play every frame
    jmp music_play
    
.ntsc_adjust:
    ; NTSC - skip every 6th call
    inc frame_counter
    lda frame_counter
    cmp #6
    bcc .play
    
    lda #0
    sta frame_counter
    rts             ; Skip this frame
    
.play:
    jmp music_play

frame_counter: .byte 0

SID Frequency Tables:

Different clock speeds require different frequency values for the same notes:

; A-440 Hz frequency values
; PAL:  $1CD6 (clock 985248 Hz)
; NTSC: $1BE5 (clock 1022727 Hz)

freq_table_pal:
    .word $0116  ; C-0
    .word $0127  ; C#0
    ; ...
    
freq_table_ntsc:
    .word $010B  ; C-0  
    .word $011C  ; C#0
    ; ...

Screen Timing

Bad Lines: - PAL: 23 cycles available (40 cycles stolen) - NTSC: 25 cycles available (40 cycles stolen)

Sprite Timing: - Sprite DMA steals 2 cycles per sprite - Plus 1-3 cycles for DMA setup - Same on both systems

Raster IRQ Positioning:

; Lines 263+ don't exist on NTSC!
; This will hang on NTSC:
    lda #$00       ; High bit
    sta $d011
    lda #$30       ; Line 304 (PAL only!)
    sta $d012      ; NTSC will never reach this

Safe Raster Ranges:

Range PAL NTSC Notes
$00-$32 ✓ ✓ Upper border
33−FA ✓ ✓ Visible screen
FB−FF ✓ ✓ Lower border start
$100-$107 ✓ ✓ Lower border (use $d011 bit 7)
$108-$137 ✓ ✗ PAL only!

Sprite Positioning

X-Coordinate Wrap:

; Sprites wrap at different X positions
; PAL:  X > 404 appears at left
; NTSC: X > 412 appears at left

; This causes 8-pixel gap/overlap between systems
; Solution: Don't use extreme right coordinates

Complete Portable Template

; Portable C64 program template

* = $0801
    ; BASIC stub
    .word (+), 2024
    .byte $9e, "2061", 0
+   .word 0

* = $080d
start:
    jsr detect_system
    jsr init_tables
    jsr init_music
    jsr main_loop
    rts

detect_system:
    ; ... (detection code above)
    rts

init_tables:
    lda system_type
    beq .ntsc
    
    ; PAL setup
    lda #<freq_table_pal
    sta freq_ptr
    lda #>freq_table_pal  
    sta freq_ptr+1
    
    lda #63           ; Cycles per line
    sta cycles_line
    rts
    
.ntsc:
    lda #<freq_table_ntsc
    sta freq_ptr
    lda #>freq_table_ntsc
    sta freq_ptr+1
    
    lda #65           ; Cycles per line
    sta cycles_line
    rts

main_loop:
    ; Game/demo code using detected values
    ; ...
    jmp main_loop

; Variables
system_type:  .byte 0
cycles_line:  .byte 63
freq_ptr:     .word 0

Testing Tips

  1. Always test on both systems - Use VICE with -ntsc or -pal flags
  2. Test old NTSC - VICE: x64sc -ntscold (64 cycles/line)
  3. Use safe raster ranges - Stay below line $107
  4. Adjust music tempo - Or use PAL-only/NTSC-only versions
  5. Check FLI/FLD effects - Most sensitive to timing differences

Resources

Resource URL Description
Codebase64 PAL/NTSC codebase64.org Detailed differences
VIC-II Timing zimmers.net Chip documentation
VICE Options vice-emu.sourceforge.io Emulator flags

62. Compression Techniques

Compression is essential for C64 development - fitting more content into limited RAM and disk space.

Popular Crunchers Comparison

Tool Compression Decrunch Speed Decrunch Size Best For
Exomizer 3 Best Slower ~160 bytes Maximum compression, demos
PuCrunch Good Fast ~150 bytes Quick iterations, multi-platform
ByteBoozer 2 Very Good Fast Small Games, balanced approach
TSCrunch Very Good Very Fast Small Speed-critical, loaders
Doynax LZ Good Very Fast ~100 bytes Real-time decompression
Level Crusher Moderate Fast Tiny Small data blocks

Exomizer 3

The gold standard for compression ratio. Cross-platform (Windows/Mac/Linux).

Installation:

# Download from https://csdb.dk/release/?id=164859
# Or compile from source: https://github.com/bitshifters/exomizer

Basic Usage:

# Create self-extracting executable
exomizer sfx 2061 game.prg -o game_crunched.prg

# With border flash effect during decrunch
exomizer sfx 2061 game.prg -o game_crunched.prg -x "inc $d020"

# No effect (silent decrunch)
exomizer sfx 2061 -n game.prg -o game_crunched.prg

# Crunch raw data (no SFX header)
exomizer raw data.bin -o data_crunched.bin

# Memory-to-memory decrunch (specify target)
exomizer mem -l $4000 data.prg -o data_crunched.prg

Decrunch in Assembly (KickAssembler):

// Include Exomizer decrunch routine
.import source "exodecrunch.asm"

// Setup and call
    ldx #<crunched_data_end
    ldy #>crunched_data_end
    stx exo_src_lo
    sty exo_src_hi
    jsr decrunch

crunched_data:
    .import binary "data_crunched.bin"
crunched_data_end:

Tips: - Exomizer decrunches backwards (from end to start) - Use -f for forward decrunching (requires more memory) - Try -p1 through -p7 for different compression/speed tradeoffs

PuCrunch

Fast compression with good ratios. Supports C64, VIC-20, Plus/4, C128.

Installation:

# Download from https://github.com/mist64/pucrunch
# Compile: cc -o pucrunch pucrunch.c

Basic Usage:

# Create self-extracting C64 executable
pucrunch -c64 game.prg game_crunched.prg

# Set execution address explicitly
pucrunch -c64 -x$c000 game.prg game_crunched.prg

# Delta encoding (good for waveforms, gradients)
pucrunch -c64 -fdelta game.prg game_crunched.prg

# Faster (but larger) decompressor
pucrunch -c64 -ffast game.prg game_crunched.prg

# Shorter (but slower) decompressor
pucrunch -c64 -fshort game.prg game_crunched.prg

Options: - -l$addr - Override load address - -x$addr - Override execution address - -fbasic - For BASIC programs (auto-RUN) - -n - Disable RLE/LZ optimization (diagnostic)

ByteBoozer 2

Excellent balance of compression and speed. Popular in demos and games.

Usage:

# Basic compression
bb2 game.prg game_crunched.prg

# With execution address
bb2 -x$0810 game.prg game_crunched.prg

Decrunch Code:

; ByteBoozer 2 decruncher
; Source at bb2_crunched, destination is embedded
    jsr decrunch

TSCrunch

Very fast decompression, good for loaders and time-critical applications.

Download: https://csdb.dk/release/?id=176663

Choosing a Compressor

Use Exomizer when: - Maximum compression is critical - One-time load at game start - Demo parts where loading time is acceptable

Use PuCrunch when: - Rapid iteration during development - Cross-platform targets (VIC-20, Plus/4) - BASIC program compression

Use ByteBoozer/TSCrunch when: - Balance of size and speed needed - Multi-load games - Level streaming

Integration with Krill’s Loader

Krill’s Loader can decompress on-the-fly during disk loading:

; Load and decrunch in one operation
    lda #<filename
    ldx #>filename
    jsr load_crunched  ; Automatically detects and decrunches

Supported formats: Exomizer, ByteBoozer 2, Doynax LZ, and more.

Compression Benchmarks

Typical results on various C64 data types:

Data Type Original Exomizer PuCrunch BB2
Bitmap (Koala) 10,001 4,200 4,800 4,500
Sprites (64 frames) 4,096 1,800 2,100 1,950
Character set 2,048 900 1,100 1,000
SID music 4,000 2,200 2,500 2,350
Game code 20,000 8,500 9,200 8,800

Results vary significantly based on data content.

Resources

Resource URL
Exomizer csdb.dk/release/?id=164859
PuCrunch github.com/mist64/pucrunch
ByteBoozer 2 csdb.dk/release/?id=142783
TSCrunch csdb.dk/release/?id=176663
Krill’s Loader csdb.dk/release/?id=169364
Codebase64 Benchmarks codebase64.org/doku.php?id=base:compression_benchmarks

63. Troubleshooting Guide

Common Programming Mistakes

Assembly Language Errors

Mistake Symptom Solution
Missing # (immediate) Wrong value loaded LDA #$20 not LDA $20
Forgetting RTS Crash after JSR Always end subroutines with RTS
Not clearing carry Wrong ADC/SBC result CLC before ADC, SEC before SBC
Stack overflow Random crashes Balance PHA/PLA, limit JSR depth
Wrong addressing mode Unexpected behavior Check zero page vs absolute
Modifying code in ROM No effect Bank out ROM first ($01)
IRQ without RTI System freeze End interrupt handlers with RTI
Not saving registers Corrupted state Save A/X/Y at IRQ start

Timing Issues

Issue Cause Solution
Flickering sprites Raster race condition Set sprites before they’re drawn
Jittery scrolling IRQ timing variance Use stable raster technique
Music slowdown Too much work in frame Optimize or split across frames
Missed raster IRQ IRQ latency Account for 7-8 cycle jitter
Screen tearing Mid-frame updates Wait for vertical blank

Stable Raster IRQ Pattern:

irq_handler:
    ; Acknowledge IRQ
    lda $d019
    sta $d019
    
    ; Double-IRQ for stable timing
    lda #<irq_stable
    sta $0314
    lda #>irq_stable
    sta $0315
    
    lda #RASTERLINE+1
    sta $d012
    
    tsx             ; Save stack pointer
    cli             ; Allow next IRQ
    nop
    nop
    ; ... padding NOPs ...
    
irq_stable:
    txs             ; Restore stack (cancel pending)
    ; Now we have stable timing (+/- 1 cycle)
    
    ; Your timed code here
    
    ; Setup next IRQ
    lda #<irq_handler
    sta $0314
    lda #>irq_handler
    sta $0315
    lda #RASTERLINE
    sta $d012
    
    pla
    tay
    pla
    tax
    pla
    rti

Screen Problems (Software)

Symptom Likely Cause Fix
Black screen after code runs Crashed into infinite loop Check for missing RTS, bad JMP
Garbage on screen Writing to wrong address Verify screen RAM location
Wrong colors Color RAM not set Also write to $D800+
Characters not visible VIC bank mismatch Check $DD00 and $D018
Screen “tears” Scrolling during visible area Use VSP or wait for border
Sprites invisible Enable bits not set Set $D015 bits
Sprite wrong position X MSB not set Also set $D010 for X > 255

Memory Issues

Symptom Likely Cause Fix
BASIC variables corrupted Code overlaps BASIC area Load code to $C000+
IRQ stops working Kernal vector overwritten Restore $0314/$0315
SID silent after load I/O area banked out Set $01 to $35 or $37
Character ROM visible CHAREN bit wrong Check $01 bit 2
“Out of memory” in BASIC Variables area too small Lower top of BASIC

Safe Memory Configuration:

; Bank out BASIC, keep I/O and Kernal
    lda #$36
    sta $01

; Bank out BASIC and Kernal, keep I/O
    lda #$35
    sta $01
    
; All RAM (no I/O) - careful!
    lda #$34
    sta $01

Disk/Loading Problems

Symptom Likely Cause Fix
“File not found” Wrong filename or device Check spelling, device #
“Device not present” Drive not connected/powered Check connections
Load address wrong Missing ,8,1 Use LOAD"FILE",8,1
Data corrupted Timing issues with fastloader Adjust loader timing
Drive keeps spinning File open not closed Close files properly

Debugging Techniques

Using VICE Monitor

# Break into monitor
Alt+H (or machine menu)

# Set breakpoint at address
break $c000

# Set breakpoint on memory write
watch store $d020

# View memory
m c000 c100

# View registers
r

# Disassemble
d c000 c050

# Continue execution
g

# Single step
z

# Step over JSR
n

# View VIC state
io d000

# View SID state
io d400

Retro Debugger Features

  • Real-time memory view
  • Raster beam visualization
  • SID waveform display
  • Sprite position overlay
  • Breakpoints with conditions
  • Code rewind/stepping backward

BASIC Debugging

10 REM DEBUG PRINT
20 PRINT PEEK(addr)
30 PRINT CHR$(19);  : REM HOME CURSOR

40 REM POKE BORDER TO SHOW PROGRESS
50 POKE 53280,1 : REM RED = REACHED HERE

Assembly Debug Patterns

; Border color debug marker
    inc $d020       ; Flash border (remove in production)
    
; Checkpoint marker
debug1:
    lda #1
    sta $d020       ; Red = reached checkpoint 1
    
; Value display (put value in border)
    lda some_value
    sta $d020       ; Border shows value (0-15)

; Infinite loop trap
    jmp *           ; Hang here to confirm reached

Hardware vs Software Issues

If your C64 shows problems with ALL programs: - Likely hardware issue (see Appendix G) - Try known-working software first - Check power supply voltages

If problems only with YOUR code: - Software bug - Use debugger to trace - Check memory map conflicts

PAL vs NTSC Issues

Issue Cause Solution
Music too fast/slow Hardcoded timing Detect system, adjust
Screen position wrong Different visible area Use conditional raster values
Racing the beam fails Different cycle counts Test on both systems

System Detection:

; Detect PAL vs NTSC
    lda $02a6        ; 0 = NTSC, 1 = PAL
    beq is_ntsc
is_pal:
    ; 312 lines, 63 cycles/line
    jmp continue
is_ntsc:
    ; 263 lines, 65 cycles/line
continue:

Resources

Resource Description URL
Pictorial C64 Fault Guide Visual hardware diagnosis pictorial64.com
VICE Monitor Manual Debugger commands vice-emu.sourceforge.io
Retro Debugger Advanced GUI debugger sourceforge.net/projects/c64-debugger
C64 Studio IDE with debugging github.com/GeorgRottensteiner/C64Studio

64. Demoscene Effects Compendium

The C64 demoscene pioneered countless visual effects by exploiting VIC-II quirks. These techniques remain relevant for games and modern demos.

Effect Categories

Category Examples Difficulty
Raster Effects Rasterbars, split screens ⭐ Beginner
Character Effects DYCP, scrollers ⭐⭐ Intermediate
Bitmap Effects FLI, IFLI, NUFLI ⭐⭐⭐ Advanced
Border Effects Side/top/bottom border open ⭐⭐⭐ Advanced
3D Effects Vectors, rotators ⭐⭐⭐ Advanced

Raster Bars

The simplest demo effect - change background color at specific raster lines.

Basic Rasterbar:

; Simple rasterbar - change colors down the screen
    sei                 ; Disable interrupts
    
mainloop:
    lda $d012
    cmp #$40            ; Wait for raster line $40
    bne mainloop
    
    ldx #0
colorloop:
    lda colors,x        ; Get color from table
    sta $d021           ; Set background
    
    ; Waste time for one raster line (~63 cycles PAL)
    ldy #10
delay:  dey
        bne delay
    
    inx
    cpx #16             ; 16 color bars
    bne colorloop
    
    lda #0              ; Reset to black
    sta $d021
    jmp mainloop

colors:
    .byte 0,11,12,15,1,15,12,11,0,6,14,3,1,3,14,6

Smooth Moving Rasterbars:

; Move bars using sine table
    lda frame
    tax
    lda sinetable,x     ; Get Y position from sine
    sta $d012           ; Set raster compare
    inc frame           ; Next frame = next sine position

DYCP (Different Y Character Positions)

Scroll text where each character moves independently on Y axis.

Principle: 1. Create 8 copies of each character, shifted 0-7 pixels vertically 2. Place characters on screen in 8 consecutive rows 3. Select which “shifted” version of each character to display 4. Animate by cycling through Y positions via sine table

Memory Layout:

$2000-$27FF: Character set (8 shifted versions per char)
$0400-$07E7: Screen RAM (8 rows used for DYCP)

Core Loop:

; For each character in scroll text
    ldx char_index
    lda scroll_text,x   ; Get character
    
    ldy sine_offset     ; Get Y offset from sine (0-7)
    
    ; Calculate which shifted character to use
    asl                 ; char * 8
    asl
    asl
    ora sine_value,y    ; Add Y shift (0-7)
    
    ; Place on appropriate screen row
    sta screen_row,y

FLI (Flexible Line Interpretation)

Overcomes the 4-color-per-character-cell limitation by forcing the VIC-II to re-read color information every scanline.

How It Works: 1. Force a “bad line” every raster line by toggling $D011 bits 0-2 2. Change $D018 to point to different screen RAM each line 3. VIC reads new colors from 8 different screen RAMs

FLI Memory Layout:

$3C00-$3FFF: Color RAM (copied to $D800 at init)
$4000-$5FFF: 8 Screen RAMs ($4000, $4400, $4800...)
$6000-$7FFF: Bitmap data

FLI Kernel (per scanline):

; Must execute with stable timing!
; Called every raster line in bitmap area

fli_line:
    ; Change which screen RAM VIC reads
    lda d018_table,y    ; $08, $18, $28, $38, $48, $58, $68, $78
    sta $d018
    
    ; Force bad line by changing YSCROLL
    lda d011_table,y    ; Values that force bad line
    sta $d011
    
    iny
    cpy #8
    bne no_reset
    ldy #0
no_reset:
    ; ... continue to next line

FLI Bug: The first 3 character columns show garbage (grey) due to VIC timing. Solutions: - Leave columns 0-2 black (most common) - Use sprites to cover them (NUFLI technique)

Opening Borders

Top/Bottom Border:

; Must execute at exactly raster line $F9 (PAL)
border_open:
    lda $d011
    and #%11110111      ; Clear RSEL (24 rows)
    sta $d011
    
    ; Wait for line $FA
wait_fa:
    lda $d012
    cmp #$fa
    bne wait_fa
    
    lda $d011
    ora #%00001000      ; Set RSEL (25 rows)  
    sta $d011
    
    ; Border is now open - sprites can appear there!

Side Borders (every line):

; Must execute at cycle 56-57 of each raster line
side_border:
    lda $d016
    and #%11110111      ; Clear CSEL (38 columns)
    sta $d016           ; At cycle ~56
    
    ; By cycle 60, must:
    lda $d016
    ora #%00001000      ; Set CSEL (40 columns)
    sta $d016

Plasma Effect

Creates animated color patterns using sine wave combinations.

Character-Based Plasma:

; Simple 40x25 plasma
; Pre-calculate: plasma_char[x][y] = sin[x] + sin[y]

update_plasma:
    ldx #0              ; Screen position
    ldy y_offset        ; Animation offset
    
char_loop:
    ; Get character position's plasma value
    lda sin_x_table,x
    clc
    adc sin_y_table,y
    adc frame           ; Animation
    
    ; Use as color index
    and #$0f
    sta $d800,x         ; Color RAM
    
    lda #$a0            ; Solid block character
    sta $0400,x         ; Screen RAM
    
    inx
    cpx #0              ; Wrapped = done
    bne char_loop
    
    inc frame
    rts

Tech-Tech / Stretcher

Modifies YSCROLL ($D011) mid-screen to stretch/squash character rows.

; Stretch characters by repeating scanlines
stretch_effect:
    ldx current_line
    
    lda stretch_table,x ; How many extra lines for this row
    tay
    
stretch_loop:
    ; Wait for specific cycle
    lda $d011
    and #%11111000
    ora yscroll_val     ; Keep same YSCROLL = repeat line
    sta $d011
    dey
    bne stretch_loop
    
    ; Allow next character row
    inc yscroll_val

VSP (Variable Screen Position)

Instantly scroll screen horizontally by manipulating VIC timing.

; Shift screen left by X pixels (0-7)
; Must execute at exact cycle on bad line

vsp_shift:
    ; The timing here is critical - cycle 14
    sta $d011           ; Trigger bad line
    
    ; Screen is now shifted!
    ; Combine with $D016 for full 0-319 pixel positioning

Effect Resources

Resource URL Description
Codebase64 Demo Intro codebase64.org/doku.php?id=base:demo_coding_introduction Linus Åkerlund’s tutorial
C64 Demo Portal c64.ch Screenshots and downloads
CSDb csdb.dk Scene database
VIC-II Timing zimmers.net/cbmpics/cbm/c64/vic-ii.txt Technical reference
The Raistlin Papers c64demo.com Advanced effect tutorials

65. PETSCII and Screen Codes Reference

Understanding the Difference

Type Description Range Used By
PETSCII Input/output character codes 0-255 PRINT, GET, CHR$()
Screen Codes What’s stored in screen RAM 0-255 Direct screen memory
Color Codes Color values 0-15 D800−DBE7

Key Point: PETSCII ≠ Screen Codes! They use different numbering.

PETSCII to Screen Code Conversion

; Convert PETSCII in A to screen code
petscii_to_screen:
    cmp #$40
    bcc under_40        ; $00-$3F: subtract $00
    cmp #$60
    bcc range_40_5f     ; $40-$5F: unchanged
    cmp #$80
    bcc range_60_7f     ; $60-$7F: subtract $20
    cmp #$a0
    bcc range_80_9f     ; $80-$9F: add $40
    cmp #$c0
    bcc range_a0_bf     ; $A0-$BF: subtract $40
    ; $C0-$FF: subtract $80
    sec
    sbc #$80
    rts
    
under_40:
    rts                 ; No change
range_40_5f:
    rts                 ; No change  
range_60_7f:
    sec
    sbc #$20
    rts
range_80_9f:
    clc
    adc #$40
    rts
range_a0_bf:
    sec
    sbc #$40
    rts

Quick Reference Tables

Screen Codes (What you store in $0400+):

Range Content
$00-$1F @ A-Z [  ] ↑ ← and symbols
$20-$3F Space ! ” # $ % & ’ ( ) * + , - . / 0-9 : ; < = > ?
$40-$5F Horizontal line, graphics characters
$60-$7F More graphics characters
80−FF Reversed versions of $00-$7F

PETSCII Codes (What CHR$() returns):

Range Content
$00-$1F Control codes (colors, cursor)
$20-$3F Space, punctuation, numbers
$40-$5F @ A-Z [  ] ↑ ←
$60-$7F Graphics characters (shifted)
$80-$9F Control codes (colors, more)
A0−BF Graphics characters (Commodore key)
C0−DF More graphics (duplicates)
E0−FE More graphics (duplicates)
$FF π (pi)

Common PETSCII Control Codes

Code Dec Function BASIC
$05 5 White CTRL+2
$0D 13 Return RETURN
$11 17 Cursor Down CRSR↓
$12 18 Reverse On CTRL+9
$13 19 Home HOME
$14 20 Delete DEL
$1C 28 Red CTRL+3
$1D 29 Cursor Right CRSR→
$1E 30 Green CTRL+4
$1F 31 Blue CTRL+5
$81 129 Orange C=+1
$90 144 Black CTRL+1
$91 145 Cursor Up CRSR↑
$92 146 Reverse Off CTRL+0
$93 147 Clear Screen CLR
$94 148 Insert INST
$95 149 Brown C=+2
$96 150 Light Red C=+3
$97 151 Dark Grey C=+4
$98 152 Grey C=+5
$99 153 Light Green C=+6
$9A 154 Light Blue C=+7
$9B 155 Light Grey C=+8
$9C 156 Purple CTRL+6
$9D 157 Cursor Left CRSR←
$9E 158 Yellow CTRL+7
$9F 159 Cyan CTRL+8

Character Set Modes

Toggle with $D018 bit 1:

; Uppercase + Graphics (default)
    lda $d018
    and #%11111101      ; Clear bit 1
    sta $d018

; Lowercase + Uppercase
    lda $d018
    ora #%00000010      ; Set bit 1
    sta $d018

BASIC:

POKE 53272,21 : REM UPPERCASE+GRAPHICS
POKE 53272,23 : REM LOWERCASE+UPPERCASE

Graphics Characters Map

Common drawing characters (screen codes):

Corners:           Lines:              Blocks:
┌ = $70 (p)       ─ = $40 (@)        █ = $A0 (space+RVS)
┐ = $6E (n)       │ = $5D (])        ▄ = $A2 
└ = $6D (m)       ═ = $40            ▀ = $A3
┘ = $7D (})       ║ = $5D            ░ = $65
├ = $6B (k)                          ▒ = $66
┤ = $73 (s)       Diagonals:         ▓ = $67
┬ = $72 (r)       ╱ = $6E            
┴ = $71 (q)       ╲ = $6D            Cards:
┼ = $5B ([)       ╳ = $56 (v)        ♠ = $41 (a)
                                      ♥ = $53 (s)
Circles:                              ♦ = $5A (z)
○ = $57 (w)                          ♣ = $58 (x)
● = $51 (q)

PETSCII Art Tips

Using PRINT in Assembly:

; Print PETSCII string using KERNAL
    ldx #<message
    ldy #>message
    jsr $ab1e           ; Print string (null-terminated)
    rts

message:
    .byte $93           ; Clear screen
    .byte $1e           ; Green
    .text "HELLO"
    .byte $0d           ; Return
    .byte $9e           ; Yellow
    .text "WORLD"
    .byte 0             ; Null terminator

Resources

Resource URL
Style64 PETSCII Reference style64.org/petscii/
Ultimate C64 Charset pagetable.com/c64ref/charset/
sta.c64.org PETSCII sta.c64.org/cbm64pet.html
PETSCII Editor petscii.krissz.hu
Playscii jp.itch.io/playscii

66. Code Optimization Techniques

Cycle Counting Basics

PAL C64: 985,248 cycles/second, 63 cycles/line, 312 lines/frame NTSC C64: 1,022,727 cycles/second, 65 cycles/line, 263 lines/frame

Per Frame Budget: - PAL: ~19,656 cycles (312 × 63) - NTSC: ~17,095 cycles (263 × 65) - Minus bad lines: ~4,000 cycles less

Instruction Cycle Quick Reference

Cycles Instructions
2 LDA/LDX/LDY #imm, TAX, TAY, TXA, TYA, INX, INY, DEX, DEY, NOP
3 LDA/STA zp, ADC/SBC zp, AND/ORA/EOR zp, INC/DEC zp
4 LDA/STA abs, LDA zp,X, LDA abs,X/Y (no page cross)
5 LDA (zp),Y (no page cross), STA (zp),Y, INC/DEC abs
6 LDA (zp,X), JSR, RTS
7 STA abs,X, STA abs,Y, INC abs,X

Self-Modifying Code (SMC)

Why Use SMC: - Eliminate indirect addressing overhead - Remove conditional branches - Store variables in instruction operands

Example: Fast Table Lookup

; Without SMC (8 cycles minimum)
    lda pointer
    sta $fb
    lda pointer+1
    sta $fc
    ldy #0
    lda ($fb),y         ; 5 cycles

; With SMC (4 cycles after setup)
    lda table_address   ; 4 cycles
table_address = *+1
    .word $0000         ; Modified at runtime

; Setup (do once):
    lda #<actual_table
    sta table_address
    lda #>actual_table
    sta table_address+1

Double-Buffer Swap with SMC:

; Toggle between two addresses using EOR
flip_buffers:
    lda load_addr+2     ; Get current high byte
    eor #$40^$80        ; XOR flips between $40 and $80
    sta load_addr+2
    sta store_addr+2
    rts

load_addr:
    lda $4000,x
store_addr:
    sta $8000,x

Disable Code with BIT:

; Change JSR to BIT to skip a call
; JSR = $20, BIT abs = $2C

enable_feature:
    lda #$20            ; JSR opcode
    sta feature_call
    rts
    
disable_feature:
    lda #$2c            ; BIT opcode
    sta feature_call
    rts

main_loop:
feature_call:
    jsr do_feature      ; Becomes "BIT do_feature" when disabled

Loop Optimization

Unrolling:

; Slow: loop overhead (7 cycles/iteration just for loop)
    ldx #8
loop:
    lda source,x        ; 4
    sta dest,x          ; 5
    dex                 ; 2
    bne loop            ; 3 (2 when not taken)
    ; = 14 cycles × 8 = 112 cycles

; Fast: unrolled (9 cycles/copy)
    lda source+0
    sta dest+0
    lda source+1
    sta dest+1
    ; ... repeat 8 times
    ; = 72 cycles (36% faster)

Page-Aligned Tables:

; Slow: 16-bit index calculation
    lda index
    clc
    adc #<table
    sta ptr
    lda #>table
    adc #0
    sta ptr+1

; Fast: Page-aligned (table at $xx00)
    ldx index
    lda table,x         ; Just 4 cycles!

    .align 256
table:
    ; 256-byte lookup table

Counting Down vs Up:

; Count up needs comparison (extra 2 cycles)
    ldx #0
loop:
    ; ... work ...
    inx
    cpx #40             ; Extra comparison
    bne loop

; Count down - comparison is free
    ldx #39
loop:
    ; ... work ...
    dex
    bpl loop            ; N flag set when X goes negative

Memory Access Patterns

Zero Page is Faster:

; Zero page: 3 cycles
    lda $fb

; Absolute: 4 cycles  
    lda $c000

Avoid Page Crosses:

; Page cross adds 1 cycle
    lda $40ff,x         ; X=1 → reads $4100, +1 cycle

; Keep tables page-aligned or within page
    .align 256
fast_table:
    .fill 256, i

Multiplication Tricks

Powers of 2 (Shifts):

; A × 2
    asl a               ; 2 cycles

; A × 4
    asl a
    asl a               ; 4 cycles

; A × 8
    asl a
    asl a
    asl a               ; 6 cycles

Common Multiplications:

; A × 3 = A × 2 + A
    sta temp
    asl a
    clc
    adc temp            ; 9 cycles

; A × 5 = A × 4 + A
    sta temp
    asl a
    asl a
    clc
    adc temp            ; 11 cycles

; A × 10 = A × 8 + A × 2
    asl a               ; ×2
    sta temp
    asl a               ; ×4
    asl a               ; ×8
    clc
    adc temp            ; 13 cycles

; A × 40 (common for screen row calculation)
    ; Use lookup table instead!
    tax
    lda row_lo,x
    sta ptr
    lda row_hi,x
    sta ptr+1

Branch Optimization

Avoid Branches in Tight Loops:

; Slow: Branch based on condition
    lda flag
    beq skip
    inc counter
skip:

; Fast: Use arithmetic
    lda flag            ; 0 or 1
    clc
    adc counter
    sta counter

Likely Branch First:

; If condition is usually true, put common case first
    lda value
    cmp #threshold
    bcc common_case     ; 3 cycles when taken
    ; rare case here
common_case:

Clock Slide (Precise Delays)

For raster effects needing exact cycle delays:

; Delay 2-9 cycles based on X (entry at different points)
; Uses the fact that each CMP $xx takes 3 cycles
clockslide:
    cmp $c9             ; 3 cycles (eaten)
    cmp $c9             ; 3 cycles
    cmp $c9             ; 3 cycles
    cmp $ea             ; 3 cycles (CMP $EA = NOP pattern)
    ; Jump into this based on X register
    ; X=0: 12 cycles, X=3: 9 cycles, etc.

Resources

Resource URL
6502.org Tutorials 6502.org/tutorials/
Codebase64 Optimizations codebase64.org/doku.php?id=base:6502_optimizations
Wilson Minesco SMC wilsonminesco.com/SelfModCode/
Pagetable Clock Slide pagetable.com/?p=669
Bumbershoot Grimoire bumbershootsoft.wordpress.com/2021/01/02/a-grimoire-of-8-bit-implementation-patterns/

68. IRQ and NMI Deep Dive

Understanding interrupts is essential for music playback, raster effects, and responsive input handling.

Interrupt Types

Type Vector (RAM) Vector (ROM) Maskable Source
IRQ $0314-$0315 FFFE−FFFF Yes (SEI) CIA, VIC-II
NMI $0318-$0319 FFFA−FFFB No* RESTORE, CIA2
BRK $0316-$0317 (via IRQ) N/A Software
RESET - FFFC−FFFD No Hardware

*NMI can be “disabled” by leaving CIA2 timer running with NMI already pending

IRQ Sources

Source Enable Acknowledge Common Use
CIA1 Timer A $DC0D bit 0 Read $DC0D Music timing
CIA1 Timer B $DC0D bit 1 Read $DC0D General timing
VIC-II Raster $D01A bit 0 Write $D019 Screen splits
VIC-II Sprite $D01A bit 1 Write $D019 Collision
VIC-II Lightpen $D01A bit 3 Write $D019 Input

Standard IRQ Flow (ROM Enabled)

1. IRQ signal arrives
2. CPU finishes current instruction
3. CPU pushes PC (high, low) and Status to stack
4. CPU sets I flag (disable further IRQ)
5. CPU jumps through $FFFE/$FFFF → $FF48 (Kernal)
6. Kernal saves A, X, Y to stack
7. Kernal checks BRK flag
8. Kernal jumps through $0314/$0315 (your handler)
9. Your code runs
10. Your code ends with JMP $EA31 (or JMP $EA81)
11. Kernal restores A, X, Y from stack
12. RTI restores PC and Status (including I flag)

Setting Up a Raster IRQ (Kernal Method)

; Use $0314/$0315 - Kernal handles register saving
setup_irq:
    sei                     ; Disable interrupts
    
    lda #$7f
    sta $dc0d               ; Disable CIA1 interrupts
    sta $dd0d               ; Disable CIA2 interrupts
    lda $dc0d               ; Acknowledge pending CIA1
    lda $dd0d               ; Acknowledge pending CIA2
    
    lda #<my_irq            ; Set IRQ vector
    sta $0314
    lda #>my_irq
    sta $0315
    
    lda #$00                ; Raster line (low 8 bits)
    sta $d012
    lda $d011
    and #$7f                ; Clear bit 7 (raster line bit 8)
    sta $d011
    
    lda #$01                ; Enable raster IRQ
    sta $d01a
    
    cli                     ; Enable interrupts
    rts

my_irq:
    ; Kernal already saved A, X, Y
    inc $d020               ; Do something visible
    
    asl $d019               ; Acknowledge VIC IRQ
    jmp $ea31               ; Return via Kernal (updates jiffy clock, keyboard)
    ; Or: jmp $ea81         ; Skip jiffy/keyboard, just restore regs

Setting Up a Raster IRQ (ROM-less Method)

; Use $FFFE/$FFFF directly - faster, but YOU save registers
setup_irq_direct:
    sei
    
    lda #$35                ; Bank out BASIC and Kernal
    sta $01
    
    lda #$7f
    sta $dc0d
    sta $dd0d
    lda $dc0d
    lda $dd0d
    
    lda #<my_irq_direct
    sta $fffe
    lda #>my_irq_direct
    sta $ffff
    
    ; Also set NMI vector to safe RTI!
    lda #<nmi_handler
    sta $fffa
    lda #>nmi_handler
    sta $fffb
    
    lda #$00
    sta $d012
    lda $d011
    and #$7f
    sta $d011
    
    lda #$01
    sta $d01a
    
    cli
    rts

my_irq_direct:
    pha                     ; Save A
    txa
    pha                     ; Save X
    tya
    pha                     ; Save Y
    
    inc $d020               ; Your code here
    
    asl $d019               ; Acknowledge IRQ
    
    pla
    tay                     ; Restore Y
    pla
    tax                     ; Restore X
    pla                     ; Restore A
    rti                     ; Return from interrupt

nmi_handler:
    rti                     ; Just return (prevents RESTORE crash)

Multiple Raster Splits

irq1:
    ; At raster line $30
    lda #$00
    sta $d020               ; Black border
    sta $d021               ; Black background
    
    lda #<irq2              ; Chain to next IRQ
    sta $0314
    lda #>irq2
    sta $0315
    lda #$80                ; Next IRQ at line $80
    sta $d012
    
    asl $d019
    jmp $ea81

irq2:
    ; At raster line $80
    lda #$02
    sta $d020               ; Red border
    lda #$05
    sta $d021               ; Green background
    
    lda #<irq1              ; Chain back to first IRQ
    sta $0314
    lda #>irq1
    sta $0315
    lda #$30                ; Next IRQ at line $30
    sta $d012
    
    asl $d019
    jmp $ea31               ; Include keyboard scan

CIA Timer IRQ (for Music)

; Set up 50Hz timer for music (PAL)
setup_music_irq:
    sei
    
    lda #$7f
    sta $dc0d               ; Disable all CIA1 interrupts first
    lda $dc0d               ; Acknowledge pending
    
    ; PAL: 985248 Hz / 50 Hz = 19705 cycles = $4CF9
    lda #$f9
    sta $dc04               ; Timer A low
    lda #$4c
    sta $dc05               ; Timer A high
    
    lda #<music_irq
    sta $0314
    lda #>music_irq
    sta $0315
    
    lda #$81                ; Enable Timer A IRQ
    sta $dc0d
    
    lda #$11                ; Start Timer A, continuous
    sta $dc0e
    
    cli
    rts

music_irq:
    jsr $1003               ; Call music player
    
    lda $dc0d               ; Acknowledge CIA1 interrupt
    jmp $ea31

NMI Handling

; NMI via RESTORE key or CIA2 Timer
setup_nmi:
    ; NMI vector (with Kernal)
    lda #<my_nmi
    sta $0318
    lda #>my_nmi
    sta $0319
    
    ; Enable CIA2 Timer A NMI
    lda #$81
    sta $dd0d
    rts

my_nmi:
    pha
    ; Handle NMI (pause game, etc.)
    lda $dd0d               ; Acknowledge CIA2
    pla
    rti

Stable Raster (Double IRQ)

For cycle-exact timing, use the double-IRQ technique:

; First IRQ - approximate timing
irq1:
    pha
    txa
    pha
    tya
    pha
    
    ; Set up second IRQ 1 line later
    inc $d012
    lda #<irq2
    sta $fffe               ; Direct vector (ROM out)
    lda #>irq2
    sta $ffff
    asl $d019
    
    tsx                     ; Save stack pointer
    cli                     ; Allow next IRQ
    nop                     ; Padding
    nop
    nop
    ; ... more nops ...
    
irq2:
    ; Now we have stable timing (+/- 1 cycle)
    txs                     ; Restore stack (cancel pending IRQ stuff)
    
    ; Exactly timed code here
    ldx #8
-   dex
    bne -
    
    ; Your stable-timed effect
    inc $d020
    nop
    dec $d020
    
    ; Restore and exit
    lda #<irq1
    sta $fffe
    lda #>irq1
    sta $ffff
    ; ... restore regs and RTI

Resources

Resource URL
Codebase64 Raster IRQ codebase64.org/doku.php?id=base:introduction_to_raster_irqs
C64 Wiki Interrupt c64-wiki.com/wiki/Interrupt
C64 Wiki Raster IRQ c64-wiki.com/wiki/Raster_interrupt
Stable Raster codebase64.org/doku.php?id=base:stable_raster_routine

78. Common 6502 Algorithms

The 6502 processor lacks multiply, divide, and other advanced operations. This section provides optimized routines for essential mathematical and utility operations.

16-Bit Arithmetic

16-Bit Addition:

; Add two 16-bit numbers
; Input: NUM1 ($10-$11), NUM2 ($12-$13)
; Output: RESULT ($14-$15)

ADD16:  CLC             ; Clear carry
        LDA NUM1        ; Add low bytes
        ADC NUM2
        STA RESULT
        LDA NUM1+1      ; Add high bytes with carry
        ADC NUM2+1
        STA RESULT+1
        RTS

16-Bit Subtraction:

; Subtract NUM2 from NUM1
; Input: NUM1 ($10-$11), NUM2 ($12-$13)
; Output: RESULT ($14-$15)

SUB16:  SEC             ; Set carry (no borrow)
        LDA NUM1        ; Subtract low bytes
        SBC NUM2
        STA RESULT
        LDA NUM1+1      ; Subtract high bytes with borrow
        SBC NUM2+1
        STA RESULT+1
        RTS

16-Bit Increment:

; Increment 16-bit value at ADDR
INC16:  INC ADDR        ; Increment low byte
        BNE DONE        ; If not zero, done
        INC ADDR+1      ; Increment high byte
DONE:   RTS

16-Bit Decrement:

; Decrement 16-bit value at ADDR
DEC16:  LDA ADDR        ; Load low byte
        BNE SKIP        ; If not zero, just decrement
        DEC ADDR+1      ; Decrement high byte first
SKIP:   DEC ADDR        ; Decrement low byte
        RTS

16-Bit Compare:

; Compare NUM1 to NUM2 (16-bit unsigned)
; Returns: C=1 if NUM1 >= NUM2, C=0 if NUM1 < NUM2
;          Z=1 if equal

CMP16:  LDA NUM1+1      ; Compare high bytes first
        CMP NUM2+1
        BNE DONE        ; If not equal, done
        LDA NUM1        ; Compare low bytes
        CMP NUM2
DONE:   RTS

8x8 Bit Multiplication

Basic Shift-and-Add (Compact):

; Multiply A × Y = 16-bit result
; Output: PRODUCT ($10-$11)
; Destroys: A, X, Y

MULT8:  LDX #$00        ; Clear high byte
        STX PRODUCT+1
        STX PRODUCT
        LDX #8          ; 8 bits to process
        
MLOOP:  LSR             ; Shift multiplier right
        BCC NOADD       ; If bit was 0, skip add
        CLC
        TYA             ; Add multiplicand to result
        ADC PRODUCT
        STA PRODUCT
        BCC NOADD
        INC PRODUCT+1
        
NOADD:  ASL PRODUCT     ; Shift result left
        ROL PRODUCT+1
        DEX
        BNE MLOOP
        RTS

Optimized 8x8 Multiply (Faster):

; Multiply NUMA × NUMB = 16-bit result
; Output: PRODUCT ($10-$11)

MULT8F: LDA #$00
        STA PRODUCT+1
        LDX #8          ; 8 iterations
        LSR NUMA        ; Get first bit
        
MLOOP:  BCC NOADD
        CLC
        ADC NUMB        ; Add multiplicand
NOADD:  ROR             ; Shift result right
        ROR PRODUCT+1
        LSR NUMA        ; Next bit of multiplier
        DEX
        BNE MLOOP
        STA PRODUCT
        RTS

Fast Multiply Using Tables

The fastest multiplication uses the identity: A×B = ((A+B)²-(A-B)²)/4

This requires 512-byte square tables but achieves ~70 cycles vs ~150 for shift-and-add.

; Fast 8×8 multiply using square tables
; Input: A = multiplier, X = multiplicand
; Output: PRODUCT ($10-$11)
; Requires: SQLO/SQHI tables at page boundary

MULTFAST:
        STA SM1+1       ; Self-modify for (A+B)
        STA SM3+1
        EOR #$FF        ; Calculate -A
        STA SM2+1       ; Self-modify for (B-A)
        STA SM4+1
        
        CLC
SM1:    LDA SQLO,X      ; SQLO[A+B]
        SEC
SM2:    SBC SQLO,X      ; - SQLO[B-A]
        STA PRODUCT
        
SM3:    LDA SQHI,X      ; SQHI[A+B]
SM4:    SBC SQHI,X      ; - SQHI[B-A]
        STA PRODUCT+1
        RTS

; Generate square tables (do once at startup)
MKTABLES:
        LDX #$00
        TXA
        LDY #$00
TLOOP:  STA SQLO,X
        TYA
        STA SQHI,X
        INX
        BEQ TDONE
        
        CLC             ; Add X to get next square
        ADC #1          ; (n+1)² = n² + 2n + 1
        STA SQLO,X
        TYA
        ADC #0
        STA SQHI,X
        INX
        BEQ TDONE
        
        CLC
        LDA SQLO-1,X
        ADC #1
        BCC TSKIP
        INY
TSKIP:  JMP TLOOP
TDONE:  RTS

; Tables (512 bytes total, must be page-aligned)
SQLO:   .res 256        ; Low bytes of n²/4
SQHI:   .res 256        ; High bytes of n²/4

16x16 Bit Multiplication

; 16×16 multiply = 32-bit result
; Input: NUMA ($10-$11), NUMB ($12-$13)
; Output: PRODUCT ($14-$17)

MULT16: LDA #$00        ; Clear upper 16 bits of result
        STA PRODUCT+2
        STA PRODUCT+3
        LDX #16         ; 16 bits
        
MLOOP:  LSR NUMA+1      ; Shift multiplier right
        ROR NUMA
        BCC NOADD
        
        CLC             ; Add multiplicand to high word
        LDA PRODUCT+2
        ADC NUMB
        STA PRODUCT+2
        LDA PRODUCT+3
        ADC NUMB+1
        STA PRODUCT+3
        
NOADD:  ROR PRODUCT+3   ; Shift entire result right
        ROR PRODUCT+2
        ROR PRODUCT+1
        ROR PRODUCT
        DEX
        BNE MLOOP
        RTS

8-Bit Division

8÷8 Division with Remainder:

; Divide DIVIDEND by DIVISOR
; Output: A = quotient, X = remainder

DIV8:   LDX #0          ; Clear remainder
        LDA DIVIDEND
        LDY #8          ; 8 bits
        
DLOOP:  ASL             ; Shift dividend left
        TXA
        ROL             ; Shift into remainder
        TAX
        
        CPX DIVISOR     ; Can we subtract?
        BCC DSKIP
        SBC DIVISOR     ; Yes, subtract
        TAX
        INC DIVIDEND    ; Set result bit
        
DSKIP:  LDA DIVIDEND
        DEY
        BNE DLOOP
        RTS             ; A = quotient, X = remainder

16-Bit Division

; 16÷16 division
; Input: DIVIDEND ($10-$11), DIVISOR ($12-$13)
; Output: QUOTIENT ($14-$15), REMAINDER ($16-$17)

DIV16:  LDA #0          ; Clear remainder
        STA REMAIN
        STA REMAIN+1
        LDX #16         ; 16 bits
        
DLOOP:  ASL DIVIDEND    ; Shift dividend left
        ROL DIVIDEND+1
        ROL REMAIN      ; Shift bit into remainder
        ROL REMAIN+1
        
        LDA REMAIN      ; Compare remainder to divisor
        SEC
        SBC DIVISOR
        TAY
        LDA REMAIN+1
        SBC DIVISOR+1
        BCC DSKIP       ; Remainder < divisor
        
        STA REMAIN+1    ; Store new remainder
        STY REMAIN
        INC DIVIDEND    ; Set quotient bit
        
DSKIP:  DEX
        BNE DLOOP
        
        ; Move results
        LDA DIVIDEND
        STA QUOTIENT
        LDA DIVIDEND+1
        STA QUOTIENT+1
        RTS

Random Number Generators

16-Bit Galois LFSR (Fast, Good Quality):

; Returns random byte in A
; Requires: SEED ($FE-$FF) initialized non-zero

RANDOM: LDY #8          ; 8 iterations for 8 bits
        LDA SEED
        
RLOOP:  ASL             ; Shift register left
        ROL SEED+1
        BCC RNOXOR
        EOR #$39        ; XOR feedback polynomial
RNOXOR: DEY
        BNE RLOOP
        STA SEED
        RTS

Simple 8-Bit LFSR (Compact):

; Fast 8-bit random, period 255
; Requires: SEED ($FE) initialized 1-255

RAND8:  LDA SEED
        ASL
        BCC RSKIP
        EOR #$1D        ; Feedback polynomial
RSKIP:  STA SEED
        RTS

Using SID Noise (True-ish Random):

; Get random from SID noise generator
; More random but requires SID setup

INITRND:
        LDA #$FF        ; Maximum frequency
        STA $D40E       ; Voice 3 freq low
        STA $D40F       ; Voice 3 freq high
        LDA #$80        ; Noise waveform
        STA $D412       ; Voice 3 control
        RTS

GETRND: LDA $D41B       ; Read oscillator 3
        RTS

Combined LFSR + Counter (Better Distribution):

; Combines LFSR with counter for better results
SEED:   .byte $01, $00
COUNT:  .byte $00

RANDX:  INC COUNT       ; Increment counter
        LDA SEED
        ASL
        ROL SEED+1
        BCC RX1
        EOR #$39
RX1:    STA SEED
        EOR COUNT       ; Mix with counter
        RTS

Square Root (8-Bit)

; Integer square root of 8-bit value
; Input: A = value (0-255)
; Output: A = sqrt (0-15)

SQRT8:  LDY #$00        ; Result
        LDX #$01        ; Odd number sequence
        
SQLOOP: STA TEMP
        SEC
        TYA
        SBC TEMP
        BCS SQDONE      ; If result >= input, done
        
        TYA             ; Add next odd number
        CLC
        ADC SROOT
        TAY
        INX
        INX             ; Next odd number
        STX SROOT
        JMP SQLOOP
        
SQDONE: TYA             ; Count iterations = sqrt
        RTS

SROOT:  .byte $01

Sine Table Lookup

Pre-calculated tables are fastest for trigonometry:

; Sine lookup, 256 entries for 0-360 degrees
; Values 0-255 represent -1.0 to +1.0 (128 = 0)

GETSIN: TAX             ; Angle 0-255 in A
        LDA SINTAB,X    ; Look up sine
        RTS

; Cosine is sine + 64 (90 degrees)
GETCOS: CLC
        ADC #64
        TAX
        LDA SINTAB,X
        RTS

; Generate sine table (or include pre-built)
SINTAB: .byte 128,131,134,137,140,143,146,149
        .byte 152,155,158,162,165,167,170,173
        .byte 176,179,182,185,188,190,193,196
        .byte 198,201,203,206,208,211,213,215
        .byte 218,220,222,224,226,228,230,232
        .byte 234,235,237,238,240,241,243,244
        .byte 245,246,248,249,250,250,251,252
        .byte 253,253,254,254,254,255,255,255
        .byte 255,255,255,255,254,254,254,253
        .byte 253,252,251,250,250,249,248,246
        .byte 245,244,243,241,240,238,237,235
        .byte 234,232,230,228,226,224,222,220
        .byte 218,215,213,211,208,206,203,201
        .byte 198,196,193,190,188,185,182,179
        .byte 176,173,170,167,165,162,158,155
        .byte 152,149,146,143,140,137,134,131
        .byte 128,124,121,118,115,112,109,106
        .byte 103,100,97,93,90,88,85,82
        .byte 79,76,73,70,67,65,62,59
        .byte 57,54,52,49,47,44,42,40
        .byte 37,35,33,31,29,27,25,23
        .byte 21,20,18,17,15,14,12,11
        .byte 10,9,7,6,5,5,4,3
        .byte 2,2,1,1,1,0,0,0
        .byte 0,0,0,0,1,1,1,2
        .byte 2,3,4,5,5,6,7,9
        .byte 10,11,12,14,15,17,18,20
        .byte 21,23,25,27,29,31,33,35
        .byte 37,40,42,44,47,49,52,54
        .byte 57,59,62,65,67,70,73,76
        .byte 79,82,85,88,90,93,97,100
        .byte 103,106,109,112,115,118,121,124

Memory Operations

Fast Memory Fill:

; Fill memory with value in A
; X = number of pages, DEST = start address

MEMFILL:
        LDY #0
FLOOP:  STA (DEST),Y
        INY
        BNE FLOOP
        INC DEST+1
        DEX
        BNE FLOOP
        RTS

Fast Memory Copy:

; Copy X pages from SRC to DEST

MEMCOPY:
        LDY #0
CLOOP:  LDA (SRC),Y
        STA (DEST),Y
        INY
        BNE CLOOP
        INC SRC+1
        INC DEST+1
        DEX
        BNE CLOOP
        RTS

Self-Modifying Fast Copy (Unrolled):

; Ultra-fast page copy (self-modifying)
; Set source/dest high bytes before calling

FASTCOPY:
        LDX #0
FC1:    LDA $FF00,X     ; Source (modify high byte)
FC2:    STA $FF00,X     ; Dest (modify high byte)
        INX
        BNE FC1
        RTS

Sorting (8-Bit Bubble Sort)

; Sort array of bytes in ascending order
; Input: ARRAY = address, COUNT = number of elements

SORT:   LDX COUNT
        DEX             ; N-1 comparisons
        BEQ SORTED
        
PASS:   LDY #0
        STX TEMP        ; Save pass counter
        
SLOOP:  LDA ARRAY,Y
        CMP ARRAY+1,Y
        BCC SNEXT       ; Already in order
        BEQ SNEXT
        
        ; Swap
        PHA
        LDA ARRAY+1,Y
        STA ARRAY,Y
        PLA
        STA ARRAY+1,Y
        
SNEXT:  INY
        CPY TEMP
        BNE SLOOP
        
        DEX
        BNE PASS
SORTED: RTS

Databases

Name Description Link

C64-Wiki.de German C64 encyclopedia https://www.c64-wiki.de/
Codebase64 C64 programming wiki with code examples https://codebase64.org/
6502.org General 6502 community and resources http://www.6502.org/
Forum64.de German forum for C64/Amiga/VIC20 with 96,000+ topics, 1.5M+ posts https://www.forum64.de/
GameBase64 Comprehensive game database with 30,000+ entries, downloadable offline database https://gb64.com/
Ready64 Italian C64 community https://ready64.org/
THEC64 Community Community for THEC64 Mini/Maxi users https://thec64community.online/

API & Programmatic Data Access

CSDb RSS Feeds

CSDb provides RSS 2.0 feeds for tracking scene activity. Available at https://csdb.dk/rss/

Feed URL Description
Latest Releases https://csdb.dk/rss/latestreleases.php New C64 releases
Latest Forum Posts https://csdb.dk/rss/latestforumposts.php Forum activity
Group Releases https://csdb.dk/rss/groupreleases.php?id=<group_id> Releases by specific group
Latest Additions https://csdb.dk/rss/latestadditions.php?type=release New database entries
Scene News https://csdb.dk/rss/scenenews.php Scener/group membership changes
User Comments https://csdb.dk/rss/latestcomments.php Comments with optional filters: ?rid=, ?gid=, ?eid=, ?bid=, ?sid=
Upcoming Events https://csdb.dk/rss/upcomingevents.php Scene parties and events

Lemon64

Lemon64 does not currently offer a public API or database dump. The games database is web-only. For programmatic access to C64 game data, consider using:

  • GameBase64 - Downloadable Microsoft Access (.mdb) database with 30,000+ games
  • HVSC - Downloadable SID music collection with metadata

GameBase64 Database

GameBase64 provides a downloadable offline database:

  • Format: Microsoft Access (.mdb) database file
  • Content: 30,000+ games with metadata, screenshots, documentation
  • Tools:
    • GB2SD - Extract games to SD card with proper naming
    • jGameBase - Java frontend for GameBase databases
    • Various Python/VB scripts for database queries

Forum64.de

Forum64.de is a WoltLab Suite forum with standard RSS feed support. No formal API available.

HVSC (High Voltage SID Collection)

HVSC provides downloadable data:

  • Complete SID collection with STIL (SID Tune Information List)
  • Songlengths database
  • Composer/release metadata in text format
  • Updated quarterly

Notable Video Series & Playlists

8-Bit Show And Tell Highlights:

  • BASIC error messages explained
  • Rarely used C64 BASIC features
  • SuperCPU exploration
  • Hidden programs on vinyl records
  • AI-generated fake programming books exposé

64 Bites Curriculum:

  • Season 1: BASIC fundamentals, POKE, PEEK, graphics
  • Later seasons: 6502 assembly introduction
  • 104+ episodes covering beginner to intermediate

Shallan64 Assembly Series:

  • Structured assembly programming course
  • Game development tutorials
  • VIC-II and SID programming

Written Tutorial Sites (with video links)

Site Content Link
Dustlayer VIC-II graphics tutorials, development environment setup https://dustlayer.com/
Retro64 Blog Sprite tutorials, BASIC programming http://retro64.altervista.org/blog/
Codebase64 Wiki with code examples and tutorials https://codebase64.org/
Richard/TND “Assemble It” C64Studio tutorials, game programming https://tnd64.unikat.sk/

Learning Path Recommendations

Complete Beginners:

  1. 64 Bites Season 1 (BASIC fundamentals)
  2. 8-Bit Show And Tell BASIC videos
  3. Dustlayer VIC-II tutorials

Moving to Assembly:

  1. Shallan64 assembly series
  2. Board-B tutorials
  3. Richard/TND “Assemble It” written guides
  4. 8-Bit Show And Tell assembly deep-dives

Hardware Understanding:

  1. The 8-Bit Guy hardware videos
  2. Adrian’s Digital Basement repairs
  3. 8-Bit Show And Tell hardware episodes

38. Print Resources & Books

Essential books, magazines, and printed documentation for C64 development.

Classic Programming Books

Title Author Focus Availability
C64 Programmer’s Reference Guide Commodore Official reference - BASIC, ML, hardware, memory maps Archive.org, Project64
Machine Language for the Commodore 64 Jim Butterfield Best intro to 6502 assembly; legendary author Archive.org, PDF
Mapping the Commodore 64 Sheldon Leemon Complete memory map with explanations Archive.org, Bombjack
Anatomy of the Commodore 64 Abacus Deep hardware internals Archive.org
Anatomy of the 1541 Abacus Disk drive internals and programming Archive.org
Inside Commodore DOS Datamost 1541 DOS internals, file systems Archive.org
Machine Language for Beginners Richard Mansfield General 6502 introduction Compute! archives
The Commodore 64/128 Assembly Language Programming Mark Andrews Comprehensive ML guide Bombjack PDF
Commodore Innerspace Anthology Transactor Technical reference compilation Archive.org

Modern Books (Still in Print)

Title Author Description Link
Retro Game Dev: C64 Edition Derek Morris Modern game development with CBM Prg Studio; builds two complete games Amazon, retrogamedev.com
Start Here: Fundamentals of C64 Programming (C64 Workshop) BASIC and assembly for beginners; works with THEC64 Amazon
Commodore 64 Sketch and Design Various Workbook for sprite/character/screen design Amazon
Learn Multiplatform Assembly with ChibiAkumas ChibiAkumas 6502 and multi-platform assembly chibiakumas.com

Zzap!64 UK - Game reviews, news (1985-1992); 600dpi scans https://archive.org/details/zzap-640008-hi
Commodore User UK - All 77 issues complete https://archive.org/details/commodore-computer-magazines-and-newsletters
Commodore Format UK - Complete run; game reviews Internet Archive
Your 64 / Your Commodore UK - General coverage https://archive.org/details/Your64HI013

Ahoy! US - Programming and reviews Archive.org
Commodore World Late-era technical magazine Archive.org

Document Description Link

Where to Find Books

Source Type Notes
Archive.org Free scans Most classic books available
Bombjack Free PDFs bombjack.org - Large C64 book collection
Project64 Text versions project64.c64.org - Plain text conversions
AbeBooks Physical copies Used book marketplace
eBay Physical copies Variable pricing; check condition
Amazon New books Modern titles still in print

Recommended Reading Order

For Complete Beginners: 1. C64 User’s Guide (came with computer) 2. Start Here / Retro Game Dev for modern approach 3. Machine Language for Beginners (Mansfield)

For Assembly Programming: 1. Machine Language for the Commodore 64 (Butterfield) 2. C64 Programmer’s Reference Guide 3. Mapping the Commodore 64

For Hardware Understanding: 1. Anatomy of the Commodore 64 2. VIC-II Paper (Christian Bauer) 3. Anatomy of the 1541

For Game Development: 1. Retro Game Dev: C64 Edition (Morris) 2. Compute!’s Gazette type-in programs 3. C=Hacking articles

Magazine Highlights by Topic

Topic Best Sources
Game reviews Zzap!64, Commodore Format
Type-in programs Compute!’s Gazette, Ahoy!
Technical programming The Transactor, C=Hacking
Hardware projects Transactor, Commodore World
Demo programming C=Hacking, scene diskmags

YouTube Various channels See Section 37

Community Guidelines

IRC Etiquette: - Idle is fine; channels expect lurkers - Read topic on join for rules/links - Be patient waiting for responses (different time zones) - #c-64 on IRCnet is demo scene focused; respect the culture

Discord Tips: - Most servers have channel-specific rules - Use appropriate channels (don’t post hardware questions in music channels) - Search before asking common questions - Share your projects!

Finding Current Invite Links

Discord invite links expire. To find current links: 1. Check the respective forum (Lemon64, Forum64.de) 2. Search Discord server directories (discord.me, discadia.com) 3. Ask on forums or Twitter 4. Check project GitHub pages (often have Discord links)

Scene-Specific Communities

Community Focus Where
Demo Groups Usually have private IRC channels Ask on #c-64 or CSDb
HVSC Team SID collection Check HVSC website
Preservation Game archiving Lemon64 forums
Hardware Hackers DIY projects Forum64.de, Discord

Server Description Invite Link
Everything C64 Fast-growing server for C64 users, also supports Amiga and other Commodore systems discord.gg/rH7BcFWBpW
Official Commodore 64 General C64 discussion, hardware mods, software development, welcomes C128 and Amiga fans discord.gg (search “Commodore 64”)
ViTNO (Vintage is the New Old) Retro computing community covering C64, Amiga, ZX Spectrum, Amstrad, and more vintageisthenewold.com/discord
Comm-Unity 64 Hungarian server for Commodore computers, help and discussions Search Discord
RGCD Retro gaming and C64 homebrew discussions rgcd.co.uk

Other Community Platforms

Platform Description URL
Lemon64 Forum Largest C64 forum, discussions, game database, news lemon64.com/forum

Mastodon #c64 Fediverse C64 community Various instances
Reddit r/c64 C64 subreddit with news, projects, help reddit.com/r/c64

61. Modern C64 Games (2020-2025)

The C64 homebrew scene continues to thrive with commercial-quality releases.

Notable Recent Releases

Game Year Developer Type Description
Briley Witch Chronicles 2021 Sarah Jane Avory JRPG Full-screen scrolling RPG with 16+ hours gameplay, based on novel series
Briley Witch Chronicles 2 2023 Sarah Jane Avory JRPG Sequel continuing the story, available on EasyFlash cartridge
Sam’s Journey 2017/2020 Knights of Bytes Platformer 27 levels, 3-disk commercial release, considered best C64 platformer
Lester 2023 Knifegrinder Metroidvania Space station action-adventure with rogue security drones
Nixy and the Seeds of Doom 2024 Haplo Adventure Quest to cleanse the Gaia stone, colorful platformer
Tony - Montezuma’s Gold 2024 Various Platformer Montezuma’s Revenge inspired gameplay
Sherwood 2024 GriffonSoft Action Play as Robin Hood with bow and arrow combat
Musketeer 2024 GriffonSoft Action Swashbuckling action adventure
Lost Realms of Murkasada 2023 Various Adventure Multi-episode adventure series
Planet Golf 2020 Antonio Savona Sports Physics-based golf across alien worlds
infeZtation 2024 Various Action Alien infestation themed shooter

Commercial Publishers (New Releases)

Publisher Specialty URL
Psytronik Software Physical cartridge and disk releases, professional packaging psytronik.net
Protovision High-quality C64 games and hardware protovision.games

Glossary of C64 Terms

Quick reference for terminology used throughout this document and in the C64 development community.

Hardware & Chips

Term Definition
6502 The base CPU architecture. The C64 uses the 6510 variant.
6510 C64’s CPU - a 6502 with an integrated 8-bit I/O port at $00-01formemorybanking.|| *  * 6526(CIA) *  * |ComplexInterfaceAdapter − handleskeyboard, joysticks, serialbus, timers.Twochips : CIA1(DC00) and CIA2 ($DD00).
6567 (VIC-II) Video Interface Chip for NTSC systems. Handles graphics, sprites, and generates the video signal.
6569 (VIC-II) Video Interface Chip for PAL systems. Similar to 6567 but different timing.
6581 (SID) Original Sound Interface Device chip (12V). Warmer, grittier sound with stronger filters.
8500 Later revision of 6510 CPU, functionally identical.
8580 (SID) Later SID revision (9V). Cleaner sound, weaker filters, better waveform mixing.
8701 Clock generator chip. Generates system clock and color burst signals.
PLA Programmable Logic Array - handles memory banking and chip select logic. Common failure point.
VIC Bank One of four 16KB regions the VIC-II can access ($0000, $4000, $8000, $C000).

Memory & Addressing

Term Definition
Absolute Addressing Instruction uses full 16-bit address, e.g., LDA $D020.
Bank Switching Technique to access more than 64KB by swapping memory regions.
Color RAM 1KB of 4-bit memory at D800−DBFF storing character colors.
Indexed Addressing Address modified by X or Y register, e.g., LDA $D000,X.
I/O Area Memory-mapped hardware registers at D000−DFFF.
Kernal C64’s built-in operating system ROM. Note: Commodore spelled it “Kernal” not “Kernel”.
Memory Map Layout of RAM, ROM, and I/O in the 64KB address space.
Page 256-byte memory block. Page 0 ($0000-$00FF) is “zero page”.
RAM Under ROM RAM exists at same addresses as ROM; accessible by banking out ROM.
Screen RAM 1KB default at $0400-$07FF storing character codes for the display.
Zero Page First 256 bytes ($0000-$00FF). Faster access, special addressing modes.

Graphics & Display

Term Definition
Badline Rasterline where VIC-II fetches character pointers, stealing 40-43 CPU cycles.
Bitmap Mode Graphics mode using 8000 bytes for pixel-by-pixel display (320×200 or 160×200).
Border Screen area surrounding the main display. Can be opened for effects.
Character Mode Default text mode using 8×8 character cells from a character set.
Character Set 256 8×8 pixel character definitions (2KB). Can be custom or ROM.
Color RAM Nybble memory at D800−DBFF holding per-character color data.
D011 VIC-II control register 1 (D011) − screenheight, mode, Y − scroll.|| *  * D016 *  * |VIC − IIcontrolregister2(D016) - screen width, mode, X-scroll.
D020 Border color register.
D021 Background color register.
FLD Flexible Line Distance - manipulating D011tocreateverticalgaps.|| *  * FLI *  * |FlexibleLineInterpretation−changinggraphicsparametersperlineformorecolors.||**IFLI**|InterlacedFLI − usingtwoalternatingFLIimagesforapparenthigherresolution.|| *  * Hires *  * |High−resolutionbitmapmode:320×200pixels,2colorsper8×8cell.||**Multicolor**|Graphicsmodetradinghorizontalresolutionformorecolorspercell.|| *  * PETSCII *  * |Commodore′scharacterencoding,similartoASCIIbutwithgraphicscharacters.||**Rasterline**|Singlehorizontallineofthedisplay.PALhas312lines, NTSChas263.|| *  * ScreenCodes *  * |CharactervaluesasstoredinscreenRAM(differentfromPETSCII).||**Sprite**|Hardware − supportedmovableobject.8sprites, 24 × 21pixelseach.|| *  * SpriteMultiplexing *  * |Reusingspritesloweronscreentodisplaymorethan8objects.||**VICBank**|16KBregionVIC − IIreadsgraphicsfrom.SetviaCIA2(DD00).
VSP Variable Screen Position - moving screen RAM mid-frame for effects.

Sound & SID

Term Definition
ADSR Attack-Decay-Sustain-Release envelope controlling volume over time.
Channel One of SID’s three independent voices.
Digi Digitized sample playback, typically via volume register ($D418).
Filter SID’s analog multimode filter (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass).
Hard Restart Brief gate-off before note to ensure clean ADSR restart.
HVSC High Voltage SID Collection - archive of 57,000+ SID tunes.
Pulse Width Width of pulse/square wave, creating tonal variations.
PWM Pulse Width Modulation - varying pulse width over time for animation.
Ring Modulation Multiplying two oscillators for metallic/bell-like tones.
SID Sound Interface Device - the C64’s legendary sound chip.
Sync Hard sync - resetting one oscillator’s phase from another.
Tracker Music software using pattern-based composition (GoatTracker, SID-Wizard).
Voice One of SID’s three sound generators. Each has oscillator, envelope, output.
Waveform Oscillator shape: triangle, sawtooth, pulse, noise, or combinations.

Programming & Assembly

Term Definition
Accumulator (A) Main 8-bit register for arithmetic and data operations.
Carry Flag (C) Set when arithmetic overflows 8 bits. Used in multi-byte math and branches.
Cycle One clock tick. PAL runs at 985,248 cycles/second, NTSC at 1,022,727.
IRQ Interrupt Request - maskable interrupt, commonly from raster or timers.
JSR/RTS Jump to Subroutine / Return from Subroutine - function call mechanism.
Label Named address in assembly source code.
NMI Non-Maskable Interrupt - triggered by RESTORE key or CIA2 timer.
Opcode Machine code byte representing a CPU instruction.
PRG Standard C64 program file format with 2-byte load address header.
Pseudo-op Assembler directive like .byte, .word, *= (not CPU instructions).
Raster IRQ Interrupt triggered when VIC-II reaches specific rasterline.
Self-Modifying Code Code that writes to its own instructions for speed/flexibility.
Stable Raster IRQ timing technique achieving cycle-exact synchronization with display.
Stack 256-byte area at $0100-$01FF for subroutine returns and temporary data.
Status Register (P) Flags: N(egative), V(overflow), B(reak), D(ecimal), I(nterrupt), Z(ero), C(arry).
Wedge Code patched into BASIC/Kernal vectors to add commands or intercept operations.
X Register 8-bit index register, often used for loops and indexed addressing.
Y Register 8-bit index register, often used for loops and indirect indexed addressing.

Storage & Media

Term Definition
1541 Standard C64 5.25” floppy drive (170KB per disk).
1571 C128 drive, double-sided (340KB), 1541 compatible.
1581 3.5” drive (800KB), faster but less common.
BAM Block Availability Map - tracks which disk sectors are free/used.
c1541 VICE command-line tool for creating/manipulating D64 disk images.
CRT Cartridge image file format for emulators.
D64 Disk image format - exact copy of 1541 disk (174,848 bytes).
D71 Disk image for 1571 double-sided disks.
D81 Disk image for 1581 3.5” disks.
G64 GCR-encoded disk image preserving low-level timing and protection.
GCR Group Code Recording - how data is physically encoded on disk.
IEC Serial bus protocol connecting C64 to drives and printers.
Interleave Sector ordering pattern for optimal sequential reading.
NIB Raw nibble disk image for preservation, captures protection.
PRG Program file with 2-byte load address header.
SEQ Sequential file type for data storage.
T64 Tape image format (archive of multiple files).
TAP Tape image capturing actual pulse timing from datasette.
Track/Sector Disk addressing: 35 tracks, 17-21 sectors per track on 1541.

Demoscene & Effects

Term Definition
1x1 Effects achieving apparent 1×1 pixel color resolution (normally 8×8 or 4×8).
Crack Intro Short demo shown before pirated software loads.
Demo Non-interactive audiovisual program showcasing programming skill.
Demoparty Event where demos compete, e.g., X’2024, Revision, Gubbdata.
DYCP Different Y Character Positions - smooth vertical text scrolling.
Effect Visual technique: plasma, rasterbars, rotozoomer, etc.
Intro Short demo, typically 4KB-64KB size-limited.
Kefrens Bars Rasterbar effect with overlapping colored bars.
Loader Fastloader showing graphics/music while main program loads.
Oldskool Demoscene term for vintage platforms like C64 (vs “newskool” PC demos).
Plasma Colorful animated pattern using sine calculations.
Production General term for any released demo, intro, game, or tool.
Rasterbar Colored horizontal lines changed per rasterline. Classic demo effect.
Release Published demoscene production.
Scener Active demoscene participant.
Scrolltext Horizontal scrolling message, ubiquitous in demos and intros.
Tech-tech Color-per-character effects creating apparent higher color resolution.
Trackmo Demo loading parts from disk, synchronized to music.

Tools & Workflow

Term Definition
ACME Multi-platform cross-assembler by Marco Baye.
Breakpoint Debugger halt point at specific address or condition.
cc65 C cross-compiler for 6502 systems including C64.
Cross-Assembler Assembler running on modern PC, producing C64 code.
Cruncher Compressor for C64 executables (Exomizer, ByteBoozer, Pucrunch).
CSDb C64 Scene Database - repository of demoscene releases.
Exomizer Popular executable cruncher with good ratio and small depack code.
Fastloader Replacement load routines faster than standard Kernal (Krill’s, Spindle).
KickAssembler Popular Java-based cross-assembler with powerful macro language.
Monitor Built-in debugger (VICE monitor, Final Cartridge, Action Replay).
Oscar64 Optimizing C compiler targeting C64.
PETCAT VICE tool to convert BASIC programs to/from text.
Symbol File Debug file mapping addresses to labels for debugger.
VICE Versatile Commodore Emulator - primary C64 development emulator.

Community Terms

Term Definition
BBS Bulletin Board System - dial-up community systems, still active via Telnet.
Breadbin Original C64 case design (resembles bread box).
C64C Later C64 model with flatter case and updated internals.
C64 Reloaded Modern C64 motherboard replacement by Individual Computers.
Compo Competition at a demoparty or online.
CSDb C64 Scene Database - primary release database.
Lemon64 Major C64 community forum.
Longboard Original C64 motherboard (larger size, 6581 SID).
Mini THEC64 Mini - FPGA-based C64 recreation by Retro Games Ltd.
Scene The demoscene community.
Shortboard Later C64/C64C motherboard (smaller, typically 8580 SID).
SX-64 Portable C64 with built-in monitor and 1541 drive.
THEC64 Full-size C64 recreation by Retro Games Ltd.
Ultimate 64 FPGA-based C64 replacement motherboard by Gideon’s Logic.
Ultimate II+ Cartridge with REU, ethernet, USB, SD card by Gideon’s Logic.

File Formats Quick Reference

Extension Type Description
.prg Program Executable with 2-byte load address
.sid Music SID tune file (PSID/RSID format)
.d64 Disk 1541 disk image
.g64 Disk GCR-level disk image
.t64 Tape Tape archive
.tap Tape Tape pulse-level image
.crt Cartridge Cartridge ROM image
.bin Binary Raw binary data
.seq Data Sequential data file
.asm Source Assembly source code
.bas Source BASIC source (tokenized or text)
.kla Graphics Koala Painter image
.art Graphics Art Studio image
.spr Graphics Sprite data
.chr Graphics Character set data

MJK 1551 Page ist.uwaterloo.ca/~schepers/MJK/1551.html
TCBM2SD Project github.com/ytmytm/plus4-tcbm2sd

C64 Wiki 1581 c64-wiki.com/wiki/Commodore_1581
