Lyonsden 1581 lyonsden.net/commodore-1581-disk-drive/

General Drive Resources

Resource URL
Zimmers Drive Archive zimmers.net/anonftp/pub/cbm/schematics/drives/
Secret Weapons Drives floodgap.com/retrobits/ckb/secret/periph.html
Drive ROM Dumps zimmers.net/anonftp/pub/cbm/firmware/drives/

Section 76: KERNAL Routines Reference

The KERNAL is the C64’s built-in operating system, occupying the ROM space from E000−FFFF (8KB). It provides 39 documented routines accessible through a stable jump table, enabling hardware abstraction and cross-platform compatibility with other Commodore 8-bit systems.

KERNAL Architecture

Component Address Range Description
KERNAL ROM E000−FFFF 8KB operating system
Jump Table FF81−FFF3 39 entry points (3 bytes each)
Hardware Vectors FFFA−FFFF NMI, RESET, IRQ/BRK
RAM Vectors $0314-$0333 Hookable indirect vectors

Device Numbers

Device Number Description
Keyboard 0 Default input device
Datasette 1 Tape drive
RS-232 2 User port serial
Screen 3 Default output device
Printer 4-5 Serial bus printers
Plotter 6 Plotter device
Disk Drive 8-11 Floppy drives (8 = default)
Serial Bus 4-30 All IEC serial devices

Complete Jump Table Reference (FF81−FFF3)

Initialization Routines

Address Name Description Input Output Registers
$FF81 SCINIT Initialize VIC, clear screen, set PAL/NTSC - - A, X, Y
$FF84 IOINIT Initialize CIA, SID, memory config, timers - - A, X
$FF87 RAMTAS Clear ZP, test RAM, set BASIC pointers - - A, X, Y
$FF8A RESTOR Restore default KERNAL vectors ($0314-$0333) - - -
$FF8D VECTOR Copy vectors to/from user table C=0: user→vectors, C=1: vectors→user; X/Y=pointer - A, Y

Message and Memory Routines

Address Name Description Input Output Registers
$FF90 SETMSG Set KERNAL message mode A=mode ($00=off, $40=errors, $80=control, $C0=all) - -
$FF99 MEMTOP Get/set top of memory C=0: set from X/Y, C=1: get to X/Y X/Y=address X, Y
$FF9C MEMBOT Get/set bottom of memory C=0: set from X/Y, C=1: get to X/Y X/Y=address X, Y

Keyboard and Screen Routines

Address Name Description Input Output Registers
$FF9F SCNKEY Scan keyboard matrix - Updates $00CB, $028D, keyboard buffer A, X, Y
$FFE1 STOP Check RUN/STOP key - Z=1 & C=1 if pressed A, X
$FFE4 GETIN Get character from input device - A=character (0 if buffer empty for keyboard) A, X, Y
$FFED SCREEN Get screen size - X=columns (40), Y=rows (25) X, Y
$FFF0 PLOT Get/set cursor position C=0: set X=col, Y=row; C=1: get X=column, Y=row X, Y

Serial Bus Routines (Low-Level IEC)

Address Name Description Input Output Registers
$FF93 LSTNSA Send secondary address after LISTEN A=secondary address - A
$FF96 TALKSA Send secondary address after TALK A=secondary address - A
$FFA2 SETTMO Set serial bus timeout A=timeout value - -
$FFA5 IECIN Read byte from serial bus - A=byte read A
$FFA8 IECOUT Write byte to serial bus A=byte to write - -
$FFAB UNTALK Send UNTALK to serial bus - - A
$FFAE UNLSTN Send UNLISTEN to serial bus - - A
$FFB1 LISTEN Send LISTEN to device A=device number - A
$FFB4 TALK Send TALK to device A=device number - A

File Management Routines

Address Name Description Input Output Registers
$FFB7 READST Read I/O status byte - A=status (see STATUS bits) A
$FFBA SETLFS Set logical file parameters A=logical#, X=device#, Y=secondary addr - -
$FFBD SETNAM Set filename A=length, X/Y=pointer to name - -
$FFC0 OPEN Open logical file - C=1 on error, A=error code A, X, Y
$FFC3 CLOSE Close logical file A=logical file number - A, X, Y
$FFC6 CHKIN Set input channel X=logical file number - A, X
$FFC9 CHKOUT Set output channel X=logical file number - A, X
$FFCC CLRCHN Restore default I/O - - A, X
$FFE7 CLALL Close all files, call CLRCHN - - A, X

Character I/O Routines

Address Name Description Input Output Registers
$FFCF CHRIN Read byte from input channel - A=byte read A, Y
$FFD2 CHROUT Write byte to output channel A=byte to write - -

Load/Save Routines

Address Name Description Input Output Registers
$FFD5 LOAD Load or verify file A=0:load, 1-255:verify; X/Y=address (if SA=0) C=1 on error, A=error; X/Y=end address A, X, Y
$FFD8 SAVE Save memory to file A=ZP pointer to start addr; X/Y=end+1 C=1 on error, A=error code A, X, Y

Time Routines

Address Name Description Input Output Registers
FFDB|SETTIM|Setjiffyclock(A0-$A2) A=high, X=mid, Y=low (jiffies, 1/60 sec) - -
$FFDE RDTIM Read jiffy clock - A=high, X=mid, Y=low A, X, Y
$FFEA UDTIM Update jiffy clock (call from IRQ) - - A, X

Miscellaneous

Address Name Description Input Output Registers
$FFF3 | IOBASE | Get CIA #1 base address | - | X/Y=$DC00 X, Y

RAM Vectors ($0314-$0333)

These vectors can be modified to hook KERNAL functions:

Address Name Default Description
$0314-$0315 CINV $EA31 IRQ interrupt vector
$0316-$0317 CBINV $FE66 BRK instruction vector
$0318-$0319 NMINV $FE47 NMI interrupt vector
$031A-$031B IOPEN $F34A Indirect OPEN vector
$031C-$031D ICLOSE $F291 Indirect CLOSE vector
$031E-$031F ICHKIN $F20E Indirect CHKIN vector
$0320-$0321 ICKOUT $F250 Indirect CHKOUT vector
$0322-$0323 ICLRCH $F333 Indirect CLRCHN vector
$0324-$0325 IBASIN $F157 Indirect CHRIN vector
$0326-$0327 IBSOUT $F1CA Indirect CHROUT vector
$0328-$0329 ISTOP $F6ED Indirect STOP vector
$032A-$032B IGETIN $F13E Indirect GETIN vector
$032C-$032D ICLALL $F32F Indirect CLALL vector
$032E-$032F USRCMD $FE66 Unused (monitor on later machines)
$0330-$0331 ILOAD $F4A5 Indirect LOAD vector
$0332-$0333 ISAVE $F5ED Indirect SAVE vector

Hardware Vectors (FFFA−FFFF)

Address Name Default Description
FFFA−FFFB NMI $FE43 Non-Maskable Interrupt
FFFC−FFFD RESET $FCE2 Cold start / reset
FFFE−FFFF IRQ/BRK $FF48 IRQ or BRK instruction

STATUS Byte ($90) Bit Definitions

The STATUS variable (ST in BASIC, $0090 in memory) indicates I/O operation results:

Bit Value Datasette Serial Bus RS-232
0 $01 - Timeout direction (0=read, 1=write) Parity error
1 $02 - Timeout error Framing error
2 $04 Block too short (<192 bytes) - Receive buffer overrun
3 $08 Block too long (>192 bytes) - Receive buffer empty
4 $10 Verify error Verify error CTS signal missing
5 $20 Checksum error - -
6 $40 End of file (EOF) End of file (EOF) DSR signal missing
7 $80 - Device not present BREAK detected

Common status values: - $00 = OK, no errors - $40 = End of file reached (normal completion) - $80 = Device not present - $42 = EOF + timeout (read completed)

KERNAL Error Codes

Returned in A register when carry is set after LOAD/SAVE/OPEN:

Code Hex KERNAL Message BASIC Message
1 $01 I/O ERROR #1 TOO MANY FILES
2 $02 I/O ERROR #2 FILE OPEN
3 $03 I/O ERROR #3 FILE NOT OPEN
4 $04 I/O ERROR #4 FILE NOT FOUND
5 $05 I/O ERROR #5 DEVICE NOT PRESENT
6 $06 I/O ERROR #6 NOT INPUT FILE
7 $07 I/O ERROR #7 NOT OUTPUT FILE
8 $08 I/O ERROR #8 MISSING FILENAME
9 $09 I/O ERROR #9 ILLEGAL DEVICE NUMBER

Programming Examples

Example 1: Print String to Screen

; Print "HELLO WORLD" using CHROUT
; CHROUT = $FFD2

            LDX #$00        ; String index
LOOP        LDA MESSAGE,X   ; Get character
            BEQ DONE        ; Zero = end of string
            JSR $FFD2       ; CHROUT - print character
            INX             ; Next character
            BNE LOOP        ; Continue (max 255 chars)
DONE        RTS

MESSAGE     .TEXT "HELLO WORLD"
            .BYTE $0D       ; Carriage return
            .BYTE $00       ; String terminator

Example 2: Load File to Specific Address

; Load a file to address $4000
; Uses SETNAM, SETLFS, LOAD

LOAD_ADDR   = $4000

            ; Set filename
            LDA #FNAME_END-FNAME  ; Filename length
            LDX #<FNAME           ; Filename address low
            LDY #>FNAME           ; Filename address high
            JSR $FFBD             ; SETNAM

            ; Set file parameters
            LDA #$01              ; Logical file number
            LDX $BA               ; Last used device (or use #$08)
            BNE SKIP
            LDX #$08              ; Default to device 8
SKIP        LDY #$00              ; Secondary address 0 = load to X/Y
            JSR $FFBA             ; SETLFS

            ; Load file
            LDA #$00              ; 0 = load (not verify)
            LDX #<LOAD_ADDR       ; Load address low
            LDY #>LOAD_ADDR       ; Load address high
            JSR $FFD5             ; LOAD
            BCS ERROR             ; Carry set = error
            ; X/Y now contains end address+1
            RTS

ERROR       ; A contains error code:
            ; $00 = RUN/STOP pressed
            ; $04 = File not found
            ; $05 = Device not present
            ; $1D = Load error
            RTS

FNAME       .TEXT "FILENAME"
FNAME_END

Example 3: Load File to Its Native Address

; Load file to address stored in file (first 2 bytes)
; Secondary address 1 = use file's load address

            LDA #FNAME_END-FNAME
            LDX #<FNAME
            LDY #>FNAME
            JSR $FFBD             ; SETNAM

            LDA #$01
            LDX #$08              ; Device 8
            LDY #$01              ; Secondary addr 1 = use file address
            JSR $FFBA             ; SETLFS

            LDA #$00              ; Load
            JSR $FFD5             ; LOAD
            BCS ERROR
            RTS

Example 4: Save Memory Region to Disk

; Save memory from $4000-$5FFF to disk

START_ADDR  = $4000
END_ADDR    = $6000         ; End address + 1

            ; Set filename
            LDA #FNAME_END-FNAME
            LDX #<FNAME
            LDY #>FNAME
            JSR $FFBD             ; SETNAM

            ; Set file parameters
            LDA #$01              ; Logical file number
            LDX #$08              ; Device 8
            LDY #$01              ; Secondary address 1 (write)
            JSR $FFBA             ; SETLFS

            ; Set up save parameters
            LDA #<START_ADDR
            STA $C1               ; Start address low (ZP)
            LDA #>START_ADDR
            STA $C2               ; Start address high (ZP)

            ; Save
            LDA #$C1              ; ZP pointer to start address
            LDX #<END_ADDR        ; End address low
            LDY #>END_ADDR        ; End address high
            JSR $FFD8             ; SAVE
            BCS ERROR
            RTS

ERROR       RTS

FNAME       .TEXT "SAVEDFILE"
FNAME_END

Example 5: Read Sequential File Byte-by-Byte

; Open and read a sequential file

            ; Set filename
            LDA #FNAME_END-FNAME
            LDX #<FNAME
            LDY #>FNAME
            JSR $FFBD             ; SETNAM

            ; Set file parameters
            LDA #$02              ; Logical file #2
            LDX #$08              ; Device 8
            LDY #$02              ; Secondary address 2 (data channel)
            JSR $FFBA             ; SETLFS

            ; Open file
            JSR $FFC0             ; OPEN
            BCS OPEN_ERR

            ; Set input channel
            LDX #$02              ; Logical file #2
            JSR $FFC6             ; CHKIN
            BCS READ_ERR

            ; Read loop
READ_LOOP   JSR $FFB7             ; READST - check status
            BNE EOF               ; Non-zero = EOF or error
            JSR $FFCF             ; CHRIN - get byte
            JSR $FFD2             ; CHROUT - print to screen
            JMP READ_LOOP

EOF         ; Close file
            LDA #$02
            JSR $FFC3             ; CLOSE
            JSR $FFCC             ; CLRCHN
            RTS

OPEN_ERR
READ_ERR    JSR $FFCC             ; CLRCHN
            RTS

FNAME       .TEXT "DATAFILE,S,R"
FNAME_END

Example 6: Write Sequential File

; Write data to a sequential file

            ; Set filename (with ,S,W for SEQ write)
            LDA #FNAME_END-FNAME
            LDX #<FNAME
            LDY #>FNAME
            JSR $FFBD             ; SETNAM

            ; Set file parameters
            LDA #$02              ; Logical file #2
            LDX #$08              ; Device 8
            LDY #$02              ; Secondary address
            JSR $FFBA             ; SETLFS

            ; Open file
            JSR $FFC0             ; OPEN
            BCS WRITE_ERR

            ; Set output channel
            LDX #$02
            JSR $FFC9             ; CHKOUT
            BCS WRITE_ERR

            ; Write data
            LDX #$00
WRITE_LOOP  LDA DATA,X
            BEQ WRITE_DONE
            JSR $FFD2             ; CHROUT - write byte
            INX
            BNE WRITE_LOOP

WRITE_DONE  ; Close file
            LDA #$02
            JSR $FFC3             ; CLOSE
            JSR $FFCC             ; CLRCHN
            RTS

WRITE_ERR   JSR $FFCC
            RTS

FNAME       .TEXT "OUTPUT,S,W"
FNAME_END

DATA        .TEXT "HELLO FROM ASSEMBLY!"
            .BYTE $00

Example 7: Read Disk Error Channel

; Read drive error channel (channel 15)

            ; No filename for command channel
            LDA #$00
            JSR $FFBD             ; SETNAM (empty name)

            ; Open command channel
            LDA #$0F              ; Logical file 15
            LDX #$08              ; Device 8
            LDY #$0F              ; Secondary address 15
            JSR $FFBA             ; SETLFS
            JSR $FFC0             ; OPEN
            BCS ERR_FAIL

            ; Set input
            LDX #$0F
            JSR $FFC6             ; CHKIN

            ; Read error message
ERR_LOOP    JSR $FFB7             ; READST
            BNE ERR_DONE
            JSR $FFCF             ; CHRIN
            JSR $FFD2             ; Print character
            JMP ERR_LOOP

ERR_DONE    JSR $FFCC             ; CLRCHN
            LDA #$0F
            JSR $FFC3             ; CLOSE
ERR_FAIL    RTS

Example 8: Hook IRQ Vector

; Install custom IRQ handler

            SEI                   ; Disable interrupts

            ; Save old vector
            LDA $0314
            STA OLD_IRQ
            LDA $0315
            STA OLD_IRQ+1

            ; Install new vector
            LDA #<NEW_IRQ
            STA $0314
            LDA #>NEW_IRQ
            STA $0315

            CLI                   ; Enable interrupts
            RTS

; Custom IRQ handler
NEW_IRQ     INC $D020             ; Flash border (example)
            JMP (OLD_IRQ)         ; Chain to original handler

OLD_IRQ     .WORD $0000           ; Storage for old vector

Example 9: Get Keyboard Input (Non-Blocking)

; Poll keyboard with GETIN (returns 0 if no key)

WAIT_KEY    JSR $FFE4             ; GETIN
            BEQ WAIT_KEY          ; Loop until key pressed
            ; A now contains PETSCII code
            CMP #$0D              ; Check for RETURN
            BEQ GOT_RETURN
            CMP #$51              ; Check for 'Q'
            BEQ GOT_QUIT
            ; Handle other keys...
            RTS

GOT_RETURN  ; Handle RETURN key
            RTS

GOT_QUIT    ; Handle Q key
            RTS

Example 10: Set/Read Jiffy Clock

; Set clock to 0 and read it later

            ; Set clock to 0
            LDA #$00
            TAX
            TAY
            JSR $FFDB             ; SETTIM

            ; ... do something ...

            ; Read clock
            JSR $FFDE             ; RDTIM
            ; A = high byte (hours*65536/60 roughly)
            ; X = mid byte
            ; Y = low byte (jiffies mod 256)
            ; Total jiffies = A*65536 + X*256 + Y
            RTS

Common Routine Combinations

Task Sequence
File Loading SETNAM → SETLFS → LOAD
File Saving SETNAM → SETLFS → SAVE
Sequential Read SETNAM → SETLFS → OPEN → CHKIN → [CHRIN/READST loop] → CLOSE → CLRCHN
Sequential Write SETNAM → SETLFS → OPEN → CHKOUT → [CHROUT loop] → CLOSE → CLRCHN
Direct Disk Command SETNAM (command) → SETLFS (SA=15) → OPEN → CLOSE

KERNAL Compatibility Notes

Feature VIC-20 C64 C128 Plus/4
Jump table base $FF81 $FF81 $FF81 Different
Vector base $0314 $0314 $0314 Different
NMI vector $0318 $0318 $0318 Missing
IOBASE result $9110 $DC00 $DC00 Different

Programs using only the jump table (not direct KERNAL calls) have the best compatibility across Commodore 8-bit systems.

Important Zero Page Locations Used by KERNAL

Address Name Description
$90 STATUS I/O status byte (ST)
$91 STKEY Stop key flag ($7F=pressed)
$93 VERCK Load/verify flag
$98 LDTND Number of open files
$99 DTEFLD Input device
$9A DFLTO Output device
$9D MSGFLG KERNAL message control
A0−A2 TIME Jiffy clock (24-hour, 3 bytes, big-endian)
$B7 FNLEN Current filename length
$B8 LA Current logical file
$B9 SA Current secondary address
$BA FA Current device number
BB−BC FNADR Pointer to filename
C1−C2 STAL Save start address pointer

Section 76 Resources

Resource URL
Ultimate C64 Reference (Pagetable) pagetable.com/c64ref/kernal/
sta.c64.org KERNAL Functions sta.c64.org/cbm64krnfunc.html
C64-Wiki KERNAL c64-wiki.com/wiki/Kernal
Codebase64 File Examples codebase64.org/doku.php?id=base:loading_a_file
C64 OS KERNAL Analysis c64os.com/post/c64kernalrom
cx16.dk KERNAL Routines cx16.dk/c64-kernal-routines/

77. File Format Specifications

Understanding native C64 file formats is essential for writing loaders, editors, emulators, and conversion tools. This section documents the byte-level structure of the most common formats.

PRG - Program Files

The simplest C64 file format, used for all loadable programs.

Structure:

Offset Size Description
$00-$01 2 bytes Load address (little-endian)
$02+ Variable Program data

Notes: - Load address specifies where data should be placed in C64 memory - BASIC programs typically load to $0801 - Machine code can load anywhere in RAM - The KERNAL LOAD routine reads bytes $00-$01 and uses them to set the destination address (unless secondary address is 0, which relocates to BASIC start)

Example: A program loading to $C000 with 256 bytes of code:

Byte 0: $00 (low byte of $C000)
Byte 1: $C0 (high byte of $C000)
Bytes 2-257: Program code
Total size: 258 bytes

D64 - 1541 Disk Image

The most common disk image format, representing a complete 1541 floppy disk.

File Sizes:

Tracks Sectors Size (no errors) Size (with errors)
35 683 174,848 bytes 175,531 bytes
40 768 196,608 bytes 197,376 bytes
42 802 205,312 bytes 206,114 bytes

Disk Layout:

Tracks Sectors/Track Sector Range
1-17 21 0-20
18-24 19 0-18
25-30 18 0-17
31-35 17 0-16
36-40 17 0-16 (extended)

Sector Structure (256 bytes each):

Offset Description
$00 Next track (0 = last sector in chain)
$01 Next sector (or bytes used if last sector)
02−FF Data (254 bytes)

Track 18 - Directory Track:

Sector Purpose
0 BAM (Block Availability Map)
1-18 Directory entries (8 per sector)

BAM Structure (Track 18, Sector 0):

Offset Size Description
$00-$01 2 Track/sector of first directory sector (18/1)
$02 1 DOS version (‘A’)
$03 1 Reserved ($00)
$04-$8F 140 BAM entries (4 bytes per track × 35 tracks)
$90-$9F 16 Disk name (PETSCII, padded with $A0)
A0−A1 2 $A0 padding
A2−A3 2 Disk ID
$A4 1 $A0 padding
A5−A6 2 DOS type (“2A”)
A7−AA 4 $A0 padding
AB−FF 85 Unused

Directory Entry Structure (32 bytes each):

Offset Size Description
$00-$01 2 Next track/sector (first entry only)
$02 1 File type
$03-$04 2 Track/sector of first data block
$05-$14 16 Filename (PETSCII, padded with $A0)
$15-$16 2 REL file: side-sector track/sector
$17 1 REL file: record length
$18-$1D 6 Unused
$1E-$1F 2 File size in blocks

File Type Byte ($02):

Bits Value Meaning
0-3 Type 0=DEL, 1=SEQ, 2=PRG, 3=USR, 4=REL
4 - Unused
5 - Used during SAVE @
6 Locked 1 = File is locked (>)
7 Closed 1 = File properly closed

Calculating File Offset in D64:

; Track 1-17: 21 sectors each
; Track 18-24: 19 sectors each  
; Track 25-30: 18 sectors each
; Track 31-35: 17 sectors each

sector_offset = 0
for t = 1 to track-1:
    if t <= 17: sector_offset += 21
    elif t <= 24: sector_offset += 19
    elif t <= 30: sector_offset += 18
    else: sector_offset += 17

byte_offset = (sector_offset + sector) * 256

Error Bytes (optional, appended after disk data):

Code Meaning
$01 OK
$02 Header block not found
$03 No sync sequence
$04 Data block not found
$05 Data block checksum error
$09 Header block checksum error
$0B ID mismatch

T64 - Tape Archive

Container format originally designed for the C64S emulator, storing multiple files.

Header (64 bytes):

Offset Size Description
$00-$1F 32 Signature: “C64 tape image file” or “C64S tape file”
$20-$21 2 Version ($0100 or $0101)
$22-$23 2 Maximum directory entries
$24-$25 2 Used directory entries
$26-$27 2 Reserved
$28-$3F 24 Tape name (PETSCII)

Directory Entry (32 bytes each, starting at offset $40):

Offset Size Description
$00 1 Entry type (0=free, 1=normal, 3=snapshot)
$01 1 C64 file type (PRG, SEQ, etc.)
$02-$03 2 Start address
$04-$05 2 End address
$06-$07 2 Reserved
$08-$0B 4 Offset to data in file
$0C-$0F 4 Reserved
$10-$1F 16 Filename (PETSCII)

Notes: - File data follows directory entries - End address is actually end+1 (data length = end - start) - Many T64 files have incorrect end addresses; use file offset differences

TAP - Raw Tape Image

Captures the raw timing data from a Datasette, preserving fast loaders and copy protection.

Header (20 bytes):

Offset Size Description
$00-$0B 12 Signature: “C64-TAPE-RAW”
$0C 1 TAP version (0 or 1)
$0D-$0F 3 Reserved
$10-$13 4 Data size (little-endian)

Data Format:

Each byte represents a pulse length: - Version 0: Pulse length = byte × 8 CPU cycles - Version 1: If byte = $00, next 3 bytes are 24-bit length (little-endian) - Standard data pulses: Short (~$30), Medium (~$42), Long (~$56)

Standard C64 Tape Encoding: - Bit 0: Short-Medium pulse pair - Bit 1: Medium-Short pulse pair - Byte marker: Long-Medium pulse pair

G64 - GCR Disk Image

Raw track image preserving GCR encoding, copy protection, and non-standard formats.

Header:

Offset Size Description
$00-$07 8 Signature: “GCR-1541”
$08 1 Version
$09 1 Number of tracks (usually 84 for half-tracks)
$0A-$0B 2 Maximum track size

Track Table (starting at $0C):

Offset Size Description
$0C-$15F 4×84 Track data offsets (0 = empty track)
$160-$1AB 4×84 Speed zone table

Track Data: - First 2 bytes: Actual track data length - Remaining bytes: Raw GCR-encoded track data

SID/PSID/RSID - Music Files

Contains C64 music with metadata for playback in SID players.

Header (v1: 118 bytes, v2+: 124 bytes):

Offset Size Description
$00-$03 4 Magic ID: “PSID” or “RSID”
$04-$05 2 Version (1-4, big-endian)
$06-$07 2 Data offset ($0076 v1, $007C v2+)
$08-$09 2 Load address (0 = use first 2 bytes of data)
$0A-$0B 2 Init address
$0C-$0D 2 Play address (0 = use IRQ/NMI)
$0E-$0F 2 Number of songs
$10-$11 2 Default song (1-based)
$12-$15 4 Speed flags (1 bit per song: 0=50Hz, 1=CIA)
$16-$35 32 Song name (null-terminated)
$36-$55 32 Author (null-terminated)
$56-$75 32 Copyright (null-terminated)

Version 2+ Additional Fields:

Offset Size Description
$76-$77 2 Flags (see below)
$78 1 Start page (relocatable area)
$79 1 Page length
7A|1|SecondSIDaddress(D4x0)
$7B 1 Third SID address (v3+)

Flags Field:

Bit Meaning
0 MUS format (1 = Compute!’s Sidplayer)
1 PlaySID specific / C64 BASIC (RSID)
2-3 Clock: 0=Unknown, 1=PAL, 2=NTSC, 3=Both
4-5 SID Model: 0=Unknown, 1=6581, 2=8580, 3=Both

PSID vs RSID: - PSID: Designed for emulation, may use PlaySID-specific features - RSID: Requires real C64 environment, stricter requirements: - loadAddress must be 0 (embedded in data) - playAddress must be 0 (uses IRQ) - speed must be 0

Koala (.KOA) - Multicolor Bitmap

Standard format for multicolor bitmap images from Koala Painter.

Structure (10,003 bytes):

Offset Size Description
$00-$01 2 Load address ($6000)
$02-$1F41 8,000 Bitmap data
$1F42-$2329 1,000 Screen RAM (color info)
$232A-$2711 1,000 Color RAM (color info)
$2712 1 Background color

Memory Layout When Loaded to $6000:

Address Size VIC-II Use
$6000-$7F3F 8,000 Bitmap
$7F40-$8327 1,000 Screen RAM (move to $0400 or $CC00)
$8328-$870F 1,000 Color RAM (move to $D800)
8710|1|Backgroundcolor(D021)

Pixel Encoding (Multicolor Mode):

Each byte in bitmap represents 4 pixels (2 bits each):

Bits Color Source
00 Background ($D021)
01 Screen RAM upper nybble
10 Screen RAM lower nybble
11 Color RAM

Display Code:

; Display Koala image loaded at $6000
        LDA #$18        ; Bank 1 ($4000-$7FFF)
        STA $D018       ; Screen at $6000, charset at $6000
        
        LDA $DD00       ; Set VIC bank
        AND #$FC
        ORA #$02        ; Bank 1
        STA $DD00
        
        LDA #$3B        ; Bitmap mode on
        STA $D011
        LDA #$18        ; Multicolor on
        STA $D016
        
        ; Copy screen RAM to $0400
        LDX #$00
COPY1:  LDA $7F40,X
        STA $0400,X
        LDA $8040,X
        STA $0500,X
        LDA $8140,X
        STA $0600,X
        LDA $8240,X
        STA $0700,X
        INX
        BNE COPY1
        
        ; Copy color RAM to $D800
        LDX #$00
COPY2:  LDA $8328,X
        STA $D800,X
        LDA $8428,X
        STA $D900,X
        LDA $8528,X
        STA $DA00,X
        LDA $8628,X
        STA $DB00,X
        INX
        BNE COPY2
        
        LDA $8710       ; Background color
        STA $D021

Art Studio (.ART) - Hires Bitmap

Format for high-resolution (non-multicolor) bitmap images.

Structure (9,009 bytes):

Offset Size Description
$00-$01 2 Load address ($2000)
$02-$1F41 8,000 Bitmap data
$1F42-$2329 1,000 Screen RAM
$232A 1 Border color
$232B 1 Background color

SpritePad (.SPD) - Sprite Data

Modern sprite editor format supporting multiple sprites and animations.

Header:

Offset Size Description
$00-$02 3 Signature: “SPD”
$03 1 Version
$04 1 Number of sprites - 1
$05 1 Number of animations
$06 1 Background color
$07 1 Multicolor 1
$08 1 Multicolor 2

Sprite Data (64 bytes each): - 63 bytes of sprite pattern data - 1 byte: Bits 0-3 = color, Bit 7 = multicolor flag

CharPad (.CTM) - Character/Tile Data

Character set and tile map editor format.

Header:

Offset Size Description
$00-$02 3 Signature: “CTM”
$03 1 Version
$04 1 Colors (BG, MC1, MC2, char color)
$05 1 Color mode
$06-$07 2 Number of characters
$08-$09 2 Number of tiles
$0A 1 Tile width
$0B 1 Tile height
$0C-$0D 2 Map width
$0E-$0F 2 Map height

CRT - Cartridge Image

Container format for cartridge ROM images.

Header (64 bytes):

Offset Size Description
$00-$0F 16 Signature: “C64 CARTRIDGE”
$10-$13 4 Header length ($00000040)
$14-$15 2 Version ($0100)
$16-$17 2 Cartridge type
$18 1 EXROM line state
$19 1 GAME line state
$1A-$1F 6 Reserved
$20-$3F 32 Cartridge name

CHIP Packet (follows header, one per ROM bank):

Offset Size Description
$00-$03 4 Signature: “CHIP”
$04-$07 4 Packet length
$08-$09 2 Chip type (0=ROM, 1=RAM, 2=Flash)
$0A-$0B 2 Bank number
$0C-$0D 2 Load address
$0E-$0F 2 ROM size
$10+ Variable ROM data

Common Cartridge Types:

Type Name Banks
0 Normal (8K/16K) 1
1 Action Replay Multiple
5 Ocean Up to 64
15 C64 Game System Up to 64
17 Dinamic 16
19 Magic Desk Up to 128
32 EasyFlash 64

Appendix C: C64 Memory Map

Complete reference for the C64’s 64KB address space.

Overview Map

$0000-$00FF   Zero Page (256 bytes) - Fast access, system variables
$0100-$01FF   Stack (256 bytes) - CPU stack, grows downward
$0200-$03FF   System Variables (512 bytes) - BASIC/Kernal workspace
$0400-$07FF   Screen RAM (1024 bytes) - Default text screen
$0800-$9FFF   BASIC Program Area (~38KB) - User programs
$A000-$BFFF   BASIC ROM (8KB) - Or RAM underneath
$C000-$CFFF   Upper RAM (4KB) - Free for programs
$D000-$DFFF   I/O Area (4KB) - VIC-II, SID, CIA, Color RAM
$E000-$FFFF   Kernal ROM (8KB) - Or RAM underneath

Detailed Memory Map

Address Size Description
Zero Page ($0000-$00FF)
$0000 1 6510 Data Direction Register
$0001 1 6510 Processor Port (memory banking, tape)
$0002 1 Unused
$0003-$0004 2 Float-to-integer conversion pointer
$0005-$0006 2 Integer-to-float conversion pointer
$0007 1 Search character
$0014-$0015 2 Pointer to current BASIC line
$002B-$002C 2 Start of BASIC program
$002D-$002E 2 Start of BASIC variables
$0031-$0032 2 Top of BASIC strings
$0033-$0034 2 Limit of BASIC memory
$0039-$003A 2 Current BASIC line number
$0043-$0044 2 Pointer for DATA statements
$00FB-$00FE 4 Free zero page for user programs
Stack ($0100-$01FF)
$0100-$01FF 256 CPU Stack (grows from $01FF down)
System Area ($0200-$03FF)
$0200-$0258 89 Input buffer (BASIC line input)
$0277-$0280 10 Keyboard buffer
$028A 1 Repeat key flag
$0291 1 Shift key flag ($01=Shift, $02=Commodore)
$02A7-$02FF 89 Reserved
$0300-$030B 12 Jump vectors for BASIC errors, etc.
$0314-$0315 2 IRQ vector (default: $EA31)
$0316-$0317 2 BRK vector
$0318-$0319 2 NMI vector (default: $FE47)
$033C-$03FB 192 Tape buffer
Screen RAM ($0400-$07FF)
$0400-$07E7 1000 Screen memory (40×25 characters)
$07F8-$07FF 8 Sprite pointers (default location)
BASIC Area ($0800-$9FFF)
$0800 1 Must be $00 for BASIC programs
$0801-9FFF|38911|BASICprogram, variables, arrays, strings|| *  * BASICROM(A000-$BFFF)**
A000−BFFF 8192 BASIC interpreter (RAM underneath)
Upper RAM (C000−CFFF)
C000−CFFF 4096 Free RAM (often used for code)
I/O Area (D000−DFFF)
D000−D02E 47 VIC-II registers
D400−D41C 29 SID registers
D800−DBE7 1000 Color RAM (4-bit, 1000 nybbles)
DC00−DC0F 16 CIA #1 (keyboard, joystick, IRQ)
DD00−DD0F 16 CIA #2 (serial bus, VIC bank, NMI)
DE00−DEFF 256 I/O Area 1 (expansion)
DF00−DFFF 256 I/O Area 2 (expansion)
Kernal ROM (E000−FFFF)
E000−FFFF 8192 Kernal operating system (RAM underneath)
FFFA−FFFB 2 NMI vector
FFFC−FFFD 2 RESET vector
FFFE−FFFF 2 IRQ/BRK vector

Processor Port ($0001) - Memory Banking

Bits 0-2 A000−BFFF D000−DFFF E000−FFFF
%000 RAM RAM RAM
%001 RAM Character ROM RAM
%010 RAM Character ROM Kernal ROM
%011 BASIC ROM Character ROM Kernal ROM
%100 RAM RAM RAM
%101 RAM I/O RAM
%110 RAM I/O Kernal ROM
%111 BASIC ROM I/O Kernal ROM

Default value: $37 (%110111) - BASIC ROM, I/O, Kernal ROM visible

Common configurations:

lda #$35    ; I/O visible, ROMs off
sta $01     ; Pure 64KB RAM + I/O

lda #$34    ; All RAM, no I/O
sta $01     ; Full 64KB RAM (no hardware access)

lda #$37    ; Default C64 config  
sta $01     ; BASIC + I/O + Kernal

VIC-II Banks (via CIA2 $DD00)

$DD00 bits 0-1 VIC Bank Address Range
%xxxxxx11 Bank 0 $0000-$3FFF (default)
%xxxxxx10 Bank 1 $4000-$7FFF
%xxxxxx01 Bank 2 8000−BFFF
%xxxxxx00 Bank 3 C000−FFFF

Note: Character ROM appears at $1000/$9000 in Banks 0 and 2 only.

Free Memory for Programs

Location Size Notes
$0002 1 Unused zero page byte
$00FB-$00FE 4 Free zero page
$0334-$033B 8 Unused
C000−CFFF 4096 Upper RAM - safe for code
Under BASIC ROM 8192 A000−BFFF (bank out BASIC)
Under Kernal ROM 8192 E000−FFFF (bank out Kernal)

Appendix D: SID Register Map

Complete reference for the MOS 6581/8580 SID chip at D400−D41C.

Register Overview

Offset Address Name R/W Description
Voice 1
$00 $D400 FREQLO1 W Frequency Low Byte
$01 $D401 FREQHI1 W Frequency High Byte
$02 $D402 PWLO1 W Pulse Width Low Byte
$03 $D403 PWHI1 W Pulse Width High Nybble (bits 0-3)
$04 $D404 VCREG1 W Voice 1 Control Register
$05 $D405 ATDCY1 W Attack/Decay
$06 $D406 SUREL1 W Sustain/Release
Voice 2
$07 $D407 FREQLO2 W Frequency Low Byte
$08 $D408 FREQHI2 W Frequency High Byte
$09 $D409 PWLO2 W Pulse Width Low Byte
$0A $D40A PWHI2 W Pulse Width High Nybble
$0B $D40B VCREG2 W Voice 2 Control Register
$0C $D40C ATDCY2 W Attack/Decay
$0D $D40D SUREL2 W Sustain/Release
Voice 3
$0E $D40E FREQLO3 W Frequency Low Byte
$0F $D40F FREQHI3 W Frequency High Byte
$10 $D410 PWLO3 W Pulse Width Low Byte
$11 $D411 PWHI3 W Pulse Width High Nybble
$12 $D412 VCREG3 W Voice 3 Control Register
$13 $D413 ATDCY3 W Attack/Decay
$14 $D414 SUREL3 W Sustain/Release
Filter
$15 $D415 CUTLO W Filter Cutoff Low (bits 0-2)
$16 $D416 CUTHI W Filter Cutoff High Byte
$17 $D417 RESSION W Filter Resonance/Routing
$18 $D418 SIGVOL W Volume/Filter Mode
Read-Only
$19 $D419 POTX R Paddle X (analog)
$1A $D41A POTY R Paddle Y (analog)
$1B $D41B OSC3 R Oscillator 3 Output
$1C $D41C ENV3 R Envelope 3 Output

Control Register Bits (D404/D40B/$D412)

Bit Name Description
0 GATE 1 = Start Attack/Decay/Sustain, 0 = Start Release
1 SYNC 1 = Sync with Voice N-1 (hard sync)
2 RING 1 = Ring modulate with Voice N-1
3 TEST 1 = Disable oscillator (pulse stays high, noise resets)
4 TRI 1 = Triangle waveform
5 SAW 1 = Sawtooth waveform
6 PUL 1 = Pulse/Square waveform
7 NOI 1 = Noise waveform

Waveform combinations: Multiple bits can be set for combined waveforms (AND logic). Common combinations: $41 (pulse+gate), $21 (saw+gate), $11 (tri+gate), $81 (noise+gate).

ADSR Registers

Attack/Decay (D405/D40C/$D413): | Bits | Value | Description | |——|——-|————-| | 4-7 | 0-15 | Attack time | | 0-3 | 0-15 | Decay time |

Sustain/Release (D406/D40D/$D414): | Bits | Value | Description | |——|——-|————-| | 4-7 | 0-15 | Sustain level (0=off, 15=max) | | 0-3 | 0-15 | Release time |

ADSR Timing Table:

Value Attack Decay/Release
0 2ms 6ms
1 8ms 24ms
2 16ms 48ms
3 24ms 72ms
4 38ms 114ms
5 56ms 168ms
6 68ms 204ms
7 80ms 240ms
8 100ms 300ms
9 250ms 750ms
10 500ms 1.5s
11 800ms 2.4s
12 1s 3s
13 3s 9s
14 5s 15s
15 8s 24s

Filter Registers

Resonance/Voice Routing ($D417): | Bit | Description | |—–|————-| | 0 | FILT1 - Route Voice 1 through filter | | 1 | FILT2 - Route Voice 2 through filter | | 2 | FILT3 - Route Voice 3 through filter | | 3 | FILTEX - Route external audio through filter | | 4-7 | RES - Filter resonance (0-15) |

Mode/Volume ($D418): | Bit | Description | |—–|————-| | 0-3 | VOL - Master volume (0-15) | | 4 | LP - Low-pass filter | | 5 | BP - Band-pass filter | | 6 | HP - High-pass filter | | 7 | 3OFF - Disconnect Voice 3 output (for modulation) |

Frequency Calculation

Formula:

Frequency (Hz) = (FREQHI*256 + FREQLO) × Fclk / 16777216

PAL:  Fclk = 985248 Hz
NTSC: Fclk = 1022727 Hz

Register value for desired frequency:

PAL:  FREQ = Frequency × 17.02841924
NTSC: FREQ = Frequency × 16.40426588

Note frequency table (PAL, A4=440Hz): | Note | FREQ Value | Note | FREQ Value | |——|————|——|————| | C-0 | $0112 | C-4 | $1120 | | C-1 | $0224 | C-5 | $2241 | | C-2 | $0448 | C-6 | $4483 | | C-3 | $0890 | C-7 | $8905 |

Pulse Width

12-bit value (000−FFF): - $000 = Silence (0% duty) - $800 = Square wave (50% duty) - $FFF = Almost DC (100% duty)

Example: Play a Note

; Play middle C on Voice 1
        lda #$00
        sta $d404     ; Gate off first
        
        lda #$05      ; Attack=0, Decay=5
        sta $d405
        lda #$a9      ; Sustain=10, Release=9  
        sta $d406
        
        lda #$20      ; Frequency low (C-4)
        sta $d400
        lda #$11      ; Frequency high
        sta $d401
        
        lda #$08      ; Pulse width 50%
        sta $d403
        lda #$00
        sta $d402
        
        lda #$0f      ; Volume max
        sta $d418
        
        lda #$41      ; Pulse wave + Gate
        sta $d404

6581 vs 8580 Differences

Feature 6581 8580
Supply voltage 12V 9V
Filter sound Stronger, grittier Weaker, cleaner
Combined waveforms Distinctive Different character
Digi playback $D418 volume trick Needs hardware mod
Filter cutoff Non-linear More linear

Appendix E: VIC-II Register Map

The MOS 6567 (NTSC) / 6569 (PAL) / 856x Video Interface Chip II controls all graphics output.

Register Overview (D000−D02E)

Address Name R/W Description
$D000 M0X R/W Sprite 0 X position (bits 0-7)
$D001 M0Y R/W Sprite 0 Y position
$D002 M1X R/W Sprite 1 X position (bits 0-7)
$D003 M1Y R/W Sprite 1 Y position
$D004 M2X R/W Sprite 2 X position (bits 0-7)
$D005 M2Y R/W Sprite 2 Y position
$D006 M3X R/W Sprite 3 X position (bits 0-7)
$D007 M3Y R/W Sprite 3 Y position
$D008 M4X R/W Sprite 4 X position (bits 0-7)
$D009 M4Y R/W Sprite 4 Y position
$D00A M5X R/W Sprite 5 X position (bits 0-7)
$D00B M5Y R/W Sprite 5 Y position
$D00C M6X R/W Sprite 6 X position (bits 0-7)
$D00D M6Y R/W Sprite 6 Y position
$D00E M7X R/W Sprite 7 X position (bits 0-7)
$D00F M7Y R/W Sprite 7 Y position
$D010 MSIGX R/W Sprite X position MSB (bit 8 for each sprite)
$D011 CR1 R/W Control register 1 (see below)
$D012 RASTER R/W Raster position (read) / Raster compare (write)
$D013 LPX R Light pen X position
$D014 LPY R Light pen Y position
$D015 SPENA R/W Sprite enable (1 bit per sprite)
$D016 CR2 R/W Control register 2 (see below)
$D017 YXPAND R/W Sprite Y expansion (1 bit per sprite)
$D018 VMCSB R/W Memory pointers (screen/char/bitmap)
$D019 IRQST R/W Interrupt status register
$D01A IRQEN R/W Interrupt enable register
$D01B SPBGPR R/W Sprite-to-background priority
$D01C SPMC R/W Sprite multicolor mode
$D01D XXPAND R/W Sprite X expansion
$D01E SPSPCL R Sprite-sprite collision
$D01F SPBGCL R Sprite-background collision
$D020 EXTCOL R/W Border color
$D021 BGCOL0 R/W Background color 0
$D022 BGCOL1 R/W Background color 1 (multicolor/ECM)
$D023 BGCOL2 R/W Background color 2 (multicolor/ECM)
$D024 BGCOL3 R/W Background color 3 (ECM)
$D025 SPMC0 R/W Sprite multicolor 0
$D026 SPMC1 R/W Sprite multicolor 1
$D027 SP0COL R/W Sprite 0 color
$D028 SP1COL R/W Sprite 1 color
$D029 SP2COL R/W Sprite 2 color
$D02A SP3COL R/W Sprite 3 color
$D02B SP4COL R/W Sprite 4 color
$D02C SP5COL R/W Sprite 5 color
$D02D SP6COL R/W Sprite 6 color
$D02E SP7COL R/W Sprite 7 color

Control Register 1 ($D011)

Bit Name Description
7 RST8 Raster compare bit 8 (MSB)
6 ECM Extended color mode (1=enable)
5 BMM Bitmap mode (1=enable)
4 DEN Display enable (1=screen on)
3 RSEL Row select (0=24 rows, 1=25 rows)
2-0 YSCROLL Vertical scroll (0-7 pixels)

Control Register 2 ($D016)

Bit Name Description
7-6 - Unused (read as 1)
5 RES Reset VIC (active low, normally 1)
4 MCM Multicolor mode (1=enable)
3 CSEL Column select (0=38 columns, 1=40 columns)
2-0 XSCROLL Horizontal scroll (0-7 pixels)

Memory Pointer Register ($D018)

Bits Description
7-4 Screen memory pointer (offset/1024 within VIC bank)
3-1 Character memory / Bitmap pointer (offset/2048)
0 Unused

Screen Memory Locations (relative to VIC bank): - %0000xxxx → $0000 - %0001xxxx → $0400 (default) - %0010xxxx → $0800 - … up to %1111xxxx → $3C00

Character/Bitmap Locations: - %xxxx000x → $0000 (Character ROM visible in banks 0 & 2) - %xxxx001x → $0800 - %xxxx010x → $1000 (Character ROM visible in banks 0 & 2) - %xxxx011x → $1800 - %xxxx100x → $2000 (bitmap mode) - %xxxx101x → $2800 - %xxxx110x → $3000 - %xxxx111x → $3800

Interrupt Register ($D019) - Status

Bit Name Description
7 IRQ Interrupt occurred (any source)
3-6 - Unused
3 ILP Light pen interrupt
2 IMMC Sprite-sprite collision interrupt
1 IMBC Sprite-background collision interrupt
0 IRST Raster compare interrupt

Write 1 to a bit to acknowledge/clear that interrupt.

Interrupt Enable Register ($D01A)

Bit Description
3 Enable light pen interrupt
2 Enable sprite-sprite collision interrupt
1 Enable sprite-background collision interrupt
0 Enable raster interrupt

VIC Bank Selection (via CIA2 $DD00)

Bits 0-1 Bank Address Range
%11 0 $0000-$3FFF (default)
%10 1 $4000-$7FFF
%01 2 8000−BFFF
%00 3 C000−FFFF

Note: Character ROM is visible at $1000 and $9000 in banks 0 and 2 only.

Video Modes

ECM BMM MCM Mode Resolution
0 0 0 Standard character mode 40×25 chars
0 0 1 Multicolor character mode 40×25 chars
0 1 0 Standard bitmap mode 320×200 pixels
0 1 1 Multicolor bitmap mode 160×200 pixels
1 0 0 Extended color mode 40×25 chars
1 x x Invalid (black screen) -

Color Palette

Value Color Hex (approximate)
0 Black #000000
1 White #FFFFFF
2 Red #880000
3 Cyan #AAFFEE
4 Purple #CC44CC
5 Green #00CC55
6 Blue #0000AA
7 Yellow #EEEE77
8 Orange #DD8855
9 Brown #664400
10 Light Red #FF7777
11 Dark Grey #333333
12 Medium Grey #777777
13 Light Green #AAFF66
14 Light Blue #0088FF
15 Light Grey #BBBBBB

Sprite Positioning

  • X range: 0-511 (requires MSB in $D010)
  • Y range: 0-255
  • Visible X: approximately 24-343
  • Visible Y: approximately 50-249 (PAL) / 50-229 (NTSC)
  • Sprite size: 24×21 pixels (or 12×21 in multicolor mode)

Raster Line Values

System First visible Last visible Total lines
PAL 51 250 312
NTSC 51 230 263

Appendix F: CIA Register Map

The MOS 6526 Complex Interface Adapter provides I/O, timers, and real-time clock. Two CIAs are present: CIA1 at $DC00 (keyboard, joysticks, IRQ) and CIA2 at $DD00 (serial bus, VIC bank, NMI).

CIA Generic Register Set (offset from base)

Offset Name R/W Description
$00 PRA R/W Peripheral Data Register A
$01 PRB R/W Peripheral Data Register B
$02 DDRA R/W Data Direction Register A (0=input, 1=output)
$03 DDRB R/W Data Direction Register B
$04 TALO R Timer A Low byte (current value)
$05 TAHI R Timer A High byte (current value)
$06 TBLO R Timer B Low byte (current value)
$07 TBHI R Timer B High byte (current value)
$04 TALO W Timer A Latch Low byte
$05 TAHI W Timer A Latch High byte
$06 TBLO W Timer B Latch Low byte
$07 TBHI W Timer B Latch High byte
$08 TOD10 R/W Time of Day - 1/10 seconds (BCD)
$09 TODSEC R/W Time of Day - Seconds (BCD)
$0A TODMIN R/W Time of Day - Minutes (BCD)
$0B TODHR R/W Time of Day - Hours (BCD, bit 7=PM)
$0C SDR R/W Serial Data Register
$0D ICR R/W Interrupt Control Register
$0E CRA R/W Control Register A
$0F CRB R/W Control Register B

CIA1 (DC00−DC0F) - Keyboard and Joysticks

Address Function
$DC00 Port A: Keyboard column / Joystick 2
$DC01 Port B: Keyboard row / Joystick 1
$DC02 DDRA: Default $FF (output)
$DC03 DDRB: Default $00 (input)
DC04−DC07 Timer A and B
DC08−DC0B Time of Day clock
$DC0C Serial port
$DC0D Interrupt control (triggers IRQ)
DC0E−DC0F Timer control registers

Joystick Reading (DC00/DC01):

Bit Direction/Button
0 Up (active low)
1 Down
2 Left
3 Right
4 Fire 
; Read Joystick 2 (Port A)
    LDA $DC00
    AND #$1F        ; Mask bits 0-4
    ; Now A contains joystick state (0=pressed)

; Read Joystick 1 (Port B)  
    LDA $DC01
    AND #$1F

Keyboard Matrix: The keyboard uses an 8×8 matrix. Write column select to $DC00, read row results from $DC01.

CIA2 (DD00−DD0F) - Serial Bus and VIC Bank

Address Function
$DD00 Port A: VIC bank, serial bus
$DD01 Port B: User port data
$DD02 DDRA: Default $3F
$DD03 DDRB: User port direction
DD04−DD07 Timer A and B
DD08−DD0B Time of Day clock
$DD0C Serial port
$DD0D Interrupt control (triggers NMI)
DD0E−DD0F Timer control registers

Port A ($DD00) Bits:

Bit Function
7 Serial DATA IN
6 Serial CLK IN
5 Serial DATA OUT
4 Serial CLK OUT
3 Serial ATN OUT
2 RS-232 TXD
1-0 VIC Bank select (inverted!)

VIC Bank Selection:

; Select VIC Bank 1 ($4000-$7FFF)
    LDA $DD00
    AND #%11111100  ; Clear bits 0-1
    ORA #%00000010  ; Set for bank 1
    STA $DD00

Control Register A ($DC0E / $DD0E)

Bit Name Description
7 TODIN TOD clock source (0=60Hz, 1=50Hz)
6 SPMODE Serial port mode (0=input, 1=output)
5 INMODE Timer A input (0=Phi2, 1=CNT pin)
4 LOAD Force load latch into timer (strobe)
3 RUNMODE Timer mode (0=continuous, 1=one-shot)
2 OUTMODE Timer output mode (0=pulse, 1=toggle)
1 PBON Timer output on PB6
0 START Start timer (1=running)

Control Register B ($DC0F / $DD0F)

Bit Name Description
7 ALARM TOD registers (0=set clock, 1=set alarm)
6-5 INMODE Timer B input mode (see below)
4 LOAD Force load latch into timer
3 RUNMODE Timer mode (0=continuous, 1=one-shot)
2 OUTMODE Timer output mode
1 PBON Timer output on PB7
0 START Start timer

Timer B Input Modes (bits 6-5):

Bits Mode
%00 Count Phi2 clock pulses
%01 Count positive CNT transitions
%10 Count Timer A underflows
%11 Count Timer A underflows while CNT high

Interrupt Control Register ($DC0D / $DD0D)

Reading (Status):

Bit Name Description
7 IR Interrupt occurred
4 FLG FLAG pin edge detected
3 SP Serial port full/empty
2 ALARM TOD alarm match
1 TB Timer B underflow
0 TA Timer A underflow

Reading clears all flags.

Writing (Enable/Disable):

Bit 7 Effect
0 Clear enable bits that are set to 1
1 Set enable bits that are set to 1

Common Timer Setup

; Set up a 60Hz IRQ using Timer A on CIA1
    SEI                     ; Disable interrupts
    
    ; Timer value for 60Hz: $4025 (PAL) or $4295 (NTSC)
    LDA #<$4025
    STA $DC04               ; Timer A low
    LDA #>$4025
    STA $DC05               ; Timer A high
    
    LDA #%00010001          ; Enable timer, continuous mode
    STA $DC0E
    
    LDA #%10000001          ; Enable Timer A interrupt
    STA $DC0D
    
    CLI                     ; Enable interrupts

Time of Day Clock

The TOD registers use BCD format: - Reading freezes display until all registers read - Writing to hours register stops clock until 1/10s written - 50/60Hz input configured via CRA bit 7

; Read current time
    LDA $DC0B       ; Read hours (freezes display)
    AND #$1F        ; Mask AM/PM bit
    ; Hours in BCD
    LDA $DC0A       ; Minutes
    LDA $DC09       ; Seconds  
    LDA $DC08       ; 1/10 seconds (unfreezes)

Chip Variants

Chip Speed Notes
6526 1 MHz Original, used in early C64
6526A 2 MHz Used in later C64 and C128
8521 2 MHz HMOS version, used in late C64C

Some timing-critical code may behave differently between chip versions.

Appendix G: 6502/6510 Opcode Reference

The C64’s 6510 CPU is fully compatible with the 6502 instruction set.

Registers

Register Size Description
A 8-bit Accumulator - main math/logic register
X 8-bit Index register X - indexing, counting
Y 8-bit Index register Y - indexing, counting
SP 8-bit Stack pointer ($0100-$01FF)
PC 16-bit Program counter
P 8-bit Processor status (flags)

Status Flags (P Register)

Bit Flag Name Set When
7 N Negative Result bit 7 = 1
6 V Overflow Signed overflow occurred
5 - Unused Always 1
4 B Break BRK instruction executed
3 D Decimal BCD mode enabled
2 I IRQ Disable Interrupts disabled
1 Z Zero Result = 0
0 C Carry Unsigned overflow/borrow

Addressing Modes

Mode Syntax Example Bytes Description
Immediate #$nn LDA #$20 2 Use literal value
Zero Page $nn LDA $20 2 Address $0000-$00FF
Zero Page,X $nn,X LDA $20,X 2 ZP + X register
Zero Page,Y $nn,Y LDX $20,Y 2 ZP + Y register
Absolute $nnnn LDA $1234 3 Full 16-bit address
Absolute,X $nnnn,X LDA $1234,X 3 Address + X
Absolute,Y $nnnn,Y LDA 1234, Y|3|Address + Y||Indirect|(nnnn) JMP (1234)|3|Addressatpointer||IndexedIndirect|(nn,X) LDA (20, X)|2|PointeratZP + X||IndirectIndexed|(nn),Y
Implied TAX 1 No operand
Accumulator A LSR A 1 Operate on A
Relative $nn BEQ $20 2 Branch offset (-128 to +127)

Complete Instruction Set

Load/Store Operations

Mnemonic Description Flags Modes
LDA Load Accumulator N,Z imm,zp,zpx,abs,abx,aby,izx,izy
LDX Load X Register N,Z imm,zp,zpy,abs,aby
LDY Load Y Register N,Z imm,zp,zpx,abs,abx
STA Store Accumulator - zp,zpx,abs,abx,aby,izx,izy
STX Store X Register - zp,zpy,abs
STY Store Y Register - zp,zpx,abs

Register Transfers

Mnemonic Description Flags Cycles
TAX Transfer A to X N,Z 2
TAY Transfer A to Y N,Z 2
TXA Transfer X to A N,Z 2
TYA Transfer Y to A N,Z 2
TSX Transfer SP to X N,Z 2
TXS Transfer X to SP - 2

Arithmetic

Mnemonic Description Flags Notes
ADC Add with Carry N,V,Z,C A = A + M + C
SBC Subtract with Carry N,V,Z,C A = A - M - (1-C)
CMP Compare A N,Z,C A - M (flags only)
CPX Compare X N,Z,C X - M (flags only)
CPY Compare Y N,Z,C Y - M (flags only)

Increment/Decrement

Mnemonic Description Flags Modes
INC Increment Memory N,Z zp,zpx,abs,abx
INX Increment X N,Z implied
INY Increment Y N,Z implied
DEC Decrement Memory N,Z zp,zpx,abs,abx
DEX Decrement X N,Z implied
DEY Decrement Y N,Z implied

Logical Operations

Mnemonic Description Flags
AND Logical AND N,Z
ORA Logical OR N,Z
EOR Exclusive OR N,Z
BIT Bit Test N,V,Z

Shift/Rotate

Mnemonic Description Flags Operation
ASL Arithmetic Shift Left N,Z,C C ← [76543210] ← 0
LSR Logical Shift Right N,Z,C 0 → [76543210] → C
ROL Rotate Left N,Z,C C ← [76543210] ← C
ROR Rotate Right N,Z,C C → [76543210] → C

Branches (2 bytes, 2-4 cycles)

Mnemonic Description Condition
BCC Branch if Carry Clear C = 0
BCS Branch if Carry Set C = 1
BEQ Branch if Equal (Zero) Z = 1
BNE Branch if Not Equal Z = 0
BMI Branch if Minus N = 1
BPL Branch if Plus N = 0
BVC Branch if Overflow Clear V = 0
BVS Branch if Overflow Set V = 1

Add 1 cycle if branch taken, add 1 more if page boundary crossed.

Jumps and Calls

Mnemonic Description Bytes Cycles
JMP abs Jump to address 3 3
JMP (ind) Jump indirect 3 5
JSR Jump to Subroutine 3 6
RTS Return from Subroutine 1 6
RTI Return from Interrupt 1 6
BRK Force Interrupt 1 7

Stack Operations

Mnemonic Description Flags Cycles
PHA Push A - 3
PLA Pull A N,Z 4
PHP Push Status - 3
PLP Pull Status All 4

Flag Operations

Mnemonic Description Affected
CLC Clear Carry C = 0
SEC Set Carry C = 1
CLD Clear Decimal D = 0
SED Set Decimal D = 1
CLI Clear IRQ Disable I = 0
SEI Set IRQ Disable I = 1
CLV Clear Overflow V = 0

Miscellaneous

Mnemonic Description Cycles
NOP No Operation 2

Cycle Counts Quick Reference

Addressing Mode Base Cycles Page Cross
Immediate 2 -
Zero Page 3 -
Zero Page,X/Y 4 -
Absolute 4 -
Absolute,X/Y 4 +1
Indirect,X 6 -
Indirect,Y 5 +1

Undocumented Opcodes (Use with Caution)

Some undocumented opcodes are stable and useful:

Opcode Mnemonic Description Cycles
$A3 LAX (izx) LDA + LDX 6
$A7 LAX zp LDA + LDX 3
$AF LAX abs LDA + LDX 4
$B3 LAX (izy) LDA + LDX 5*
$B7 LAX zpy LDA + LDX 4
$BF LAX aby LDA + LDX 4*
$83 SAX (izx) Store A AND X 6
$87 SAX zp Store A AND X 3
$8F SAX abs Store A AND X 4
$97 SAX zpy Store A AND X 4
$CB SBX imm X = (A AND X) - imm 2
$EB SBC imm Same as legal SBC 2
$4B ALR imm AND + LSR 2
$6B ARR imm AND + ROR 2
$0B ANC imm AND, copy N to C 2
$2B ANC imm AND, copy N to C 2

Unstable opcodes (behavior varies): $8B, $AB, $93, $9B, $9C, $9E, $9F

Common Code Patterns

16-bit Increment:

    inc ptr
    bne skip
    inc ptr+1
skip:

16-bit Add:

    clc
    lda ptr
    adc #amount_lo
    sta ptr
    lda ptr+1
    adc #amount_hi
    sta ptr+1

Multiply by 2:

    asl a           ; A = A * 2

Divide by 2:

    lsr a           ; A = A / 2 (unsigned)

Wait for Raster:

wait:
    lda $d012
    cmp #$80
    bne wait
